grafana_setup.configuring_alloy_otlp: Configuring Alloy to Receive OTLP Metrics
grafana_setup.enable_remote_write_description: Edit the Prometheus systemd service file to add the --web.enable-remote-write-receiver flag:
grafana_setup.prometheus_config_description: Prometheus must be configured to accept remote write requests from Grafana Alloy. This requires enabling the remote write receiver feature.
grafana_setup.prometheus_warning: Without the --web.enable-remote-write-receiver flag, Alloy will receive "404 Not Found" errors when attempting to write metrics to Prometheus. This is a required configuration step.
grafana_setup.restart_alloy_description: If Alloy was already running and encountering errors before Prometheus was configured, restart it to clear any retry backoff state:
grafana_setup.subtitle: Complete guide to integrating SysManage with Grafana for advanced metrics visualization and monitoring using OpenTelemetry.
grafana_setup.title: Grafana Integration Setup
grafana_setup.alloy_config_description: Configure Alloy to receive OTLP metrics on port 4317 and forward them to Prometheus.
grafana_setup.alloy_description: Grafana Alloy is the OpenTelemetry collector that receives OTLP metrics from managed hosts and forwards them to Prometheus for storage and visualization.
grafana_setup.auto_refresh_text: The OpenTelemetry Status card refreshes automatically every 30 seconds to reflect deployment status changes.
grafana_setup.check_alloy_description: On the Grafana server, verify Alloy is receiving metrics:
grafana_setup.check_service_description: On the managed host, verify the OpenTelemetry collector is running:
grafana_setup.connect_description: If a host was deployed before Grafana integration was enabled, you can connect it:
grafana_setup.connect_step2: Navigate to the host detail page
grafana_setup.connect_step3: Click Connect to Grafana in the OpenTelemetry Status card
grafana_setup.dashboard_step1: Log in to Grafana web interface
grafana_setup.dashboard_step3: Add panels with Prometheus queries for OpenTelemetry metrics
grafana_setup.dashboard_step4: Save and share the dashboard with your team
grafana_setup.deploy_step1_navigate: Navigate to the host detail page for the target host
grafana_setup.deploy_step4_wait: Wait for deployment to complete (typically 30-60 seconds)
grafana_setup.deploy_step5_verify: Verify the status shows "Deployed: Yes" and "Service Status: running"
grafana_setup.deployment_description: Once Grafana integration is enabled, you can deploy OpenTelemetry collectors to individual hosts.
grafana_setup.disconnect_description: To stop sending metrics while keeping OpenTelemetry installed:
grafana_setup.disconnect_step1: Navigate to the host detail page
grafana_setup.disconnect_step3: The collector will remain installed but stop exporting metrics
grafana_setup.other_platforms_text: For macOS, Windows, or other Linux distributions, refer to the official Grafana Alloy installation documentation.
grafana_setup.overview_description: SysManage integrates with Grafana to provide powerful visualization and monitoring capabilities for OpenTelemetry metrics collected from your managed hosts. This guide walks you through the complete setup process, from installing Grafana Alloy to verifying metrics flow.
grafana_setup.performance_batching_text: Alloy's batch processor groups metrics before sending to Prometheus, reducing network overhead. Adjust batch size and timeout based on metric volume.
grafana_setup.performance_collection_text: Adjust metric collection intervals based on your monitoring needs and system resources. More frequent collection provides better granularity but increases resource usage.
grafana_setup.performance_retention_text: Configure Prometheus retention policies to balance historical data availability with storage requirements. Consider using remote storage for long-term metrics.
grafana_setup.performance_scaling_text: For large deployments, consider running multiple Alloy instances behind a load balancer, or using Prometheus federation for distributed metrics collection.
grafana_setup.prerequisite_access: Administrative access to the Grafana server
grafana_setup.prerequisite_grafana: A running Grafana instance (version 9.0 or later recommended)
grafana_setup.prerequisite_network: Network connectivity between SysManage, Grafana, and managed hosts
grafana_setup.query_prometheus_description: Access Prometheus (typically at http://your-grafana-server:9090) and run a query:
grafana_setup.remove_description: To completely uninstall OpenTelemetry from a host:
grafana_setup.remove_step1: Navigate to the host detail page
grafana_setup.remove_step3: The collector package will be completely removed, including configuration files
grafana_setup.requirement_grafana_enabled: Grafana integration must be enabled in SysManage settings
grafana_setup.requirement_permission: User must have the DEPLOY_OPENTELEMETRY permission
grafana_setup.requirement_privileged: Host must have a privileged SysManage agent running (with root/administrator access)
grafana_setup.restart_description: To restart the OpenTelemetry collector service:
grafana_setup.restart_step1: Navigate to the host detail page
grafana_setup.restart_step2: Click Restart in the OpenTelemetry Status card
grafana_setup.security_auth_1: Configure Alloy with authentication for OTLP endpoint
grafana_setup.security_auth_2: Use API keys or mTLS for additional security
grafana_setup.security_monitoring_1: Review Alloy logs for unauthorized access attempts
grafana_setup.security_monitoring_2: Set up alerts for connection anomalies
grafana_setup.security_monitoring_3: Audit OpenTelemetry deployment history in SysManage
grafana_setup.security_network_1: Restrict port 4317 access to only SysManage-managed hosts
grafana_setup.security_network_2: Use network segmentation or VPN for metrics traffic
grafana_setup.security_network_3: Implement firewall rules to limit exposure
grafana_setup.security_tls_1: Configure Alloy to use TLS for OTLP receiver
grafana_setup.security_tls_2: Use valid SSL certificates from a trusted CA
grafana_setup.security_tls_3: Update SysManage Grafana URL to use https://
grafana_setup.solution_check_agent: Ensure SysManage agent is running in privileged mode
grafana_setup.solution_check_alloy: Ensure Grafana Alloy is running: systemctl status alloy
grafana_setup.solution_check_firewall: Check firewall rules allowing traffic on port 4317
grafana_setup.solution_check_logs: Check agent logs for error messages: ~/dev/sysmanage-agent/logs/agent.log
grafana_setup.solution_check_package_manager: Confirm package manager (apt/yum) is working correctly on the target host
grafana_setup.solution_redeploy: Redeploy OpenTelemetry to the affected hosts to update their configuration
grafana_setup.solution_review_alloy_config: Review Alloy configuration for correct OTLP receiver setup
grafana_setup.solution_update_url: Update the Grafana URL in SysManage settings to use port 4317
grafana_setup.solution_verify_collector: Verify OpenTelemetry collector is running on the host: systemctl status otelcol-contrib
grafana_setup.solution_verify_connectivity: Verify network connectivity from host to Grafana server on port 4317
grafana_setup.solution_verify_url: Verify Grafana URL in SysManage settings points to port 4317 (not 3000)
grafana_setup.solution_wrong_port: This indicates the collector is trying to send OTLP to Grafana's web interface (port 3000) instead of Alloy (port 4317)
grafana_setup.step1_navigate_settings: Navigate to Settings in the SysManage web interface
grafana_setup.sysmanage_config_description: Enable Grafana integration in SysManage to allow OpenTelemetry deployment to hosts.
grafana_setup.url_note: The URL should point to the Grafana Alloy OTLP endpoint (port 4317), not the Grafana web interface (typically port 3000).
grafana_setup.verification_description: After deployment, verify that metrics are flowing from hosts through Alloy to Prometheus and Grafana.

web_interface.automatic_detection_desc: When you add a repository for a specific operating system (e.g., Ubuntu 24.04), SysManage automatically:

overview.description: SysManage provides centralized management of third-party package repositories across multiple operating systems. This feature allows administrators to add, configure, and manage additional software sources for all supported platforms except OpenBSD, which by design does not support third-party repositories.

migration.description: SysManage has been fully migrated from sequential integer IDs to UUIDs using a comprehensive migration strategy.

step1.description: Alembic migrations converted all primary keys and foreign keys from integers to UUIDs

step2.description: Existing data was migrated with new UUID identifiers while preserving relationships

database_security.title: Database Security - SysManage

distributed.description: UUIDs are globally unique, preventing ID conflicts in distributed systems and reducing attack surface in multi-tenant environments.
distributed.title: Distributed System Security

enumeration.description: Sequential IDs (1, 2, 3...) allow attackers to easily enumerate resources. UUIDs eliminate this vulnerability by using non-predictable identifiers.
enumeration.title: ID Enumeration Attack Prevention

gdpr.description: UUIDs support data anonymization requirements by providing identifiers that cannot be easily correlated across systems.
gdpr.title: GDPR Compliance Support

information_leakage.description: Sequential IDs can reveal business metrics (number of users, hosts, etc.). UUIDs prevent this information disclosure.
information_leakage.title: Information Leakage Prevention

privacy.description: UUIDs provide non-correlatable identifiers that enhance user privacy and prevent cross-system tracking.
privacy.title: Enhanced Privacy Protection

replay.description: Non-sequential identifiers prevent attackers from predicting valid resource IDs for replay attacks or automated scanning.
replay.title: Replay Attack Protection

uuid_architecture.description: SysManage implements UUID-based primary keys across all database tables instead of traditional sequential integer IDs. This architectural decision provides significant security advantages and prevents common attack vectors.
uuid_architecture.title: UUID-Based Primary Key Architecture

deploy_early.description: Deploy antivirus software immediately after host approval for maximum protection.
deploy_early.title: Deploy Early

monitor_regularly.description: Regularly review antivirus status across your infrastructure to ensure all systems are protected.
monitor_regularly.title: Monitor Regularly

privileged_agents.description: Ensure agents run with elevated privileges (root/administrator) to enable full antivirus management capabilities.
privileged_agents.title: Run Agents with Privileges

role_based_access.description: Restrict antivirus management permissions to trusted administrators only, especially REMOVE_ANTIVIRUS and DISABLE_ANTIVIRUS roles.
role_based_access.title: Use Role-Based Access Control

standards.description: Use the default configuration feature to ensure consistent antivirus deployment across all hosts of the same operating system.
standards.title: Maintain Consistent Standards

test_deployments.description: Test antivirus deployment on non-production hosts before rolling out to production systems.
test_deployments.title: Test Deployments

defaults.description: SysManage allows administrators to define which antivirus software should be deployed for each operating system. These defaults are used when deploying antivirus to hosts.
defaults.permissions_description: Managing antivirus defaults requires the MANAGE_ANTIVIRUS_DEFAULTS security role. Contact your administrator if you need this permission.
defaults.step1: Navigate to Settings in the main menu
defaults.step3: For each operating system, select the desired antivirus package from the dropdown
defaults.step4: Select "None" to disable automatic antivirus deployment for that OS
defaults.title: Setting Default Antivirus Software

deployment.overview_description: Antivirus deployment in SysManage is a fully automated process that installs and configures the antivirus software appropriate for the target host's operating system.

process.description: The deployment process varies slightly by platform but generally follows these steps:

linux_bsd.step2: ClamAV and related packages are downloaded and installed
linux_bsd.step3: freshclam service is enabled and started (updates virus definitions)
linux_bsd.step4: clamd service is enabled and started (real-time scanning daemon)
linux_bsd.step5: Initial virus definition update is performed
linux_bsd.step6: Agent reports deployment status back to server

macos.step2: freshclam is configured and virus definitions are updated
macos.step3: ClamAV service is started via brew services

process.title: What Happens During Deployment

windows.step1: ClamAV for Windows installer is downloaded
windows.step3: ClamAV Windows service is configured and started

requirements.active_host: Host must be active and connected to the server
requirements.default_configured: A default antivirus package must be configured for the host's operating system
requirements.network: Host must have internet access to download antivirus packages
requirements.privileged_agent: Agent must be running with elevated privileges (root/administrator)
requirements.title: Prerequisites

steps.step1: Navigate to Hosts in the main menu
steps.step2: Click on the desired host to view its details
steps.step3: Scroll to the Antivirus card on the host detail page
steps.step4: If no antivirus is detected, you will see a "Deploy Antivirus" button
steps.step6: Confirm the deployment in the dialog that appears
steps.step7: Monitor the deployment progress - the antivirus card will update when deployment completes
steps.title: Deploying via Web Interface

deployment.title: Deploying Antivirus Software

indicators.disabled: Disabled (Yellow): Antivirus is installed but not running

monitoring.overview_description: SysManage agents automatically detect installed antivirus software and report status information to the server. This information is displayed in real-time on the host detail page.

status.enabled_state: Enabled/Disabled: Whether the antivirus service is currently running
status.install_path: Installation Path: Where the antivirus software is installed
status.last_updated: Last Updated: When the status information was last refreshed
status.software_name: Software Name: The name of the detected antivirus software (e.g., "ClamAV")

status.version: Version: The installed version of the antivirus software

overview.description: SysManage provides centralized management of open-source antivirus software across all supported platforms. The platform automates deployment, enables real-time monitoring of antivirus status, and provides tools to enable, disable, or remove antivirus software remotely.

features.auto_detection: Automatic Detection: Agents automatically detect and report antivirus software status
features.centralized_config: Centralized Configuration: Define default antivirus packages per operating system
features.cross_platform: Cross-Platform Support: Manage antivirus on Linux, BSD, macOS, and Windows systems
features.realtime_status: Real-time Status: Monitor antivirus status, version, and enabled/disabled state
features.remote_deployment: Remote Deployment: Deploy antivirus software from the web interface with one click
features.service_control: Service Control: Enable or disable antivirus services remotely

security_roles.deploy: Allows user to deploy antivirus software to hosts
security_roles.disable: Allows user to disable antivirus services on hosts
security_roles.enable: Allows user to enable antivirus services on hosts
security_roles.manage_defaults: Allows user to configure default antivirus packages per operating system
security_roles.remove: Allows user to completely remove antivirus software from hosts

disable.description: To temporarily disable antivirus without removing it:
disable.step1: Navigate to the host detail page
disable.step2: In the Antivirus card, click Disable Antivirus
disable.step3: Confirm the action in the dialog
disable.step4: The agent will stop the antivirus service
disable.title: Disabling Antivirus
disable.warning: ⚠️ Security Warning: Disabling antivirus leaves the system vulnerable to malware. Only disable antivirus when necessary for troubleshooting.

enable.description: If antivirus is installed but disabled, you can enable it remotely:
enable.step1: Navigate to the host detail page
enable.step2: In the Antivirus card, click Enable Antivirus
enable.step3: Confirm the action in the dialog
enable.step4: The agent will start the antivirus service and report the new status
enable.title: Enabling Antivirus

remove.description: To completely remove antivirus software from a host:
remove.step1: Navigate to the host detail page
remove.step2: In the Antivirus card, click Remove Antivirus
remove.step5: The agent will stop services, uninstall packages, and clean up configuration files
remove.title: Removing Antivirus
remove.warning1: Antivirus removal is irreversible from the web interface
remove.warning2: System will be unprotected after removal
remove.warning3: Virus definition databases and logs will be deleted
remove.warning4: Use the "Deploy Antivirus" button to reinstall if needed

architecture.agent_collector: Firewall Collector (src/sysmanage_agent/operations/firewall_collector.py): Detects and parses firewall status
architecture.flow_step1: User clicks firewall button in web interface
architecture.flow_step10: Frontend receives real-time update via WebSocket
architecture.flow_step2: Frontend sends REST API request to server
architecture.flow_step4: Server enqueues firewall command message for agent
architecture.flow_step5: Agent retrieves command from outbound message queue
architecture.flow_step8: Agent sends status update to server via inbound queue

architecture.server_models: Database Models (backend/persistence/models/core.py): FirewallStatus table for storing firewall state

best_practices.configuration_practice1: Regularly review and audit firewall rules
best_practices.configuration_practice2: Use restart operation after manual configuration changes
best_practices.configuration_practice3: Document all manual firewall rule modifications
best_practices.configuration_practice4: Implement firewall configuration as code where possible
best_practices.deployment_practice1: Test firewall deployment on non-production hosts first
best_practices.deployment_practice2: Ensure you have alternate access (console, IPMI) before enabling firewalls on remote hosts
best_practices.deployment_practice3: Document custom port requirements before deployment
best_practices.deployment_practice4: Use gradual rollout for firewall deployment across large infrastructures
best_practices.monitoring_practice1: Set up alerts for hosts with disabled firewalls
best_practices.monitoring_practice2: Regularly review open ports across your infrastructure
best_practices.monitoring_practice3: Verify firewall status as part of security audits
best_practices.monitoring_practice4: Monitor for unexpected firewall status changes
best_practices.security_practice1: Grant firewall management roles only to trusted administrators
best_practices.security_practice2: Monitor firewall disable operations in security logs
best_practices.security_practice3: Implement alerts for firewall status changes
best_practices.security_practice4: Regularly verify firewall status matches security policy
best_practices.security_practice5: Use principle of least privilege when opening ports
best_practices.title: Best Practices

firewall.meta_description: Complete guide to deploying, managing, and monitoring firewall software across your infrastructure with SysManage.

monitoring.automatic_updates_description: The agent automatically collects firewall status and sends updates to the server:
monitoring.description: The Firewall Status card on each host detail page provides real-time information about the firewall configuration.

info.firewall_name: Firewall Name: The detected firewall software (e.g., ufw, firewalld, IPFW, PF, NPF, Windows Defender Firewall)
info.ipv4_ports: IPv4 Ports: Ports configured for IPv4 traffic
info.ipv6_ports: IPv6 Ports: Ports configured for IPv6 traffic
info.last_updated: Last Updated: Timestamp of the last status update from the agent
info.tcp_ports: TCP Open Ports: List of TCP ports with active allow rules
info.udp_ports: UDP Open Ports: List of UDP ports with active allow rules

monitoring.title: Firewall Status Monitoring

updates.after_operations: Immediate update after deploy, enable, disable, or restart operations
updates.periodic: Periodic collection as part of regular system metrics gathering
updates.realtime_display: Real-time display updates via WebSocket communication

deploy.automatic_ports_description: When deploying or enabling a firewall, SysManage automatically opens the following ports:
deploy.description: The Deploy Firewall operation installs and configures firewall software on hosts that don't have it installed or enabled.
deploy.port_agent: Agent Communication Port: Dynamically determined from agent configuration
deploy.port_server: Server Ports (if applicable): Opened if SysManage server is detected on the host
deploy.step1: Navigate to the host detail page for the target system
deploy.step4: SysManage will automatically detect the appropriate firewall for the OS
deploy.step5: The agent installs the firewall package and configures initial rules
deploy.step6: Agent communication ports are automatically opened to prevent lockout
deploy.title: Deploy Firewall

disable.description: The Disable Firewall operation stops the firewall service, allowing all traffic through the host.
disable.step1: Navigate to the host detail page
disable.step2: Click the Disable Firewall button in the Firewall Status card
disable.step4: Firewall status will update to "Disabled" in real-time
disable.title: Disable Firewall
disable.warning_description: Disabling the firewall removes network protection and exposes all services to the network. Only disable firewalls in controlled environments or when troubleshooting network connectivity issues.

enable.description: The Enable Firewall operation starts the firewall service on a host where firewall software is installed but disabled.
enable.step1: Navigate to the host detail page
enable.step2: Click the Enable Firewall button in the Firewall Status card
enable.step3: The firewall service will start and required ports will be opened
enable.step4: Firewall status will update to "Enabled" in real-time
enable.title: Enable Firewall

restart.description: The Restart Firewall operation stops and starts the firewall service, reloading configuration and rules.
restart.step1: Navigate to the host detail page
restart.step2: Click the Restart Firewall button in the Firewall Status card
restart.step3: The firewall service will restart and reload all rules
restart.step4: Use this after manually modifying firewall configuration files
restart.title: Restart Firewall

overview.description: SysManage provides centralized management of firewall software across all supported platforms. The platform automates deployment, enables real-time monitoring of firewall status, and provides tools to enable, disable, restart, or deploy firewall software remotely.

features.agent_protection: Agent Protection: Automatically opens required ports for agent communication when enabling firewall
features.auto_detection: Automatic Detection: Agents automatically detect and report firewall software status
features.cross_platform: Cross-Platform Support: Manage firewalls on Linux, BSD, macOS, and Windows systems
features.port_monitoring: Port Monitoring: Real-time monitoring of open ports and firewall rules
features.realtime_status: Real-time Status: Monitor firewall status, enabled/disabled state, and port configuration
features.remote_deployment: Remote Deployment: Deploy firewall software from the web interface with one click
features.service_control: Service Control: Enable, disable, or restart firewall services remotely

overview.title: Overview

roles.assigning_note: Note: When roles are assigned, SysManage automatically sends the new firewall configuration to the agent, which updates the host's firewall rules in real-time.
roles.assigning_roles_description: Once you've created firewall roles, you can assign them to hosts from the host detail page:
roles.assigning_step1: Navigate to the host detail page for the target host
roles.assigning_step4: Select one or more roles to assign from the dropdown
roles.benefit_auditing: Auditing: Clear visibility into which ports are open on which hosts and why
roles.benefit_consistency: Consistency: Ensure all hosts with the same role have identical firewall configurations
roles.benefit_efficiency: Efficiency: Define once, apply to many hosts
roles.benefit_flexibility: Flexibility: Easily add or remove roles from hosts as requirements change
roles.common_ports_description: When adding ports, you can select from a dropdown of common ports for quick configuration:
roles.creating_role_step1: Navigate to Configuration in the main navigation menu
roles.creating_role_step2: Click on Firewall Roles in the submenu
roles.creating_role_step4: Enter a descriptive name for the role (e.g., "Web Server", "Database Server")
roles.creating_role_step5: Add ports to the role by specifying the port number and protocol (TCP/UDP)
roles.creating_role_step6: Select whether the port should be open for IPv4, IPv6, or both
roles.description: Firewall Roles provide centralized, reusable firewall configurations that can be assigned to multiple hosts. Instead of configuring firewall rules individually on each host, you define roles that specify which ports should be open, and then assign those roles to hosts as needed.
roles.example_assign: This role can then be assigned to all your web servers, ensuring consistent firewall configuration across your web infrastructure.
roles.example_description: Here's an example of creating a typical web server firewall role:
roles.example_port_ssh: Port 22 (TCP) - SSH access (usually already included by default)
roles.platform_support_description: Firewall Roles work across all platforms supported by SysManage:
roles.port_config_number: Port Number: The port to open (1-65535, or 0 for any port)
roles.port_config_tcp: TCP: Enable to allow TCP traffic on this port
roles.port_config_udp: UDP: Enable to allow UDP traffic on this port
roles.removing_note: Note: When a role is removed, the ports that were opened by that role are automatically closed, unless another assigned role also requires those ports.
roles.removing_roles_description: To remove a firewall role from a host:
roles.removing_step1: Navigate to the host detail page
roles.removing_step2: Find the role you want to remove in the Firewall Roles section
roles.removing_step3: Click the Remove button next to the role
roles.title: Firewall Roles
roles.assigning_description: Once a firewall role is created, assign it to hosts to apply the firewall configuration.
roles.assigning_step6: The ports defined in the role will be opened on the host's firewall
roles.benefit1: Centralized Management: Define firewall rules in one place and apply to many hosts
roles.benefit2: Consistency: Ensure all hosts with the same role have identical firewall configurations
roles.benefit3: Reusability: Create roles for common server types (web server, database, etc.) and reuse them
roles.benefit4: Protocol Control: Configure TCP and/or UDP for each port independently
roles.benefit5: IP Version Support: Configure IPv4 and/or IPv6 rules separately
roles.benefit6: Common Ports: Quick selection of well-known ports (SSH, HTTP, HTTPS, etc.)
roles.common_port_more: And many more including SMTP, IMAP, Redis, MongoDB, RDP, VNC, NTP, SNMP, OpenVPN, and WireGuard
roles.creating_description: Create firewall roles to define sets of ports that should be open for specific types of servers.
roles.creating_step1: Navigate to Administration in the main menu
roles.creating_step2: Select Firewall Roles from the submenu
roles.creating_step4: Enter a descriptive name for the role (e.g., "Web Server", "Database Server")
roles.creating_step5: Add ports using either the common ports dropdown or by entering custom port numbers
roles.creating_step6: For each port, select the protocol (TCP, UDP, or both) and IP version (IPv4, IPv6, or both)
roles.multiple_roles_description: A host can have multiple firewall roles assigned. All ports from all assigned roles will be opened. Duplicate ports are handled automatically.
roles.removing_description: When a firewall role is removed from a host, the ports defined in that role are closed.
roles.removing_step5: The ports from that role will be removed from the host's firewall
roles.removing_warning_description: SSH (port 22) and agent communication ports are always preserved to prevent lockout, even when removing firewall roles.

security.assignment_step4: Select the appropriate firewall management roles
security.description: Firewall management operations are protected by role-based access control (RBAC). Users must have specific security roles to perform firewall operations.
security.role_assignment_description: Administrators can assign firewall roles to users through the user management interface:
security.role_deploy: DEPLOY_FIREWALL: Required to deploy firewall software to hosts
security.role_view: VIEW_FIREWALL_STATUS: Required to view firewall status (automatically granted with any firewall role)
security.title: Security and Access Control

bsd.description: BSD platforms support multiple firewall frameworks:
bsd.ipfw: IPFW: FreeBSD's stateful firewall with flexible rule syntax
bsd.npf: NPF: NetBSD's modern, efficient packet filter designed for scalability
bsd.pf: PF: OpenBSD's sophisticated packet filter available on all BSD variants
bsd.title: BSD Systems

linux.description: SysManage supports the following firewall solutions on Linux:
linux.firewalld: firewalld - Dynamic firewall management tool for RHEL, CentOS, Fedora, and openSUSE
linux.iptables: iptables - Legacy netfilter firewall for Linux systems
linux.nftables: nftables - Modern packet filtering framework replacing iptables
linux.title: Linux Systems
linux.ufw: UFW (Uncomplicated Firewall) - User-friendly firewall frontend for iptables on Ubuntu/Debian systems

macos.pf: PF (Packet Filter): OpenBSD's packet filter ported to macOS
macos.title: macOS

windows.defender: Windows Defender Firewall: Integrated host-based firewall with advanced security features
windows.profiles: Support for Domain, Private, and Public network profiles
windows.rules: Inbound and outbound rule management via PowerShell and netsh
windows.title: Windows

firewall.title: Firewall Management - SysManage Administration

troubleshooting.deploy_fails_solution1: Verify the agent is running with elevated privileges (root/Administrator)
troubleshooting.deploy_fails_solution2: Check network connectivity to package repositories
troubleshooting.deploy_fails_solution3: Review agent logs for detailed error messages
troubleshooting.deploy_fails_solution4: Ensure package manager is functioning (apt, yum, dnf, pkg, etc.)
troubleshooting.deploy_fails_symptoms: Symptoms: Deploy operation returns error or times out
troubleshooting.locked_out_solution1: This should not happen - agent automatically opens required ports
troubleshooting.locked_out_solution2: Access host via console or out-of-band management
troubleshooting.locked_out_solution3: Manually disable firewall and check agent configuration
troubleshooting.locked_out_solution4: Verify agent port configuration matches firewall rules
troubleshooting.locked_out_symptoms: Symptoms: Cannot connect to agent after enabling firewall
troubleshooting.no_firewall_detected_solution1: Click "Deploy Firewall" to install firewall software automatically
troubleshooting.no_firewall_detected_solution2: Verify the agent has privileges to detect firewall status
troubleshooting.no_firewall_detected_solution3: Check agent logs for firewall detection errors

access_groups.description: Group-based RBAC scoping. Each access group lists which hosts the members can see and act on. Pair with security roles (RBAC) for the per-permission gating. Default access group lets any user in it auto-approve registrations matching its scope.
access_groups.title: Access Groups (Pro+)

antivirus.description: Default AV product per platform, default scan schedule (daily / weekly / monthly + time-of-day), definition-update cadence. Used as starting values when creating a new AV deployment policy. Permission: Manage Antivirus Defaults
antivirus.title: Antivirus Defaults (Pro+)

auto_approve.description: UUID tokens that, when included in a host's first registration, bypass manual approval. Created automatically when a child host is provisioned with auto-approve enabled. List + revoke from this tab. Tokens are single-use: cleared from the database the moment the matching agent registers.
auto_approve.title: Auto-Approve Tokens

branding.description: Replace the default SysManage logo + report header / footer for white-label deployments. Logos are uploaded as PNG / SVG; report templates are HTML+CSS that the reporting engine substitutes per generated report.
branding.title: Branding (Pro+)

compliance.description: Allowlist / blocklist of packages. Hosts with a blocklisted package present (or required-allowlist package missing) appear in the Compliance Engine's non-compliant report. Configurable per fleet group.
compliance.title: Package Compliance Lists (Pro+)

email.description: SMTP relay used for password-reset emails, alert notifications (Pro+), and report delivery (Pro+). Reads / writes email.smtp.*
email.title: Email / SMTP

firewall.description: Default firewall roles available for assignment, default policy templates per platform (UFW / firewalld / pf / npf / Windows / macOS), and rule conflict-detection sensitivity. Permissions: Add Firewall Role
firewall.title: Firewall Defaults (Pro+)

grafana.description: Grafana base URL + admin token. The engine auto-provisions the SysManage Postgres datasource and the starter dashboard pack on connect. See the Grafana setup guide
grafana.title: Grafana (Pro+)

graylog.description: Graylog input URL, port, TLS CA, and default sources to forward (syslog / journald / log file globs). Per-host attach happens via the host's Connect to Graylog action. Permission: Enable Graylog Integration
graylog.title: Graylog (Pro+)

header.description: Section-by-section reference for the Settings page. Each section documents what the controls do, where they're stored, and which permission gates them.

settings.meta_description: Reference for every section of the SysManage Settings page: SMTP, OpenTelemetry, Graylog, antivirus and firewall defaults, branding, auto-approve tokens, access groups, registration keys, upgrade profiles, package compliance lists.

otel.description: Default OTEL backend endpoint (OTLP HTTP / gRPC / Prometheus remote-write), default sampling rate, default exporter TLS settings. Per-host overrides happen on the host's Deploy OpenTelemetry dialog. Permission: Deploy OpenTelemetry
otel.title: OpenTelemetry (Pro+)

reg_keys.description: Pre-shared keys an unattended-install agent can present at registration in lieu of an approval click. Useful for OS images that bake the agent in: the registration-key value goes in the agent's /etc/sysmanage-agent.yaml
reg_keys.title: Registration Keys

security_roles.description: Permission catalog: every action in the UI is gated by a named permission (e.g. Reboot Host
security_roles.title: Security Roles (RBAC)

upgrade_profiles.description: Named software-update policies: which packages to auto-apply, which to defer, blackout windows, max-concurrency. Assigned to fleet groups so a "production" profile can be more conservative than "staging".
upgrade_profiles.title: Upgrade Profiles (Pro+)

mfa.enroll_1: User opens Profile → Security Information and clicks Enable MFA.
mfa.enroll_2: Server generates a fresh TOTP secret (encrypted at rest with Fernet) and returns the otpauth:// provisioning URI plus the raw base32 secret.
mfa.enroll_3: User adds the account to their authenticator app (scan the URI with a QR generator, or paste the secret manually).
mfa.enroll_4: User enters the first 6-digit code their app shows; on success the server issues 10 single-use backup codes shown exactly once.
mfa.enroll_5: User saves the backup codes somewhere safe, ticks the acknowledgement, and is fully enrolled.
mfa.intro: Phase 10.3 ships an OSS Multi-Factor Authentication implementation. MFA is opt-in by default, and a global "admin-required" toggle (with a configurable grace period) lets administrators force enrollment fleet-wide.
mfa.login_1: User submits username + password as usual.
mfa.login_2: If they're enrolled, the server returns {mfa_required: true, pending_token: "..."} instead of a session token. The pending token is short-lived (5 minutes).
mfa.login_3: The login UI prompts for a verification code — either a current TOTP code or one of the backup codes (in XXXX-XXXX format, but the dash is optional).
mfa.login_4: The client posts pending-token + code to /api/auth/mfa/verify; on success it receives a normal session token.
mfa.method_backup: Backup codes: Single-use Argon2-hashed recovery codes (10 by default) issued at enrollment for when the user loses access to their authenticator.
mfa.method_totp: TOTP (Time-based OTP): Any RFC 6238 authenticator app — Google Authenticator, Authy, 1Password, Bitwarden, etc.
mfa.recovery_admin: Admin-driven: delete the user's user_mfa_enrollment row directly (or via the Users admin page) — the user can sign in with their password and re-enrol on next login.
mfa.recovery_user: User-driven: sign in with a backup code, then re-enrol from the profile page (this rotates the secret and issues a fresh set of backup codes).
mfa.roadmap: Roadmap: SMS / email-code fallback, FIDO2/WebAuthn — tracked in the 10.3 follow-up tasks.
mfa.setting_admin_required: admin_required — when true, users who pass the grace period without enrolling are blocked at login until they enrol.
mfa.setting_backup_count: backup_code_count — codes issued per enrollment / regeneration (default 10; 0 disables backup codes entirely).
mfa.setting_grace: grace_period_days — days from account creation during which a user can sign in without MFA even when admin_required is on (default 14).
mfa.title: Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA)

airgap.meta_description: Phase 11 dual-server architecture for managing hosts on isolated networks. Collector / Repository roles, optical-media transfer, ed25519-signed manifests, and per-distro install dispatch via Copr / OBS / PPA.

header.title: Air-Gapped Deployment (Phase 11)
header.description: Dual-server architecture for managing hosts on isolated networks that have no internet access. One server (Collector) lives on the public side, mirrors upstream package and CVE feeds, and burns the result to optical media. A second server (Repository) lives on the air-gapped side, ingests the media, serves it as a local mirror, and lets managed agents update normally without ever touching the internet.

architecture.title: Architecture
architecture.summary: Two servers, both running the same SysManage binary. The role: key in sysmanage.yaml picks which half of the air-gap pair this server runs as. License is the same Enterprise SKU on both — one license includes both engines.
architecture.collector: Collector — public-side. Loads airgap_collector_engine. Captures package mirrors (apt-mirror, dnf reposync, pkg fetch), CVE / NVD snapshots, and CIS / STIG compliance feeds. Bundles the result into one or more ISO images, signs the manifest with ed25519, optionally burns to physical media.
architecture.repository: Repository — private-side. Loads airgap_repository_engine. Ingests optical media, verifies the signature against the configured public key, verifies per-file SHA-256 hashes, copies the payload into a local mirror tree, and generates native repo metadata so each managed agent's package manager can consume it. Also rewrites each agent's APT / DNF / pkg config to point at the local mirror.
architecture.standard: Standard — the default. No air-gap; manages hosts directly online. This is what every pre-Phase-11 deployment was, and what new deployments default to when role: is omitted from sysmanage.yaml.
architecture.no_shared_db: The two halves never share a database. Collector and Repository communicate exclusively through the disc — there is no network connection between them by design. The Repository records the manifest's signer_fingerprint + collector_iso_label so audit trails can correlate runs across the gap by inspecting both halves' databases after the fact.

config.title: Setting the server role
config.summary: Set the role from the web UI — no config-file editing required. Go to <strong>Settings → Server Role
config.validation: The role is also exposed via the unauthenticated <code>GET /api/v1/server-info
config.degraded_chip: If the role is set to Collector or Repository but the corresponding Pro+ engine isn't loaded (typically a license problem), the chip turns red and a tooltip explains what's missing. The server keeps running in degraded mode so you can fix the license without losing access to the rest of the UI.
config.persistence: The choice is stored in the server's database (the <code>server_configuration

collection_cycle.title: Collection cycle (Collector side)
collection_cycle.summary: Triggered by an operator (UI, API, or cron) on the public-side server. The Phase 12 "Option B" rework sources the bundle from snapshots of configured mirror_repository trees rather than running a second upstream fetch per run — one download from upstream (via the repository_mirroring engine's normal sync) is reused for both the LAN mirror and the air-gap bundle. The engine emits a deployment plan that runs locally on the collector itself; it does not dispatch to managed agents.
collection_cycle.step_request: Request — POST /api/v1/airgap/collector/runs with a body listing each target as a {mirror_id} picked from /api/mirror-repositories, plus iso_label, media_size_bytes, include_cve, include_compliance, and optional burn_device. Distro / version / repos are derived server-side from each mirror's known_version catalog entry.
collection_cycle.step_validate: Validation — each picked mirror must exist, be enabled, have a known_version_id, and share a host_id with the other picks (the collection plan dispatches to one agent, so cross-host targets aren't supported in v1). Version strings are constrained to a tight regex so they cannot smuggle shell metacharacters into the dispatch templates; iso_label is constrained the same way.
collection_cycle.step_mirror: Mirror (snapshot-sourced) — once all snapshots are ready, the orchestrator builds build_snapshot_collection_run_plan with a source_snapshots map of "<distro>:<version>" → snapshot directory path. The plan rsyncs each snapshot tree into per-target staging directories. No upstream apt-mirror / reposync / pkg fetch runs at collection time — the bundle is provably byte-for-byte identical to the snapshot. Each rsync has a 4-hour timeout ceiling; the agent's in-flight journal (Phase 11.6) covers the multi-hour run across WebSocket reconnects.
collection_cycle.step_cve: CVE / compliance snapshot — if requested, NVD and vendor-advisory feeds are captured at the same instant so the resulting media set has a coherent point-in-time view of public vulnerability data.
collection_cycle.step_iso: ISO build — xorriso -as mkisofs bundles the staging tree into one ISO at /var/lib/sysmanage/airgap-iso/<run_id>.iso with the manifest embedded at /manifest.json. The post-build step computes the ISO's SHA-256 for the transfer log.
collection_cycle.step_sign: Sign — the manifest is canonicalized (sorted keys, no whitespace) and signed with the operator's ed25519 private key. The envelope carries the signature, the SHA-256 of the public key as a fingerprint, the algorithm identifier, and the manifest format version.
collection_cycle.step_burn: Burn (optional) — if burn_device is set on the run row, the orchestrator advances ISO_BUILT → BURNING by dispatching build_burn_plan at that device path (typically /dev/sr0). The burn-step timeout ceiling is 4 hours so slow drives don't trip a watchdog mid-write. When burn_device is NULL the run goes ISO_BUILT → COMPLETE directly — the "build a downloadable ISO and stop" flow that doesn't touch optical media.
collection_cycle.multidisc_note: Multi-disc spanning (Phase 12). When sum(target.source_snapshot.size_bytes) > run.media_size_bytes the orchestrator routes to build_snapshot_multidisc_collection_plan, which first-fit-decreasing bin-packs whole target trees onto discs. Each disc gets its own staging dir + rsync + xorriso + sha256 in one inline plan; output is <run_id>-disc-<N>.iso. The orchestrator surfaces a clear error if any single target's tree exceeds the disc size — file-level splitting (chopping one mirror tree across discs) is deferred; the operator picks larger media (BD-25 → BD-50) or splits into separate runs.
collection_cycle.step_snapshot: Auto-snapshot — at run creation, one snapshot is dispatched per target mirror. Each AirgapCollectionTarget row pins its source_snapshot_id to the placeholder snapshot row so the orchestrator knows which snapshot to bundle once the agent reports completion. Snapshots run in parallel; the run sits at QUEUED until every target's last_snapshot_message_id clears.
collection_cycle.step_download: Download — GET /api/v1/airgap/collector/runs/{id}/iso streams the ISO file. For multi-disc runs, GET /runs/{id}/discs lists the disc files and /iso?disc=N picks a specific one. The Air-Gap Collections UI auto-detects multi-disc runs and pops a picker dialog when the operator clicks Download.
collection_cycle.lifecycle: Lifecycle states the airgap_run_tick orchestrator drives: QUEUED (waiting on per-target snapshots) → MIRRORING (rsync from snapshot trees into staging) → STAGING_COMPLETE (single-disc only; multi-disc skips this) → BUILDING_ISO (xorriso) → ISO_BUILT → BURNING (only when burn_device set) → COMPLETE. Any failure transitions the run to FAILED with the surfaced error message visible in the UI status column.

ingestion_cycle.title: Ingestion cycle (Repository side)
ingestion_cycle.summary: Operator inserts the optical media into the air-gapped server, then triggers ingestion via the UI or API. The repository engine refuses to serve any byte until the signature and file hashes have verified.
ingestion_cycle.step_mount: Mount — the engine emits a deployment plan that mounts the ISO read-only at /mnt/sysmanage-airgap-ingest.
ingestion_cycle.step_verify_sig: Verify signature — the manifest envelope is decoded; the engine refuses to ingest a manifest whose format_version is newer than its own software version (forward-compat fail-safe). The signature is verified against the public key configured in the repository's sysmanage.yaml. Strict mode (the default) rejects the HMAC-SHA-256 fallback envelope that the collector produces only when cryptography isn't installed.
ingestion_cycle.step_verify_hashes: Verify file hashes — every file listed in the manifest is streamed through SHA-256 and compared against the manifest's expected hash. The first mismatch aborts the entire ingestion; the partial copy is rolled back.
ingestion_cycle.step_copy: Copy — once verification passes, rsync -a --delete-after copies the payload from the mount point to the local repository root at /var/lib/sysmanage/airgap-repo.
ingestion_cycle.step_metadata: Generate native repo metadata — per distro family: apt-ftparchive packages | gzip > Packages.gz for APT, createrepo_c --update for DNF / zypper, pkg repo for FreeBSD, apk index for Alpine.
ingestion_cycle.step_unmount: Unmount — the ISO is unmounted; the operator can eject the disc.
ingestion_cycle.step_repoint: Repoint agents — the engine emits a per-host plan that rewrites each managed agent's package-manager configuration: /etc/apt/sources.list.d/sysmanage-airgap.list for Debian / Ubuntu, /etc/yum.repos.d/sysmanage-airgap.repo for RHEL family / openSUSE, /usr/local/etc/pkg/repos/sysmanage-airgap.conf for FreeBSD. Existing agents pick up the change on their next package operation.

install_channels.title: Per-distro install channels
install_channels.summary: For new child hosts created on the air-gapped network, sysmanage-agent itself needs to install from a substitutable channel — direct GitHub-release downloads can't be substituted with a private mirror. Phase 11.8 switched the per-distro agent_install_commands to consume the published upstream channels instead:
install_channels.apt: Ubuntu / Debian — Launchpad PPA (ppa:bceverly/sysmanage-agent). Repository's build_agent_repoint_plan rewrites /etc/apt/sources.list.d/ to point at the local mirror before the install runs.
install_channels.dnf: Fedora / RHEL / Rocky / Alma / Oracle Linux / CentOS Stream — Fedora Copr (bceverly/sysmanage-agent). Substitutable via /etc/yum.repos.d/sysmanage-airgap.repo.
install_channels.zypper: openSUSE / SLES — Open Build Service (home:bceverly/sysmanage-agent). Substitutable via the same yum/zypper repo file pattern.
install_channels.bsd: FreeBSD / OpenBSD / NetBSD — currently direct downloads. The repository engine can still rewrite pkg.conf to point at the local mirror, so the agent comes up via a different path than its first-boot script expects but still without internet access. A formal upstream ports / pkgsrc submission is tracked as a follow-up.

compliance.title: Compliance context across the gap
compliance.summary: Phase 11.3 wires the repository's freshness state into compliance reports so operators can distinguish two failure modes that look identical without context:
compliance.not_applied: not_applied — a newer version is on the local mirror but hasn't been installed on this host yet. Fix locally; cheap.
compliance.not_transferred: not_transferred — the public CVE feed (captured by the collector at last media transfer) lists a fix that isn't on the local mirror at all. Fix requires a new collection / burn / ingest cycle; expensive.
compliance.current: current — the host's installed version matches the mirror's latest, and no public-side CVE has surfaced since last transfer.
compliance.freshness: Each repository tracks days since last ingest. Compliance UI surfaces a freshness label: current (≤ 7 days), stale (8–30 days), very_stale (> 30 days), or never. Use this to size the air-gap transfer cadence against your organization's tolerance for delayed patch availability.

signature.title: Ingestion fails with "signature does not match manifest payload"
signature.body: The repository's configured public key doesn't match the private key that signed this disc. Either the disc was tampered with in transit, or the operator regenerated the keypair on the collector without distributing the new public key to the repository. Re-export the public key from the collector and replace the value in the repository's sysmanage.yaml.

format_version.title: Ingestion fails with "manifest format_version exceeds repository's max"
format_version.body: The collector has been upgraded to a new SysManage version that changed the manifest format, but the repository hasn't been upgraded yet. Upgrade the repository server to the same version, then re-ingest. The format-version check is a deliberate forward-compat fail-safe — older repositories cannot be tricked into ingesting media with new fields they don't understand.

role_chip_red.title: Role chip in the header bar is red
role_chip_red.body: The role: in sysmanage.yaml is set to collector or repository, but the corresponding Pro+ engine isn't loaded. Most common cause: the Enterprise license isn't valid (expired, missing, or doesn't include the air-gap engines). Check the License page in Settings; if the license is fine, check /var/log/sysmanage/server.log for module-loader errors.

long_subprocess.title: Multi-hour collection cycle gets killed mid-run
long_subprocess.body: Should not happen as of Phase 11.6 — the agent now writes a per-plan in-flight journal at ~/.sysmanage-agent/inflight/<message_id>.json before each subprocess starts, with a 30-second heartbeat. If the agent process restarts (or the WebSocket bounces), the journal carries enough state for the agent to either re-attach to the still-live subprocess or emit a synthetic command_result so the server's mirror row clears. If you see a hung mirror anyway, file an issue with the contents of the inflight journal.

airgap_runbook.meta_description: Phase 11 operational SOP for air-gapped deployments: chain-of-custody for optical media, ed25519 key rotation cadence, disc labeling conventions, transport-loss procedures, signature-failure incident response.

header.title: Air-Gap Operational Runbook
header.description: Operational procedures complementary to the Air-Gap Deployment guide. Where the deployment guide explains the technical architecture, this runbook covers the day-to-day operational concerns: who handles which disc, how often keys rotate, what to do when transport goes wrong, and how to respond when a signature verification fails.

custody.title: Chain of custody
custody.summary: Every air-gap disc is a privileged-access artifact: it carries the operator's ed25519 signature and, on the public→private direction, the union of every package the air-gapped fleet will install on its next update cycle. Treat each disc the same way you would treat a signed-installer USB key for production hosts.
custody.label: Label every disc immediately at burn time with: collector hostname, ISO label, run timestamp, and disc N of M (for multi-disc sets). The run timestamp matches what's in the manifest, so a misplaced disc can be cross-referenced against the collector's audit log.
custody.transport_log: Maintain a transport log (paper or air-gapped spreadsheet) listing: who carried each disc set, when it left the public-side facility, when it was inserted at the private-side facility, and the SHA-256 of each ISO at both ends.
custody.storage: Store unburned ISO masters on the collector for at least the retention window of one complete delta-collection cycle (default 30 days) so a lost-in-transit disc set can be re-burned without redoing the mirror sync.
custody.disposal: Destroy obsolete discs (older than two retention windows) by physical shredding rather than deletion — even ingested data carries the operator's signature, which has cryptographic value beyond the package contents themselves.

keys.title: Ed25519 keypair rotation
keys.summary: The collector signs every manifest with an ed25519 private key; the repository verifies against the corresponding public key stored in its trusted-collector keyring directory (/var/lib/sysmanage/airgap/trusted-collectors/). The keypair is generated automatically the first time you set the server role to collector (no manual keygen step). Recommended cadence:
keys.annual_rotation: Annual rotation baseline. Generate a new keypair on the collector, distribute the new public key to the repository (over an existing trusted channel — sneakernet, signed email, or the previous trusted ingest), and run one transition disc that's signed by both keys before retiring the old key.
keys.compromise: Immediate rotation on suspected compromise. Symptoms include: a stolen / lost laptop that holds the collector's private key, an unauthorized signature appearing on a disc the operator didn't burn, or any indication the collector host was compromised. Rotate keys, and treat every disc signed by the old key as untrusted (re-collect + re-burn before ingesting on any repository).
keys.storage: Private-key storage. The collector's private key (/var/lib/sysmanage/airgap/collector-ed25519.pem, generated automatically on role selection, mode 0600) should be readable only by the SysManage server process and never copied off the collector. The ensure_collector_keypair step never overwrites an existing key, so re-selecting the collector role is safe. Backup via a sealed-envelope hardware token (YubiKey, Nitrokey) stored in a different physical location from the collector itself — the same threat model as your CA's signing key.
keys.public_distribution: Public-key distribution. The repository's keyring directory can hold multiple trusted public keys during a rotation window — each as a separate .pub file. New ingestions verify against any key in the directory; remove a retired key only after the last disc signed by it has been ingested. To rotate, drop the new collector's .pub into the keyring (additive — existing keys are never cleared).

transport.title: Transport-loss procedures
transport.summary: When a disc set fails to arrive at the air-gapped facility within its expected transit window:
transport.step_verify: Verify with the courier or carrier that the package was actually dispatched. Most "lost" discs are actually misrouted; reconcile the transport-log entry against tracking data.
transport.step_decide: Decide between waiting and re-burning based on operational urgency. If the air-gapped fleet has a fresh-enough mirror (compliance UI shows current or stale, not very_stale), waiting one additional courier cycle is acceptable.
transport.step_reburn: If re-burning: locate the matching ISO master in the collector's /var/lib/sysmanage/airgap-iso/ retention window, re-burn from the same source ISO so the SHA-256 matches the lost disc's transport-log entry. Do not regenerate the manifest — that would make the new disc look like a separate run to the repository's audit trail.
transport.step_after_arrival: If the original disc arrives after a re-burn has been ingested, log both ingestions in the transport log; the second ingest will succeed (idempotent — same content) and creates an audit-trail crumb that helps reconcile the next cycle's gap analysis.
transport.step_destroy: Physically destroy any disc whose chain-of-custody is broken (carrier handover skipped, package found unsealed, etc.) regardless of whether the contents seem intact. The cost of re-burning is small; the cost of ingesting tampered media on the air-gapped network is large.

signature_failure.title: Signature verification failure
signature_failure.summary: When the repository's ingestion endpoint reports signature does not match manifest payload, treat it as a security incident until proven otherwise:
signature_failure.step_isolate: Do not retry ingestion. Repeated signature-verification failures don't make the data more trustworthy. Set the disc aside in a tagged "pending-investigation" envelope.
signature_failure.step_check_keys: Verify the collector's public key is present in the repository keyring. The most common (benign) cause is a key rotation that didn't propagate fully. Re-export the public key from the collector (/var/lib/sysmanage/airgap/collector-ed25519.pub), compare to the .pub files in the repository's /var/lib/sysmanage/airgap/trusted-collectors/ keyring, drop in the missing key if absent, retry ingestion.
signature_failure.step_check_disc: If keys match, inspect the disc. Tampering would typically also break the per-file SHA-256 verification, so a signature-only failure on a disc whose hashes pass is suspicious. Inspect the disc surface for physical damage; mount the ISO read-only and verify file checksums against the manifest manually.
signature_failure.step_escalate: If the disc cannot be explained by key drift or physical damage, escalate. Quarantine the disc, file an incident with your security team, and rotate the collector's keypair as a precaution. The next collection cycle should be performed on a freshly-imaged collector if the original collector itself is suspect.

cadence.title: Recommended cadences
cadence.summary: Defaults that work for most deployments — adjust against your organization's tolerance for delayed patch availability:
cadence.collection: Collection cycle: weekly, with a delta-mode top-up daily. The weekly full-snapshot ensures the cumulative disc set stays current; the daily delta keeps the air-gapped network within one business day of upstream security advisories.
cadence.transport: Transport: match collection cadence. A disc that sits in transit longer than the next collection cycle is wasteful — schedule courier pickups for the same day as the burn step.
cadence.ingestion: Ingestion: on arrival. Don't queue media; the repository's freshness label drifts toward stale the moment a disc is sitting on the operator's desk instead of being ingested.
cadence.key_rotation: Key rotation: annual baseline; immediate on compromise. Calendar a key-rotation event for the same date every year so it doesn't get forgotten.
cadence.audit_review: Audit review: quarterly. Reconcile the transport log against the collector's AirgapCollectionRun table and the repository's AirgapIngestionRun table. Each pair should match by signer_fingerprint + collector_iso_label; discrepancies surface ingestion gaps before they grow into compliance failures.

related.title: Related documentation
related.settings: Settings reference — license config and the Settings → Server Role selector (collector / repository / standard)

keyprov.title: Automated key provisioning & ingestion
keyprov.summary: Recent releases automate the key-distribution and ingestion steps that the rotation procedures above describe manually. Understanding the automated path tells you what to check when something doesn't work:
keyprov.keygen: Keygen on role selection. Setting the server role to collector in Settings → Server Role mints the ed25519 keypair at the canonical path. The role lives in the database (not sysmanage.yaml), so a fresh server boots as standard and the key is created the moment you choose the collector role.
keyprov.embed: Public key rides the install bundle. When the Pro+ air-gap bundle is built on the collector (scripts/buildAirGapBundle.sh server), it embeds the collector's .pub into the bundle. The repository's overlay installer drops it into /var/lib/sysmanage/airgap/trusted-collectors/ automatically — so a repository provisioned from that bundle trusts the collector with no separate key exchange. Only the public key travels; the private signing key never leaves the collector.
keyprov.strict: Strict verification by default. The repository rejects unsigned and HMAC-fallback manifests unless airgap.verify_strict is set to false in sysmanage.yaml. Leave it strict in production; loosen it only for a dev loop where cryptography isn't installed on the collector.
keyprov.ingest: Ingestion is driven, not manual. POSTing an ISO path to the repository's ingest endpoint inserts a QUEUED AirgapIngestionRun; a background orchestrator then loop-mounts the ISO read-only, verifies the embedded /manifest.json signature against the keyring, rsyncs the verified payload into /var/lib/sysmanage/airgap-repo, and registers a per-distro local repository. Watch a stuck run's status: VERIFYING_SIG means the signature step is running or failed (check the keyring), COPYING means verification passed and the rsync is in flight.

upgrade.title: 8.2 Scheduled Update Profiles ⭐ PRO+
upgrade.intro: POSIX-cron profiles describing recurring fleet updates with security-only and staggered-rollout options.
upgrade.phase10_6: Phase 10.6: the cron parser and per-host dispatch were moved into the Pro+ automation_engine module. Every /api/upgrade-profiles/* route returns 402 Payment Required when the engine isn't loaded.
upgrade.list: List profiles with their next-run timestamps.
upgrade.create: Create. Body: {name, description?, cron, enabled?, security_only?, package_managers?, staggered_window_min?, tag_id?}. cron is validated by automation_engine.validate_cron_expression (lists, ranges, step intervals, day/month names, dom/dow OR-semantics). staggered_window_min is clamped to [0, 720] minutes.
upgrade.trigger: Fire a profile NOW. Resolves the target host set, enqueues an apply_updates command per host (with the profile's flags + staggered-window delay), updates last_run / next_run, and returns the dispatched host list.
upgrade.tick: Driver hook for an external scheduler. Selects every enabled profile where next_run <= now, fires it, and returns the fired list. Idempotent within a tick.

overview.description: SysManage uses a sophisticated message-passing architecture that ensures reliable, ordered communication between distributed agents and the central server. The system employs persistent message queues on both sides with automatic retry logic, priority handling, and state tracking.

challenge.intro: Most enterprise applications face an impossible testing trilemma:
challenge.option1: Production Database Testing → Accurate but slow, complex setup required
challenge.option2: SQLite Testing → Fast but prone to schema drift and compatibility issues
challenge.option3: Mock Everything → Fast but misses real database integration bugs
challenge.solution: We pioneered a Dual Conftest Architecture that delivers the best of all worlds: production accuracy with development speed.
challenge.title: The Challenge We Solved

footer.description: enterprise-grade testing that's fast, maintainable, and comprehensive. Our dual conftest architecture represents a strong baseline for modern application testing.
footer.tagline: This is what confidence looks like. This is SysManage.
footer.title: This is Modern Testing Excellence

impact.fearless_desc: With 100% test coverage and zero flaky tests, developers refactor with complete confidence. Every edge case is tested, every API endpoint validated, every database operation verified.
impact.prod_desc: Automatic schema synchronization means test database always matches production. Zero "works in tests but fails in production" scenarios.
impact.rapid_desc: Sub-60-second test runs mean developers actually run tests during development. No more "I'll test before I push" — tests run continuously.
impact.scalable_desc: As the application grows, the testing infrastructure scales effortlessly. New models auto-sync, new endpoints get drop-in fixtures, new requirements are instantly covered.
impact.title: Business Impact

action_menu.description: Each action is permission-gated. Disabled items render greyed out; hover for the missing-permission tooltip.

header.description: A tab-by-tab tour of the Host Details page. Every tab, every action button, what each does, and which permission gates it.
header.title: Host Details Walkthrough

intro.description: From the Hosts page, click any host name. The Host Details page renders a header (FQDN, status badge, action menu) plus a vertical tab strip. Tabs are conditional: third-party-repos shows only on supported package managers; child-hosts only when the host advertises a hypervisor; ubuntu-pro only on Ubuntu hosts where the agent detected Pro availability.
intro.title: Opening Host Details

host_details.meta_description: Tab-by-tab walkthrough of the Host Details page: every tab, every action button, what each does, and which permission gates it.

tabs.access_desc: Local user accounts, groups, group memberships, and sudoers fragments. Read-only inventory in OSS edition; Pro+ tiers can add / remove user accounts via Add Host Account
tabs.certificates_desc: SSL/TLS certificates discovered on the host (cert store + filesystem scan): issuer, subject, validity, expiry. Useful for cert-rotation tracking. The host's own mTLS client cert (used to authenticate to the SysManage server) is also shown here with its expiry.
tabs.child_hosts_desc: Visible only when the host advertises a hypervisor capability (KVM, bhyve, VMM, LXD, WSL). Each hypervisor renders a status card with capabilities + an Enable/Initialize button when not yet bootstrapped, and a Create button when ready. Child-host list shows per-row Start / Stop / Restart / Delete. See the Virtualization Engine guide
tabs.compliance_desc: Pro+: per-host compliance posture from the Compliance Engine — CIS-style benchmark results plus package-allowlist / blocklist matches. Pass / warn / fail counts and per-rule drilldown.
tabs.diagnostics_desc: Run on-host diagnostics: connectivity (DNS, NTP, server reachability), agent log snapshot, queue backlog, last error. Output streams back to the host details page so you don't need shell access for routine checks.
tabs.hardware_desc: CPU model + cores + frequency, total memory, disk inventory (device, mount, size, free), network interfaces (name, MAC, IP, link state), GPU info where available. Snapshot from the agent's hardware-collection module; updates each time the host re-registers or runs update_inventory
tabs.info_desc: Identity (FQDN, IPv4/IPv6, MAC), platform (OS name + version), agent version, registration / approval state, last-seen heartbeat, and parent-host link if this host is a child of another host.
tabs.security_desc: Firewall state (which firewall is active: ufw / firewalld / pf / npf / Windows Firewall / etc.) plus its current rule set. Antivirus state where applicable (commercial AV detected? sysmanage-managed AV deployed?). Per-host firewall role assignments (Pro+).
tabs.server_roles_desc: Server-role detection: which application roles (web server, database, mail, DNS, file server, etc.) the agent fingerprinted on the host. Used by reports and fleet group dynamic-criteria filters.
tabs.software_changes_desc: Audit trail of installs / upgrades / removals captured between consecutive software-inventory uploads. Useful for "what changed since last week" investigations.
tabs.software_desc: Installed packages from every detected package manager (apt, dnf, yum, zypper, pkg, pkg_add, pkgin, apk, brew, snap, flatpak, choco, scoop, winget, MSI/registry). Search box filters by name; sort by name / version / manager / install date.
tabs.third_party_repos_desc: Per-host third-party repo list (PPAs, third-party apt/yum/zypper repos, COPR, OBS, Flatpak remotes). Add / disable / enable / remove from this tab. See the dedicated guide
tabs.title: Tab-by-Tab Reference
tabs.ubuntu_pro_desc: Visible only on Ubuntu hosts where the agent reports Ubuntu Pro is available. Attach / detach an Ubuntu Pro contract token; manage which Pro services (ESM, livepatch, FIPS, USG) are enabled on the host. Permissions: Attach Ubuntu Pro

api_tokens.description: Long-lived tokens for scripted access to /api/*
api_tokens.title: API Tokens

audit.description: Per-user filtered view of the audit log: every action your account has taken (login, password change, host edits, role assignments, etc.). Useful for verifying recent activity or building a compliance trail.

best_practices.maintenance_1: Keep distributions updated with latest agent installation commands
best_practices.maintenance_2: Monitor child host disk usage to prevent parent host issues
best_practices.maintenance_3: Stop child hosts that are not actively in use
best_practices.maintenance_4: Back up important data before deleting child hosts
best_practices.naming_1: Use descriptive hostnames that include the parent host name and purpose
best_practices.naming_2: Follow a consistent naming pattern: parent-type-distro (e.g., server01-wsl-ubuntu24)
best_practices.security_1: Use strong passwords for child host user accounts
best_practices.security_2: Promptly approve or reject pending child hosts
best_practices.security_3: Regularly review and remove unused child hosts
best_practices.security_4: Apply the principle of least privilege for permissions

child_host.card_description: Create and manage child hosts including WSL instances, containers, and virtual machines with automatic agent deployment.
child_host.card_feature2: Support for LXD, VirtualBox, Hyper-V, and KVM
child_host.card_feature5: Start, stop, restart, and delete operations

create.approval_desc: After creation completes, the new child host's agent will connect to the server. An administrator must approve the new host in the Hosts list, just like any other new agent registration. Once approved, the child host becomes fully managed.
create.field_distribution: Distribution: The operating system to install (e.g., Ubuntu 24.04 LTS)
create.field_hostname: Hostname: Unique identifier for the child host (suggested format: parent-wsl-distro)
create.field_password: Password: Password for the new user account (not stored by SysManage)
create.field_type: Virtualization Type: WSL, LXD, VirtualBox, etc. (based on host capabilities)
create.field_username: Username: The non-root user account to create
create.prereq_distribution: Desired distribution must be enabled in Settings > Distributions
create.prereq_network: Network connectivity for downloading distribution images
create.prereq_permission: User must have the "Create Child Host" permission
create.prereq_virtualization: Virtualization must be enabled on the parent host
create.progress_desc: During creation, SysManage shows real-time progress including:

proplus_notice.description: Creating child hosts requires a Professional+ license with the container_engine module. Community Edition users can view existing child hosts but cannot create new ones.
proplus_notice.title: Professional+ Required

create.step1: Click "Create Child Host" button on the Child Hosts tab
create.step2: Select the virtualization type (if multiple are available)
create.step3: Choose a distribution from the dropdown list
create.step4: Enter a hostname for the new child host
create.step5: Provide a username and password for the initial user account
create.step6: Click "Create" to start the installation process
create.title: Creating Child Hosts

detection.cap_enabled: Enabled Features: Which features are enabled and ready to use
detection.cap_hardware: Hardware Virtualization: CPU support for VT-x/AMD-V extensions
detection.cap_installed: Installed Hypervisors: Which virtualization software is installed
detection.cap_reboot: Pending Configuration: Features that require a reboot to enable
detection.process_desc: SysManage automatically detects virtualization capabilities on each managed host. The agent queries the system to determine which virtualization technologies are available and reports this information to the server.
detection.title: Virtualization Detection
detection.wsl_desc: On Windows hosts, SysManage detects WSL status and can automatically enable WSL if it's not already configured:
detection.wsl_needs_reboot: Needs Reboot: WSL was enabled but requires a system restart
detection.wsl_not_installed: Not Installed: WSL feature is not enabled - can be enabled via SysManage
detection.wsl_ready: Ready: WSL is enabled and ready to create child hosts

distributions.method_apt: APT (Launchpad PPA): For Ubuntu and Debian-based distributions
distributions.method_dnf: DNF (COPR): For Fedora, RHEL, Rocky Linux, AlmaLinux, and Oracle Linux
distributions.method_manual: Manual: Custom installation commands for other distributions
distributions.methods_desc: Each distribution specifies how to install the sysmanage-agent:
distributions.op_add: Add Distribution: Add new distributions with install identifiers and agent configuration
distributions.op_delete: Delete Distribution: Remove distributions that are no longer needed
distributions.op_edit: Edit Distribution: Modify distribution settings, install commands, or display names
distributions.op_enable: Enable/Disable: Control which distributions are available for child host creation

hosts_list.filter_all: All Hosts: Shows both parent and child hosts
hosts_list.filter_desc: The main Hosts list includes a filter toggle for child hosts:
hosts_list.filter_parents: Parents Only: Hides child hosts from the list
hosts_list.nav_children: View Children: Click the "Children" button on a parent host to filter for its child hosts
hosts_list.nav_parent: View Parent: On a child host's detail page, click "View Parent" to navigate to the parent host
