kvm.creation_desc: When creating a KVM virtual machine through SysManage:
kvm.creation_step1: SysManage downloads the cloud image from the distribution's official source
kvm.creation_step2: A new disk image is created and the cloud image is applied as a backing store
kvm.creation_step3: Cloud-init configuration is generated with hostname, user account, and agent settings
kvm.creation_step4: The VM is defined in libvirt with the specified resources (CPU, memory, disk)
kvm.creation_step5: VM boots and cloud-init configures the system, creates the user, and installs the agent
kvm.creation_step6: The sysmanage-agent service starts and connects to your SysManage server
kvm.creation_step7: The VM appears in the Hosts list pending approval (or auto-approved if selected)
kvm.detect_1: KVM kernel modules are loaded (/dev/kvm exists)
kvm.detect_2: libvirt daemon is running and accessible
kvm.detect_3: The virsh command is available and functional
kvm.detect_4: At least one libvirt network is configured
kvm.detection_desc: SysManage automatically detects KVM capability by checking:
kvm.detection_role: When detected, the host is assigned the KVM Host role and KVM appears as an available virtualization type.
kvm.distributions_desc: KVM VMs can be created using cloud images from distributions where the sysmanage-agent is available:
kvm.field_cpus: vCPUs: Number of virtual CPUs - default is 2
kvm.field_disk: Disk Size: Virtual disk size (e.g., "20G", "50G") - default is 20GB
kvm.field_vm_name: VM Name: The libvirt domain name (must be unique on the host)
kvm.form_desc: When creating KVM VMs, additional configuration options are available:
kvm.lifecycle_delete: Delete: Remove the VM and its storage using virsh undefine --remove-all-storage
kvm.lifecycle_desc: Once created, KVM VMs support the full range of lifecycle operations:
kvm.lifecycle_stop: Stop: Gracefully shutdown the VM using virsh shutdown
kvm.net_dhcp: DHCP: VMs receive IP addresses automatically from the virbr0 bridge (typically 192.168.122.x)
kvm.net_dns: DNS: VMs use the host's DNS servers for name resolution
kvm.net_nat: NAT: Outbound connectivity is provided via NAT through the host
kvm.networking_bridged: For advanced networking, SysManage also supports bridged mode where VMs get IP addresses directly from your network's DHCP server.
kvm.networking_desc: KVM VMs connect to the libvirt default network which provides:
kvm.overview_desc: KVM (Kernel-based Virtual Machine) is a virtualization technology built into the Linux kernel. Combined with QEMU for hardware emulation and libvirt for management, it provides enterprise-grade virtual machine capabilities. SysManage integrates with KVM/libvirt to enable seamless VM creation and lifecycle management from the web interface.
kvm.req_cpu: CPU with hardware virtualization support (Intel VT-x or AMD-V)
kvm.req_kernel: Linux kernel with KVM modules (kvm, kvm_intel or kvm_amd)
kvm.req_network: libvirt default network or custom network bridge configured
kvm.req_user: The sysmanage-agent user must be in the libvirt group
kvm.setup_auto: Alternatively, click "Enable KVM" in the SysManage web interface to automate these setup steps.
kvm.setup_desc: To prepare a host for KVM virtual machine management:
kvm.setup_step2: Install KVM packages: sudo apt install qemu-kvm libvirt-daemon-system (Debian/Ubuntu) or sudo dnf install qemu-kvm libvirt (Fedora/RHEL)
kvm.setup_step3: Enable and start libvirtd: sudo systemctl enable --now libvirtd
kvm.setup_step4: Add agent user to libvirt group: sudo usermod -aG libvirt sysmanage-agent
kvm.setup_step5: Configure the default network: sudo virsh net-autostart default && sudo virsh net-start default
kvm.setup_step6: Restart the sysmanage-agent service to pick up group membership
kvm.title: KVM/QEMU Virtual Machine Management

lxd.comparison_desc: Both LXD and WSL provide containerized Linux environments, but they differ in several ways:
lxd.comparison_features: Features: LXD supports snapshots, profiles, and clustering; WSL focuses on developer tooling integration
lxd.comparison_images: Images: LXD pulls from the official image server; WSL uses Microsoft Store distributions
lxd.comparison_networking: Networking: LXD containers get routable IPs on lxdbr0; WSL instances share the Windows host network
lxd.comparison_platform: Platform: LXD runs on Linux hosts; WSL runs on Windows hosts
lxd.comparison_tech: Technology: LXD uses Linux containers (LXC); WSL uses a Microsoft compatibility layer
lxd.creation_desc: When creating an LXD container through SysManage:
lxd.creation_step1: SysManage launches the container from the official cloud image
lxd.creation_step2: Container receives an IP address via DHCP from lxdbr0
lxd.creation_step3: The specified user account is created with sudo privileges
lxd.creation_step4: Package repositories are configured based on the distribution
lxd.creation_step5: The sysmanage-agent is installed (via APT, DNF, or Zypper)
lxd.creation_step6: Agent configuration is generated with server connection details
lxd.creation_step7: The agent service starts and connects to SysManage
lxd.creation_step8: Container appears in the Hosts list pending approval
lxd.detect_2: Successful connection to the LXD API
lxd.detect_3: Permission to create and manage containers
lxd.detection_desc: SysManage automatically detects LXD capability by checking:
lxd.detection_role: When detected, the host is assigned the LXD Host role and LXD appears as an available virtualization type.
lxd.distributions_desc: LXD containers can be created using any distribution where the sysmanage-agent is available:
lxd.net_dhcp: DHCP: Containers receive IP addresses automatically from the lxdbr0 bridge
lxd.net_dns: DNS: Containers can resolve external hostnames through the host's DNS configuration
lxd.net_nat: NAT: Outbound connectivity is provided via NAT through the host
lxd.networking_desc: LXD containers use the default bridge network:
lxd.networking_note: For the container to communicate with the SysManage server, the host must have network access to the server, and the container must be able to route through the host.
lxd.overview_desc: LXD is a modern container and virtual machine manager for Linux. SysManage integrates with LXD to provide seamless creation and management of Linux containers directly from the web interface.
lxd.req_bridge: A network bridge must be configured (default: lxdbr0)
lxd.req_initialized: LXD must be initialized (lxd init completed)
lxd.req_installed: LXD must be installed on the host (snap or native package)
lxd.req_user: The sysmanage-agent user must be in the lxd group
lxd.setup_desc: To prepare a host for LXD container management:
lxd.setup_step1: Install LXD: sudo snap install lxd or via package manager
lxd.setup_step2: Initialize LXD: sudo lxd init (accept defaults or customize)
lxd.setup_step3: Add agent user to lxd group: sudo usermod -aG lxd sysmanage-agent
lxd.setup_step4: Restart the sysmanage-agent service to pick up group membership
lxd.title: LXD Container Management

manage.delete_desc: Permanently removes the child host and all its data. This action cannot be undone.
manage.delete_warning_desc: Deleting a child host permanently removes all data stored within it. For WSL, this runs 'wsl --unregister' which completely removes the distribution. Ensure you have backups of any important data before deletion.

proplus_notice.description: Starting, stopping, restarting, and deleting child hosts requires a Professional+ license with the container_engine module. Viewing child host status remains available in the Community Edition.
proplus_notice.title: Professional+ Required for Lifecycle Actions

manage.restart_desc: Stops and then starts the child host. Useful for applying configuration changes.
manage.start_desc: Starts a stopped child host. The child will boot and begin reporting to SysManage.
manage.status_error: Error: An error occurred - check details for more information
manage.status_pending: Pending Approval: Agent is waiting for administrator approval
manage.status_running: Running: Child host is active and responsive
manage.stop_desc: Gracefully shuts down a running child host. All processes within the child are terminated.
manage.title: Managing Child Hosts

child_host.meta_description: Comprehensive guide to managing child hosts including WSL instances, containers, and virtual machines in SysManage Professional+.

overview.concept_agent: Agent Auto-Installation: Child hosts automatically receive the sysmanage-agent during creation
overview.concept_child: Child Host: A virtualized environment (WSL, container, or VM) running on a parent host
overview.concept_distribution: Distribution: The operating system image used to create child hosts (e.g., Ubuntu, Debian, Fedora)
overview.concept_parent: Parent Host: The physical or virtual machine that hosts child environments
overview.concept_virtualization: Virtualization Type: The technology used (WSL, LXD, VirtualBox, Hyper-V, KVM, etc.)
overview.description: Child host management in SysManage enables you to create and manage virtualized environments directly from the web interface. This includes Windows Subsystem for Linux (WSL) instances, LXD/LXC containers, and various virtual machine technologies. Child hosts are automatically registered as managed hosts with the sysmanage-agent pre-installed.
overview.title: Overview
overview.type_wsl: WSL (Windows Subsystem for Linux): Run Linux distributions on Windows hosts

proplus_notice.description: Child host management is exclusive to SysManage Professional+ and requires the container_engine module to be loaded. The Community Edition does not include child-host APIs, listing, lifecycle controls, or virtualization-status views.
proplus_notice.title: Professional+ Required

rbac.role_configure: Configure Child Host: Manage distribution settings and configuration
rbac.role_create: Create Child Host: Create new WSL instances, containers, or VMs
rbac.role_delete: Delete Child Host: Delete child hosts and their data
rbac.role_start: Start Child Host: Start stopped child hosts
rbac.role_stop: Stop Child Host: Stop running child hosts
rbac.role_view: View Child Host: View child hosts and their status (read-only)
rbac.roles_desc: Child host operations require specific permissions from the Virtualization role group:
rbac.title: Permissions (RBAC)

tab.accessing_desc: The Child Hosts tab is available on each host's detail page for hosts that support virtualization:
tab.info_actions: Action Buttons: Create, start, stop, restart, and delete child hosts
tab.info_enable: Enable Button: Enable WSL or other virtualization if not yet enabled
tab.info_list: Child Host List: All child hosts with their status (running, stopped, creating, error)
tab.info_status: Virtualization Status: Shows enabled virtualization types and any pending configuration
tab.step1: Navigate to Hosts in the main menu
tab.step2: Select a host that supports virtualization
tab.title: Child Hosts Tab

child_host.title: Child Host Management - Professional+ - SysManage

troubleshooting.issue10_sol1: Verify the default network is active: virsh net-list
troubleshooting.issue10_sol2: Start the network if needed: virsh net-start default
troubleshooting.issue10_sol4: Verify virbr0 bridge exists: ip addr show virbr0
troubleshooting.issue11_sol2: Check libvirtd is running and the virsh command works
troubleshooting.issue11_sol3: Ensure sysmanage-agent user has libvirt group membership
troubleshooting.issue11_sol4: Restart sysmanage-agent after adding to libvirt group
troubleshooting.issue12_sol3: Verify DNS resolution works inside the VM
troubleshooting.issue12_sol4: Check if package installation failed due to repository access issues
troubleshooting.issue1_sol1: Verify the parent host has network connectivity
troubleshooting.issue1_sol2: Check that the distribution is enabled in Settings
troubleshooting.issue1_sol3: Ensure sufficient disk space on the parent host
troubleshooting.issue1_sol4: Review the error message in the child host status
troubleshooting.issue2_desc: This is normal behavior. Use SysManage's reboot function to restart the Windows host, then retry creating child hosts.
troubleshooting.issue3_sol1: Verify the child host is running (not stopped)
troubleshooting.issue3_sol2: Check that the sysmanage-agent service is running inside the child
troubleshooting.issue3_sol3: Verify network connectivity from the child to the SysManage server
troubleshooting.issue3_sol4: Review the agent logs for connection errors
troubleshooting.issue4_desc: Click on the child host to view the error details. Common causes include:
troubleshooting.issue5_desc: If LXD container creation fails, check the following:
troubleshooting.issue5_sol1: Verify LXD is initialized: lxc list should work without errors
troubleshooting.issue5_sol2: Check lxdbr0 network exists: lxc network list
troubleshooting.issue5_sol3: Ensure sysmanage-agent user is in the lxd group: groups sysmanage-agent
troubleshooting.issue6_desc: If the container starts but has no network connectivity:
troubleshooting.issue6_sol1: Verify lxdbr0 is running: lxc network show lxdbr0
troubleshooting.issue6_sol2: Check container network config: lxc config show CONTAINER
troubleshooting.issue6_sol3: Verify DHCP is working: lxc exec CONTAINER -- ip addr
troubleshooting.issue6_sol4: Try restarting the network: lxc restart CONTAINER
troubleshooting.issue7_desc: If a host with LXD installed doesn't show the LXD Host role:
troubleshooting.issue7_sol1: Verify LXD socket exists and is accessible
troubleshooting.issue7_sol2: Check sysmanage-agent user has lxd group membership
troubleshooting.issue7_sol3: Restart sysmanage-agent after adding to lxd group
troubleshooting.issue7_sol4: Wait for the next role detection cycle or restart the agent
troubleshooting.issue8_desc: This is expected behavior. LXD containers run unprivileged by default for security. The agent will report its capabilities accordingly. The container can still be fully managed, but some privileged operations (like installing certain packages) may require configuration changes.
troubleshooting.issue8_title: LXD Container Agent Shows Not Privileged
troubleshooting.issue9_sol1: Verify KVM is enabled: virsh list --all should work without errors
troubleshooting.issue9_sol2: Check libvirt is running: sudo systemctl status libvirtd
troubleshooting.issue9_sol3: Ensure sysmanage-agent user is in the libvirt group: groups sysmanage-agent
troubleshooting.issue9_sol5: Check the cloud image URL is accessible from the host
troubleshooting.title: Troubleshooting
troubleshooting.issue8_sol1: Verify the sudoers file exists in /etc/sudoers.d/sysmanage-agent inside the container
troubleshooting.issue8_sol2: Check the sudoers file has correct permissions (440 or 400)
troubleshooting.issue8_sol3: Test sudo access: lxc exec <container> -- sudo -u sysmanage-agent sudo -n whoami
troubleshooting.issue8_sol4: Restart the agent to refresh privilege detection

wsl.distributions_desc: SysManage ships with pre-configured support for distributions where sysmanage-agent packages are available:
wsl.enable_desc: If WSL is not enabled on a Windows host, SysManage can enable it automatically:
wsl.enable_step1: Click "Enable WSL" on the Child Hosts tab
wsl.enable_step2: The agent will run the necessary Windows commands
wsl.enable_step3: A reboot may be required - the host will show "Reboot Required"
wsl.enable_step4: After reboot, WSL will be ready for use
wsl.req_version: WSL version 2 (WSL v1 is not supported)
wsl.req_windows: Windows 10 version 2004 or later, or Windows 11
wsl.title: WSL-Specific Information

account.first_login: OIDC users must have a sysmanage row provisioned before their first sign-in (the callback rejects unknown subjects with 403); LDAP users authenticate via the password form and follow the existing user-creation flow.
account.intro: Existing sysmanage user rows pick up two new columns that link them to a directory:
account.provider: external_idp_provider_id — FK to the provider row. NULL for local-only accounts (the default).
account.subject: external_subject — IdP-side stable identifier. For LDAP this is the user's full DN; for OIDC it's the sub claim. Used to re-link if the userid changes.
account.title: Linking sysmanage Accounts to External Identities

api.intro: Every route below returns 402 without external_idp_engine loaded.
api.oidc_callback: anonymous; exchanges code, returns session JWT
api.title: API Reference

fallback.intro: When the singleton external_idp_settings row has local_account_fallback=true and the engine declines (LDAP server unreachable, password rejected, etc.), the login flow falls through to local Argon2 verification. This is the break-glass admin path — the local password row stays usable even after a user is linked to an external IdP. Setting it to false hard-locks accounts to their external directory.
fallback.title: Local-Account Fallback

header.subtitle: Sign in to SysManage using your existing LDAP/Active Directory or OIDC identity provider. External group membership maps to sysmanage roles automatically; a break-glass local-password fallback keeps you in the door if the directory is unreachable.
header.title: External Identity Providers

ldap.bind: ldap_bind_dn + ldap_bind_password_secret_id — service account used for the user-search bind. The plaintext password lives only in Vault; the server unseals it before passing to the engine.
ldap.flow: Authentication flow: the engine binds as the service account, searches for the user, then binds as the user with the supplied password to verify it. RFC 4515 filter-injection escapes are applied to every interpolated value.
ldap.group_search: ldap_group_search_base + ldap_group_search_filter — optional. When set, the engine queries the user's group memberships after a successful bind; matched cn values are passed to the role-mapping step.
ldap.server: ldap_server_url — typically ldaps://ad.example.com:636. Plain ldap:// is supported but logged as a warning.
ldap.title: LDAP / Active Directory
ldap.tls: ldap_tls_ca_bundle_path + ldap_connection_timeout — TLS verification bundle and connection timeout (default 10 s).
ldap.user_search: ldap_user_search_base + ldap_user_search_filter — where to look up the user. The filter must contain %s for the username, e.g. (sAMAccountName=%s) for AD or (uid=%s) for OpenLDAP.

idp.meta_description: Pro+ external authentication via LDAP/Active Directory bind+search and OIDC authorization-code flow, with external-group→sysmanage-role mapping and break-glass local-account fallback.

oidc.client: oidc_client_id + oidc_client_secret_secret_id — the application registered with the IdP. The client secret is Vault-stored.
oidc.discovery: oidc_discovery_url — optional override for the well-known discovery doc. Defaults to {issuer}/.well-known/openid-configuration.
oidc.flow: The login UI redirects the browser to /api/auth/oidc/{provider_id}/start, which 302s to the IdP's authorization endpoint with a CSRF-protecting state token. After the user signs in, the IdP returns to /api/auth/oidc/{provider_id}/callback?code=…&state=… and the engine exchanges the code for an ID token. The sub claim is matched against the user's external_subject column to locate the local account.
oidc.group_claim: oidc_group_claim — name of the ID-token claim that lists the user's groups (default groups; some IdPs use roles or a custom name).
oidc.redirect: oidc_redirect_uri — the URL the IdP bounces the browser to after the user signs in. Typically https://your-sysmanage.example.com/api/auth/oidc/{provider_id}/callback.
oidc.scopes: oidc_scopes — space-separated, default openid profile email. Add groups (or your IdP's group claim scope) when you want role mapping.
oidc.title: OIDC

overview.intro: The external_idp_engine Pro+ module performs the actual authentication — an LDAP bind+search for AD/LDAP providers, or an OIDC authorization-code exchange. The OSS server holds the configuration tables, the Settings UI, and the routes that 402 without the engine loaded; the engine is stateless and never touches the database directly.
overview.title: Overview

role_mapping.dedup: The result is deduped before being applied to the user's role set. Roles already granted locally aren't touched — only the names that overlap with the mapping output get reconciled.
role_mapping.default: If no entries match AND a mapping has default_for_unmapped=true, that entry's role is granted as a catch-all (e.g. read-only viewer for anyone who authenticates successfully but isn't in a privileged group).
role_mapping.intro: Each provider has a per-row mapping table that translates external group identifiers into sysmanage SecurityRole names. Rules:
role_mapping.match: If any of the user's external groups matches an entry's external_group, the entry contributes its role_name. Multiple matches are unioned.
role_mapping.title: External Group → sysmanage Role Mapping

idp.title: External Identity Providers - Professional+ - SysManage

airgap.intro: The mirroring engine produces the public-side ("collector") tree that feeds optical media or removable storage into an air-gapped network. The private-side ("repository") host imports the media into its own mirror tree using the same engine — the import is just an offline rsync of the snapshot directory. From there, hosts on the private network treat the repository host as their normal upstream.
airgap.title: Air-Gapped Deployments

api.intro: Every route below returns 402 without repository_mirroring_engine loaded.
api.snapshot: take a snapshot now (stamps last_snapshot_message_id for in-flight tracking)
api.title: API Reference
api.platform_delete: delete a platform config (refuses while it owns mirrors)
api.setup_status: read tooling setup status for a host
api.setup_install: queue an install of the tooling for a package manager

config.bandwidth: bandwidth_cap_kbps — when non-zero, every fetch is wrapped with trickle -d <cap> if available; falls through uncapped otherwise. Most ops teams cap at the network layer (tc); this is a per-job courtesy.
config.cron: sync_cron — POSIX cron (5 fields). Defaults to 0 4 * * * (4 a.m. local). Evaluated by the same parser as upgrade profiles in automation_engine.
config.host: host_id — the agent that runs the sync plan. The on-disk tree lives at {mirror_root_path}/{name} on that host.
config.intro: Each mirror is an admin-managed row in the mirror_repository table. Common fields:
config.name: name — short slug, used as the on-disk subdirectory name. Path-traversal characters are rejected.
config.pm_specific: Per-package-manager fields: APT uses suite + components + optional signing_key_url; DNF uses repoid + optional gpgkey_url; zypper uses repo_alias; pkg uses optional release (e.g. FreeBSD:14:amd64).
config.tier: network_tier — free-form tag (e.g. us-east, edge) that lets clients pick the closest mirror.
config.title: Per-Mirror Configuration
config.upstream: upstream_url — the public mirror you sync from.

header.subtitle: Mirror upstream APT, DNF, zypper, and FreeBSD pkg repositories onto a host inside your fleet. Reduce WAN cost, freeze point-in-time snapshots for reproducible patch rollouts, and feed air-gapped networks via optical media.
header.title: Repository Mirroring

mirror.meta_description: Pro+ APT/DNF/zypper/pkg repository mirroring with cron-driven sync, point-in-time snapshots, integrity checks, and air-gapped deployment support.

overview.intro: The repository_mirroring_engine Pro+ module emits apply_deployment_plan messages that the agent on the mirror host runs. The OSS server owns the configuration tables, the Settings UI, and the routes that 402 without the engine loaded; the engine owns plan generation. No agent code needs to change to support a new package manager — every plan goes through the existing apply_deployment_plan handler.
overview.title: Overview

settings.bandwidth: default_bandwidth_cap_kbps — applied when a per-mirror cap is not set (default 0 = uncapped).
settings.cadence: integrity_check_cadence_hours — how often the integrity-check plan runs against each mirror (default 24).
settings.intro: The singleton mirror_settings row carries fleet-wide defaults:
settings.keep: snapshot_count_to_keep — number of snapshots the GC plan preserves (default 10).
settings.retention: retention_window_days — days a snapshot stays on disk before being eligible for GC (default 30).
settings.root: mirror_root_path — filesystem root that every mirror's subdirectory hangs off (default /var/mirror).
settings.title: Admin Settings

snapshots.gc: Snapshot retention is governed by snapshot_count_to_keep. The garbage-collection plan lists snapshots in alphabetical (= chronological) order, drops everything older than the keep count, and rm -rfs the rest after relaxing the read-only flag.
snapshots.intro: A snapshot is an rsync -aH --delete --stats of the live tree into a sibling read-only directory at {mirror_root}/{name}/.snapshots/{snapshot_id}. Snapshot IDs are sortable timestamps (YYYYMMDDTHHMMSS). After the rsync the engine runs chmod -R a-w so the snapshot can't be modified in place. The --stats output is parsed server-side to populate size_bytes and file_count on the mirror_snapshot row — no second pass is needed to learn a snapshot's weight.
snapshots.restore: Restore is the reverse — an rsync from the chosen snapshot back into the live tree. The engine doesn't symlink-flip, because most mirror servers (httpd, nginx) cache-on-stat and the in-place rsync is simpler to reason about than coordinating a flip across worker processes.
snapshots.title: Snapshots & Restore
snapshots.sudoers: Sudoers requirement. Both snapshot and restore plans invoke sudo rsync + sudo chmod. The sysmanage-agent's packaged sudoers fragment (/etc/sudoers.d/sysmanage-agent on Linux, /etc/doas.d/sysmanage-agent on OpenBSD) explicitly allows these — a missing rsync entry would surface as a fast-failing plan with sudo's "user not allowed to run" message in the agent's command_result. If you bake your own sudoers locally, mirror the allowlist from the installer's packaged file.

supported.apt: APT (Debian / Ubuntu) — uses apt-mirror with optional GPG signing-key fetch.
supported.dnf: DNF / YUM (RHEL / Oracle Linux / Fedora / Rocky / Alma) — uses dnf reposync --newest-only --delete + createrepo_c --update.
supported.title: Supported Package Managers
supported.zypper: zypper (openSUSE / SLES) — uses a portable zypper download '*' wrapper + createrepo_c.

mirror.title: Repository Mirroring - Professional+ - SysManage

platforms.title: Per-platform tab strip (Phase 10.4.3)
platforms.intro: The Settings → Repository Mirroring page is laid out as a tab strip with one tab per package manager. Today the four tabs are Ubuntu/Debian (apt), RHEL/Fedora (dnf — covers RHEL, Fedora, Oracle Linux, Rocky, Alma, CentOS Stream), openSUSE/SLES (zypper), and FreeBSD (pkg). Each tab represents one mirror host running one PM, so the Add Mirror dialog inherits its package manager from the active tab and the host picker filters to OS-family matches (Linux hosts on apt/dnf/zypper tabs, FreeBSD hosts on the pkg tab).
platforms.layout: Each tab contains three cards stacked vertically:
platforms.card1: Platform Settings — searchable host picker, mirror root path on the host, retention window, integrity-check cadence, default bandwidth cap, and snapshot keep count. These values are scoped to this platform on this host, so a Linux mirror on one host and a FreeBSD mirror on another can have completely independent storage layouts.
platforms.card2: Mirror Setup Status — chips for each tool the engine's plan-builders shell out to (apt-mirror, reposync, createrepo_c, trickle, rsync, curl, etc.). Refresh button queues a probe; Install Tools button queues a sudo install of the platform-appropriate package set. Both go through the agent's standard apply_deployment_plan path; the card polls the cached status row until the agent's command_result lands.
platforms.card3: Mirror Repositories — the table of upstream URLs being mirrored on this platform, plus inline sync/snapshot/edit/delete actions and an Add Mirror dialog. The package manager dropdown in the dialog is filtered to those the active platform supports (apt/dnf/zypper for Linux, pkg for FreeBSD).
platforms.empty: If the active tab has no platform configuration yet, an empty-state card prompts you to pick a host. The host picker only lists hosts whose detected platform matches the tab — Linux hosts on the Linux tab, FreeBSD hosts on the FreeBSD tab — to prevent misconfigured pairings.
platforms.snaps: Snaps: not supported. Mirroring snap packages requires Canonical's commercial Snap Store Proxy product (separate auth + HTTP API, not file-level), which is out of scope for this engine. OpenBSD, NetBSD, macOS, and Windows are deferred until their own tooling probes and install plans are written.

versions.title: Version dropdown + known-version catalog (Phase 10.4.4)
versions.intro: The Add Mirror dialog's version field is sourced from a pre-populated mirror_known_version catalog instead of free-text. Selecting a row auto-fills the upstream URL and the per-PM identifier (suite for apt, repoid for dnf, repo_alias for zypper, release for pkg) so an operator can't fat-finger noblee and silently produce a broken mirror. The catalog ships seeded with the canonical Ubuntu/Debian/RHEL-family/openSUSE/FreeBSD versions; future versions land via dedicated migrations rather than auto-discovery, keeping the supported set reviewable in code.
versions.crossfamily: One physical host can mirror multiple OS families that share a package manager. RHEL/Rocky/Alma/Fedora all use dnf with different upstream URLs, so they appear as separate mirror_repository rows under the same RHEL/Fedora tab — each row carries its own known_version_id referencing a different catalog entry. The Default Package Mirrors card has independent rows for each, so an admin can assign different defaults to RHEL 9 hosts vs Rocky 9 hosts even when both pull from the same physical mirror server.

defaults.title: Default Package Mirrors (Phase 10.4.4)
defaults.intro: Settings → Host Defaults gains a "Default Package Mirrors" card that drives an apply/revert workflow against active hosts. One row per (platform, version_key, os_family) tuple drawn from the catalog; each row's dropdown lists the eligible mirrors (right PM + at least one successful sync) plus a "Cloud (upstream default)" option.
defaults.flow: Saving a non-cloud choice queues an apply plan to every active host whose platform_release matches the catalog row's regex — simultaneous rollout, no staggered windows. Choosing "Cloud" queues a revert plan to the same matching hosts. New host registrations and approvals invoke the same hook automatically, so a freshly-enrolled host of a covered family gets pointed at the mirror without operator action.
defaults.hardblock: The API hard-blocks (HTTP 409) any attempt to assign a mirror that hasn't completed a successful sync. This prevents pointing live clients at an empty or partially-built mirror tree where apt update would fail. Re-attempt once the mirror's last_sync_status reaches SUCCESS.
defaults.applymech: The apply mechanism is additive only. Each PM gets a single override file the agent drops into a directory the package manager scans alongside operator-edited config:
defaults.dnf: dnf: /etc/yum.repos.d/zzz-sysmanage-<repoid>.repo with the same [section] header as the upstream — dnf reads files in lex order and the last definition wins, so our zzz- prefix overrides whatever shipped + dnf clean all
defaults.revert: Revert is symmetric: each plan deletes the override file and refreshes the package manager's metadata cache. Operator-edited config (the original sources.list, vendor-shipped .repo files) is never modified, so a manual edit between apply and revert is preserved.
defaults.api: API surface: GET /api/mirror-known-versions for the catalog dropdown, GET /api/host-defaults/mirrors for the assignment table, PUT /api/host-defaults/mirrors/{platform}/{version_key}/{os_family} with {mirror_id} body to assign or {mirror_id: null} to revert to cloud.

ux.title: Mirror status UX
ux.intro: The Settings → Repository Mirroring page renders one status chip per action (sync / snapshot / restore) on each mirror row, rather than collapsing every plan outcome onto a single "Status" column. Each chip carries its own state:
ux.chip_idle: Idle (muted) — that action has never run on this mirror.
ux.chip_inflight: In flight (spinner + elapsed) — the dispatch endpoint stamped a last_<action>_message_id at dispatch; the result handler clears it. The chip shows a tiny spinner and "Nm ago" so operators can see at a glance that a long sync hasn't stalled.
ux.chip_success: Success — chip is green; hover reveals the timestamp of the last successful run for that action.
ux.chip_failed: Failed — chip is red and shows a help-cursor underline; hover reveals the full stderr / error text in monospace. The error attribution walks the agent's per-command results to pick the actually-failed command's stderr, not the first command's (which is typically empty when a later step in the plan failed).
ux.expand_row: Each mirror row has an expand chevron that reveals a sub-table of that mirror's snapshots — snapshot id, taken-at timestamp, size (human-readable from --stats), file count, and a Restore icon-button per row. The sub-table polls every 10s while open so a freshly-taken snapshot shows up automatically. Restore prompts for confirmation, then dispatches the restore plan and surfaces progress through the same in-flight chip pattern.
ux.action_independence: Per-action columns mean a successful sync followed by a failed snapshot no longer overwrites the sync's chip with FAILED — the sync stays green, the snapshot chip turns red. Restoring (or rerunning sync) clears each action's state independently.

airgap.title: Air-Gap Deployment - Professional+ - SysManage
airgap.meta_description: Deploy SysManage server and agent into networks with no internet connectivity using the Pro+ Air-Gap Install Bundle Builder.

header.title: Air-Gap Deployment
header.subtitle: Build a single ISO image containing the SysManage server (or agent) plus every transitive operating-system package and Python wheel it needs, then carry that ISO into a network that has no path to the public internet and install with one command.

overview.title: Overview
overview.intro: Many regulated environments — defense networks, classified labs, operational-technology segments, isolated tenancy zones — cannot pull packages from the public internet at install time. The Pro+ Air-Gap Install Bundle Builder solves this by producing a self-contained multi-OS ISO from an internet-connected SysManage host. You burn or copy the ISO onto removable media, walk it into the air-gapped network, mount it on the target machine, and run one installer script. The installer detects the host operating system and consumes only the bundled packages — it never reaches out to a remote mirror.
overview.deployment_model: The workflow always involves two distinct machines: a build host that has internet access and a SysManage server running with a Pro+ license, and one or more target hosts sitting inside the air-gapped enclave. Pro+ is required only on the build host; the resulting ISO installs unmodified SysManage server or agent packages, both of which run under any tier (Community, Pro, or Pro+) on the target.

platforms.title: Supported Target Platforms
platforms.intro: A single bundle contains installers for every platform listed below. The dispatcher script at the root of the ISO routes to the right subdirectory automatically based on the target's /etc/os-release (or uname for BSD/macOS).
platforms.note: The build also collects every transitive native-package dependency (e.g. nginx, docker.io, postgresql-client, libpq-dev on Ubuntu) and every transitive Python wheel from the package's requirements.txt, so the air-gapped target needs no upstream apt/dnf/zypper/PyPI access whatsoever.

prereqs.title: Prerequisites
prereqs.build_pro_plus: SysManage server running with a valid Pro+ license. The bundle builder UI tab and API endpoints both return 403 on Community and Pro tiers.
prereqs.build_docker: Docker must be installed and the SysManage service user (typically sysmanage, or your dev user when running make start) must be a member of the docker group. Each per-platform builder spawns a throwaway distro container.
prereqs.build_tools: Build host packages: xorriso (ISO mastering), jq (GitHub Releases parsing), curl. On Debian/Ubuntu: sudo apt install -y docker.io xorriso jq curl.
prereqs.build_disk: At least 4 GB free under /var/lib/sysmanage/airgap-bundles/ (per bundle). The server bundle currently lands around 1 GB; the agent bundle around 500 MB.
prereqs.build_network: Outbound access to ppa.launchpadcontent.net, api.github.com, github.com, deb.debian.org, dl.fedoraproject.org, your distro mirror of choice, and PyPI (pypi.org, files.pythonhosted.org). The build pulls upstream packages and wheels through the Docker container's own apt/dnf cache.
prereqs.target_os: A supported OS from the platforms list above, fresh enough that its kernel and libc match the bundle. The bundle ships amd64/x86_64 binaries for Linux/BSD and the corresponding native arch for macOS/Windows.
prereqs.target_sudo: sudo available to the operator running the install. The installer needs root to call dpkg -i / dnf install / pkg add / installer -pkg.
prereqs.target_no_internet: No internet required. The installer sets PIP_NO_INDEX=1 and PIP_FIND_LINKS so the package's post-install scripts use only the bundled wheels.

build.title: Step 1 — Build the Bundle on the Internet-Connected Host
build.intro: The bundle is built from the SysManage Settings UI on a Pro+-licensed host that has internet access. You do not need to install the air-gap target's OS on the build host — every per-platform fetch runs inside its own Docker container.
build.step_login: Log into the SysManage web UI on the build host with an admin account.
build.step_nav: Navigate to Settings → Air-Gap Install Bundles. This tab is only visible when the server has an active Pro+ license.
build.step_docker: Confirm the Docker readiness banner reads "Docker is ready". If it doesn't, the banner gives the exact remediation command — usually sudo usermod -aG docker <service-user> followed by a service restart.
build.step_click: Click Build Server Bundle for the SysManage server, or Build Agent Bundle for the SysManage agent. You can queue both — they will run sequentially.
build.step_wait: The new bundle row appears with status queued, then building. Builds typically take 15–30 minutes depending on how many platforms are enabled and the speed of the build host's network. The UI auto-polls — no manual refresh needed.
build.step_ready: When the status flips to ready, the row's Actions column shows a download icon. The recorded Version column reflects the upstream SysManage release that was baked into this bundle.
build.step_download: Click the download icon. Your browser downloads sysmanage-server-bundle-<version>.iso (or sysmanage-agent-bundle-<version>.iso) through the authenticated API. The file is large (~500 MB to ~1 GB) — keep the tab open until the download completes.
build.failure_note: If a build fails, the row's Status chip shows failed and the "Recent build failures" alert at the top of the tab summarizes the first error line. The complete per-build log lives on the server at /var/lib/sysmanage/airgap-bundles/<bundle-uuid>.log — useful for diagnosing PPA build issues, missing GitHub Releases assets, or Docker registry hiccups.

transfer.title: Step 2 — Carry the ISO into the Air-Gapped Network
transfer.intro: The ISO is a standard ISO-9660 image with Joliet and Rock Ridge extensions, so any tool that writes ISOs to media will work. Pick whichever method your site's security policy permits:
transfer.optical: Optical media — burn to DVD or Blu-ray with xorrecord, brasero, or the OS's native burning tool. Best when your enclave's data-diode/airlock policy treats optical media as one-way write.
transfer.usb: USB stick — copy the .iso file directly onto a USB device formatted with a filesystem the target can read (FAT32 / exFAT / ext4). No need to "burn" — the installer mounts the file with mount -o loop.
transfer.diode: Cross-domain solution — push the file through your site's approved data diode or unidirectional gateway. The ISO is a single self-contained binary, so it transfers cleanly without referencing external resources.
transfer.note: For repeatable air-gap installs (lab fleets, dev environments inside the same enclave), store the ISO on a small file share inside the air-gapped network and pull it from there on each install — no need to walk a USB stick around every time.

install.title: Step 3 — Install on the Air-Gapped Target
install.intro: Once the ISO is on the target host (mounted from optical media or copied as a file), the install is a single command. The dispatcher script at the root of the ISO detects the host OS and hands off to the correct platform installer.
install.detection: The dispatcher prints a single [install] Detected: id=<os> version=<ver> line and then runs the matching platform installer (for example linux-ubuntu-resolute/install.sh on Ubuntu 26.04). The platform installer in turn invokes dpkg -i, dnf install, zypper install, apk add, pkg add, pkg_add, or installer -pkg depending on the target — always against the bundled artifacts only, never reaching out to the network.
install.windows_text: Windows installs are GUI-only. Mount the ISO (right-click → Mount on Windows 10/11), open the windows\ folder, and double-click sysmanage-x64.msi or sysmanage-arm64.msi — whichever matches the target's processor architecture.
install.config_text: Once the server install finishes, configure /etc/sysmanage.yaml using the online SysManage configuration builder. Two settings matter specifically for air-gap installs: set api.host: 0.0.0.0 (so the API is reachable from the web UI on another machine — binding to localhost only works for a single-host install) and set role: repository at the top level if this server is the air-gapped half of a collector/repository pair. Then run the database migrations and start the service: cd /opt/sysmanage && sudo -u sysmanage .venv/bin/python -m alembic upgrade head && sudo systemctl enable --now sysmanage.

contents.title: What's Inside the Bundle
contents.intro: The ISO is laid out by platform, with a per-platform install.sh in each subdirectory. The root install.sh dispatches based on the detected OS. A typical server bundle looks like this:

troubleshooting.title: Troubleshooting
troubleshooting.docker_title: "Docker isn't ready" banner on the build host
troubleshooting.docker_text: Three distinct failure modes: Docker not installed, daemon not running, or the SysManage service user not in the docker group. The banner names which one applies and shows the exact remediation. After running the command, click Re-check Docker. If you added a group, you must also restart the SysManage service so the running process picks up the new group set — bash does not re-read /etc/group for a running process.
troubleshooting.zero_deps_text: The bundle was built with an old script version that failed to walk transitive dependencies. Re-build the bundle on the build host after pulling the latest SysManage server release; the per-platform build log should report downloaded N apt deps; M were unavailable/virtual with N in the hundreds.
troubleshooting.unsupported_os_title: "Unsupported or unrecognised OS" from the dispatcher
troubleshooting.unsupported_os_text: The target's /etc/os-release ID/VERSION_ID don't match any of the per-platform subdirectories on the ISO. The dispatcher lists the subdirectories present in the bundle so you can confirm. Either the bundle was built with that platform disabled, or the per-platform builder for that distro failed during the build (check the build log on the build host).
troubleshooting.ppa_pending_title: Build fails on Ubuntu with "Unable to locate package sysmanage"
troubleshooting.ppa_pending_text: The Launchpad PPA accepted the source upload but the binary build is still pending or failed. The bundle script transparently falls back to fetching the generic .deb from GitHub Releases when the PPA download fails, so this is usually transient — re-run the build once the PPA shows the codename as Published.

versioning.title: Versioning and Re-Builds
versioning.intro: Each bundle records the upstream SysManage release tag (e.g. 2.4.0.3) at build time, visible in the Version column of the bundle list. The recorded version comes from api.github.com/repos/bceverly/sysmanage/releases/latest (or sysmanage-agent) at the moment the build started.
versioning.policy: To upgrade an air-gapped fleet, build a fresh bundle from the build host (after that host has pulled the new release), carry the new ISO into the enclave, and re-run the installer on each target. The installers use dpkg -i / dnf install / apk add with the same package name, which upgrades in place. Configuration and database state are preserved across upgrades.
versioning.retention: Old bundles stay listed in the UI until you delete them — they consume disk on the build host but cost nothing else. Use the trash icon in the bundle row to remove both the row and the on-disk ISO.

security.title: Security Notes
security.signed: Bundled packages are the same signed artifacts shipped to public package archives — .deb files from the bceverly Launchpad PPA carry the project GPG signature, .rpm files are signed via the COPR/OBS pipeline, .msi files are SignPath-signed, and .pkg files for macOS are notarized.
security.unsigned_iso: The ISO itself is not signed. Treat it like any other staged build artifact — verify integrity (SHA-256) between the build host and the air-gapped network through your established change-control process before installing.
security.no_telemetry: The bundled installer makes no outbound network connections. Once installed, the SysManage server or agent connects only to the endpoints configured in /etc/sysmanage.yaml (or the agent's config file) — typically a SysManage server inside the same enclave.

related.title: Related Documentation
related.mirror: Repository Mirroring — once SysManage is installed inside the enclave via this bundle, the Pro+ mirroring engine can serve subsequent OS-package updates from a mirror host on the private network.
related.child_host: Child Host Management — agent bundles can be re-used to populate child hosts (LXD/KVM/bhyve guests) created by the parent inside the air-gapped enclave.
related.server_install: Server Installation — the standard internet-connected install procedure, for reference on what the air-gap bundle automates.

bundles.title: The Three Bundle Types
bundles.intro: The Air-Gap Install Bundles tab in Settings can produce three distinct bundle ISOs. Choose the ones that match what you intend to run inside the enclave; all three are produced from the same UI tab on the same Pro+-licensed build host.
bundles.server: Server bundle (~1 GB) — the SysManage server .deb/.rpm/.apk/.pkg/.msi for every supported platform, plus its full transitive native-package dependency closure (PostgreSQL, nginx, Python build deps) and every Python wheel from requirements.txt. Install this first on the air-gapped server host.
bundles.agent: Agent bundle (~500 MB) — the SysManage agent package for every supported platform, with its transitive native-package and wheel closures. Install this on every host inside the enclave that should be managed.
bundles.proplus: Pro+ overlay bundle (~50 MB) — the build host's Cython engine .so files, JavaScript plugin shims, license JWT, and cached license public key. Apply this on the air-gapped server host after the server bundle has been installed; it unlocks Pro+ engines locally without any phone-home to license.sysmanage.org. Not needed if you only need Community-tier features on the air-gapped server.
bundles.order: If you want Pro+ functionality on the air-gapped server, the install order is: (1) server bundle on the server host, (2) Pro+ overlay on the same server host, (3) agent bundle on each managed host. The Pro+ overlay's installer expects the SysManage server to already be in place — it copies engine modules into /var/lib/sysmanage/modules/, drops the license public key into /var/lib/sysmanage/license/, writes the license JWT into /etc/sysmanage.yaml, and restarts the service.

proplus_install.title: Step 4 — Apply the Pro+ Overlay (optional)
proplus_install.intro: Skip this step if the air-gapped server only needs Community-tier functionality. To enable Pro+ features (repository mirroring, child-host management, federation, etc.) on the air-gapped server, install the Pro+ overlay bundle on top of the already-installed server. The Pro+ overlay carries the licensed Cython engine modules, the license JWT, and the cached license public key — no network access to license.sysmanage.org is needed at any point.
proplus_install.command: From the air-gapped server, mount the Pro+ overlay ISO and run its installer:
proplus_install.what_it_does: The overlay's install.sh is idempotent — safe to re-run when you ship a refreshed overlay later. It performs five steps:
proplus_install.step1: Verifies the SysManage server is installed (looks for /opt/sysmanage/.venv/bin/python and /etc/sysmanage.yaml).
proplus_install.step2: Copies the bundled Cython engine modules and JavaScript plugin shims to /var/lib/sysmanage/modules/ (owned by sysmanage:sysmanage, mode 0644).
proplus_install.step3: Drops the cached license public key into /var/lib/sysmanage/license/public_key.pem. Without this, the license validator would try to phone-home and fail.
proplus_install.step4: Edits /etc/sysmanage.yaml via the SysManage venv's PyYAML: writes license.key (the JWT carried in the bundle), and disables geo_lookup.enabled + geo_lookup.ipapi_fallback_enabled so no DNS queries leave the enclave for IP-geolocation lookups.
proplus_install.step5: Populates the proplus_module_cache table in the SysManage database with a row per engine .so file (module code, platform, architecture, Python version, file path, SHA-512 hash). The module loader queries this table on startup to decide which engines to load — without these rows it would try to download them from license.sysmanage.org and fail on air-gap.
proplus_install.verify: The installer then runs systemctl restart sysmanage and prints commands to verify the activation. Confirm with curl -ks http://localhost:3000/api/v1/server-info | python3 -m json.tool — you should see license_tier: enterprise (or whatever tier the JWT carries) and role_engine_loaded: true. The navbar role chip in the web UI turns green after a hard refresh.
proplus_install.grace_note: About the offline grace period: the Pro+ overlay does not blank the license.phone_home_url setting. The license JWT includes an offline_days field (typically 30); the validator runs in offline-grace mode for that many days after install. Plan to rebuild the Pro+ overlay on the internet-connected build host and re-apply it on the air-gapped server before the grace window expires — otherwise Pro+ silently demotes to Community tier on the next service start. This is the intended enforcement mechanism that prevents the overlay ISO from being shared indefinitely.

backup.005: Comprehensive data protection strategies including backup procedures, disaster recovery planning, and data restoration for business continuity.
backup.007: Data protection is critical for maintaining business continuity and protecting against data loss. SysManage backup and recovery procedures ensure that your monitoring infrastructure and historical data remain protected and can be quickly restored in case of system failures, disasters, or data corruption.

backup.010: Configuration Backups: System configuration files and settings
backup.011: Certificate Backups: SSL/TLS certificates and private keys
backup.012: Log Backups: Critical log files and audit trails
backup.013: Code and Scripts: Custom scripts and automation code
backup.017: SysManage uses PostgreSQL as its primary database. Regular backups are essential for data protection.
backup.044: Critical Data: 15 minutes maximum data loss
backup.045: Standard Data: 4 hours maximum data loss
backup.046: Archive Data: 24 hours maximum data loss
backup.048: Hardware Failure: Server or storage system failure
backup.049: Software Corruption: Application or database corruption
backup.050: Natural Disasters: Fire, flood, earthquake, power outage
backup.051: Cyber Attacks: Ransomware, data breaches, DDoS attacks
backup.052: Human Error: Accidental deletion or misconfiguration

host-management.209: The Hosts page provides several controls above and below the host list for filtering, searching, and acting on the displayed hosts. The following controls are available:
host-management.210: Search box and column selector: Enter a search term to filter the list. The accompanying column selector chooses which field the term is matched against: FQDN, Platform, IPv4, or IPv6. Matching is case-insensitive and matches any host whose chosen field contains the term.
host-management.211: Filter by Tags: A multi-select control that limits the list to hosts carrying the selected tags. Selecting multiple tags uses AND logic, so a host must have all selected tags to appear.
host-management.212: Child host filter (All / Parents / Children): A toggle that limits the list to all hosts, parent (virtualization) hosts only, or child hosts only. This is a Professional+ feature and is hidden on community builds without the container engine module.
host-management.213: Column Visibility: Opens a menu to show or hide individual columns in the host list, with an option to reset back to the defaults. Your selections are remembered for this grid.
host-management.214: Refresh All Data: Requests a full inventory data refresh from each selected host. Enabled only when one or more hosts are selected.
host-management.215: Broadcast Refresh: Sends a refresh-inventory broadcast to every connected agent at once. A confirmation prompt is shown first, and the result reports how many hosts were reached.
host-management.216: Get Diagnostics: Requests diagnostic data collection from a single host. Enabled only when exactly one host is selected; results appear on that host's detail page when available.
host-management.217: The Hosts page lists every registered host with its operating system, status, tags, and pending updates.
host-management.218: Tag management on the Settings page — create, edit, and delete tags.

maintenance.005: Regular maintenance procedures, system updates, performance optimization, and capacity planning to ensure optimal SysManage performance and reliability.
maintenance.007: Regular maintenance is essential for keeping SysManage running optimally and preventing issues before they impact operations. This includes routine system updates, performance monitoring, database maintenance, log management, and capacity planning to ensure the system scales effectively with your infrastructure growth.

monitoring.115: Use a Grafana server managed by SysManage:
monitoring.116: Install Grafana on a managed host via SysManage
monitoring.117: Assign the "Monitoring Server" role with package "grafana"
monitoring.119: Enable integration and select your Grafana server
monitoring.120: Provide a Grafana API key (stored securely in OpenBAO vault)
monitoring.122: Use an existing external Grafana installation:
monitoring.127: Provide a Grafana API key (stored securely in OpenBAO vault)
monitoring.130: Important: The API key must have Admin role to automatically configure Prometheus data sources. Editor role is insufficient for data source management.
monitoring.131: Log into Grafana as an admin user
monitoring.132: Navigate to Configuration > API Keys (or Administration > Service Accounts > API Keys in newer versions)
monitoring.133: Click "Add API key" or "New API Key"
monitoring.134: Configure the key: Name: SysManage Integration Role: Admin (required for automatic data source configuration) Time to live: Set expiration as needed (or leave blank for no expiration)
monitoring.136: Copy the generated API key immediately (it will only be shown once)
monitoring.137: Store the key securely - SysManage will store it encrypted in OpenBAO vault
monitoring.139: Navigate to Settings > Integrations in SysManage web UI
monitoring.140: Scroll to the Grafana Integration card
monitoring.142: Choose your configuration method: Use Managed Server: Select a Grafana server managed by SysManage (requires "Monitoring Server" role with package "grafana") Manual Configuration: Toggle off "Use Managed Server" and enter your Grafana URL (e.g., http://grafana.example.com:3000 )
monitoring.143: Paste the API key into the "API Key" field
monitoring.145: SysManage will automatically: Securely store the API key in OpenBAO vault Verify connectivity to Grafana Create "SysManage Prometheus" data source in Grafana Configure the data source to point to your Prometheus instance
monitoring.146: Click "Check Health" to verify the connection
monitoring.148: 401 Unauthorized: API key is invalid or has been deleted in Grafana. Generate a new key.
monitoring.149: 403 Permission Denied: API key has insufficient permissions. Ensure it has Admin role, not just Editor.
monitoring.150: Connection timeout: Check that the Grafana URL is correct and the server is reachable from SysManage.
monitoring.151: Bad gateway: If using "localhost" in Grafana URL, change to the actual hostname or IP address that Grafana can reach.
monitoring.153: Note: When you configure Grafana integration with an Admin-level API key, SysManage automatically creates and configures the Prometheus data source. Manual configuration is only needed if automatic setup fails.
monitoring.155: In Grafana, navigate to Configuration > Data Sources
monitoring.156: Look for "SysManage Prometheus" in the list
monitoring.157: Click on it to view the configuration: Name: SysManage Prometheus URL: http://your-sysmanage-host:9091 Access: Server (proxy) Default: Set as default data source
monitoring.159: You should see a green success message: "Data source is working"
monitoring.161: If automatic configuration fails, you can manually add the data source:
monitoring.162: In Grafana, navigate to Configuration > Data Sources
monitoring.165: Configure the data source: Name: SysManage Prometheus URL: http://your-sysmanage-host:9091 (use actual hostname, not "localhost") Access: Server (default) Scrape interval: 15s HTTP Method: POST

package-management.005: Comprehensive guide to managing software packages across your infrastructure including installation, uninstallation, and operation tracking.
package-management.007: SysManage provides a unified interface for managing software packages across multiple operating systems and package managers. The platform supports both installation and uninstallation operations with comprehensive tracking, logging, and status monitoring.
package-management.009: Cross-Platform Support: Works with apt, yum/dnf, pkg (FreeBSD), pkg_add (OpenBSD), pkgin (NetBSD), and Homebrew
package-management.010: Batch Operations: Install or uninstall multiple packages in a single operation
package-management.011: Real-time Tracking: Monitor operation status and progress in real-time
package-management.012: Comprehensive Logging: Detailed logs for all package operations
package-management.013: User Attribution: Track which user initiated each operation
package-management.014: Safety Features: Confirmation dialogs and rollback capabilities
package-management.017: Package installation in SysManage follows a structured workflow:
package-management.018: User initiates installation from the web interface
package-management.019: Request is validated and queued on the server
package-management.020: Installation request is sent to the target agent via message queue
package-management.021: Agent downloads and installs the package using the appropriate package manager
package-management.022: Installation results are reported back and logged
package-management.026: Navigate to the host detail page
package-management.028: Use the search functionality to find available packages
package-management.029: Click the "Install" button next to the desired package
package-management.030: Confirm the installation in the dialog
package-management.031: Monitor progress in the "Software Changes" tab
package-management.033: SysManage supports installing multiple packages in a single operation through the API. This is efficient for system setup and bulk deployments.
package-management.035: Every installation operation is tracked with the following information:
package-management.036: Request ID: Unique UUID for the installation request
package-management.037: Package Details: Name, version, and package manager
package-management.038: User Attribution: Who requested the installation
package-management.039: Timestamps: Request time, start time, and completion time
package-management.040: Status: pending, queued, installing, completed, failed, cancelled
package-management.041: Results: Success/failure status and installation logs
package-management.044: Package uninstallation follows a similar but more safety-focused workflow:
package-management.045: User initiates uninstallation from the software inventory
package-management.046: System displays confirmation dialog with package details
package-management.047: Request is validated and queued with operation type "uninstall"
package-management.048: Uninstallation request is sent to the target agent
package-management.049: Agent removes the package using the appropriate package manager
package-management.050: Uninstallation results are reported back and logged
package-management.051: Software inventory is updated to reflect changes
package-management.052: Using the Web Interface for Uninstallation
package-management.054: Navigate to the host detail page
package-management.056: Find the installed package you want to remove
package-management.057: Click the "Uninstall" button next to the package
package-management.058: Important: Carefully review the confirmation dialog
package-management.060: Monitor progress in the "Software Changes" tab
package-management.062: Package uninstallation is irreversible through the UI
package-management.063: Removing system packages may cause system instability
package-management.064: Dependencies may be automatically removed by the package manager
package-management.065: Always test uninstallations in non-production environments first
package-management.066: Create system backups before removing critical packages
package-management.068: Multiple packages can be uninstalled in a single operation via the API. This is useful for:

package-management.076: Package Information: Name, version, and dependencies removed
package-management.077: Audit Trail: Complete record of who removed what and when
package-management.078: Command Output: Full package manager output for troubleshooting
package-management.079: Status Tracking: Real-time status updates during removal

package-management.084: Operation Type: Install or Uninstall label with appropriate styling
package-management.085: Package Name: Name of the package that was modified

package-uninstall-troubleshooting.005: Comprehensive guide for diagnosing and resolving package uninstallation issues in SysManage environments.
package-uninstall-troubleshooting.007: Package uninstallation can encounter various issues due to system dependencies, permissions, package manager states, and network conditions. This guide provides systematic approaches to diagnose and resolve common uninstallation problems.
package-uninstall-troubleshooting.009: Identify the Problem: Understand the specific error or failure mode
package-uninstall-troubleshooting.010: Gather Information: Collect logs, system state, and environmental details
package-uninstall-troubleshooting.011: Isolate the Cause: Determine if the issue is system, package, or SysManage related
package-uninstall-troubleshooting.012: Apply Solution: Implement appropriate fixes based on root cause
package-uninstall-troubleshooting.013: Verify Resolution: Confirm the issue is resolved and system is stable
package-uninstall-troubleshooting.014: Document Findings: Record solutions for future reference
package-uninstall-troubleshooting.016: Software Changes Tab: Real-time operation status and error messages
package-uninstall-troubleshooting.017: Agent Logs: Detailed command output and system interaction logs
package-uninstall-troubleshooting.018: System Logs: Operating system and package manager logs
