phase8-features.005: How to use the new Settings tabs, the HostDetail Compliance tab, and the Broadcast Refresh action shipped in Phase 8 (v2.0.0.0).
phase8-features.007: Access Groups + Registration Keys — hierarchical RBAC scoping and pre-shared agent-enrollment tokens.
phase8-features.008: Scheduled Update Profiles — cron-driven fleet updates with security-only and staggered-rollout options.
phase8-features.009: Package Compliance Profiles — REQUIRED / BLOCKED package policy evaluated against each host.
phase8-features.010: Audit-log enhancements — script EXECUTE entries with stdout/stderr, plus CSV export.
phase8-features.011: Broadcast Messaging — single-shot fan-out to every connected agent.
phase8-features.012: Report Branding (Pro+) — org logo and header text injected into every generated report.
phase8-features.013: Custom Report Templates (Pro+) — admin-defined column selections layered on the eight built-in reports.
phase8-features.014: Dynamic Secrets (Pro+) — short-lived OpenBAO-backed credentials that expire automatically.
phase8-features.016: An access group is a named scope you can hang hosts and users off. Groups can nest up to 10 levels deep — a user granted a top-level group inherits every descendant.
phase8-features.018: Click Add Group , fill in name + optional description, optionally pick a parent.
phase8-features.019: Use the row's edit / delete buttons to rename, reparent, or remove. Re-parenting refuses cycles and depth violations with a clear 400 error.
phase8-features.020: Registration keys live on the same tab. A key optionally binds an enrolling host to an access group AND optionally auto-approves it (skipping the manual approval gate). The plaintext key value is shown once in the create dialog — copy it before you close the modal, since there is no recovery.
phase8-features.021: To enroll a host with a key: paste it into the agent's registration_key config field before first start, or pass it as the --registration-key CLI argument.
phase8-features.023: Each profile schedules an apply_updates command against a tag-filtered host set on a POSIX cron schedule.
phase8-features.024: Open Settings → Update Profiles , click Add Profile .
phase8-features.025: Set the cron expression. The OSS parser supports lists ( 1,15,30 ), ranges ( 9-17 ), step intervals ( */15 ), day/month names ( mon-fri ), and POSIX dom/dow OR-semantics.
phase8-features.026: Toggle Security Updates Only to limit the agent to the security channel.
phase8-features.027: Set Staggered Window (minutes) to spread agent dispatch over a window — useful to avoid all hosts pulling at once.
phase8-features.028: Pick an optional tag to scope the profile (omit for the entire fleet).
phase8-features.029: Use Trigger Now on any row to fire immediately for that profile.
phase8-features.030: Settings → Compliance Profiles & HostDetail Compliance Tab
phase8-features.031: Compliance profiles list packages a host MUST or MUST NOT have. Constraints can include version comparisons ( >= , != , ~= , etc.) using PEP 440 / SemVer rules with a lex fallback for non-SemVer.
phase8-features.032: Define a profile in Settings → Compliance Profiles .
phase8-features.033: Add constraints: pick the package, set type ( Required or Blocked ), optionally constrain the version.
phase8-features.034: Open any host in the Hosts list, navigate to its Compliance tab, and choose between two evaluation modes: Scan — server evaluates against the cached software_package inventory. Fast. Live Scan — dispatches the constraints to the agent, which evaluates against its current installed-package state and reports back via the existing WS command channel. Slower but always-current.
phase8-features.035: The result row shows Compliant / Non-Compliant / Pending . Expand any non-compliant row to see the per-constraint violation list.
phase8-features.037: Click the Broadcast Refresh button on the Hosts page header to send a single refresh_inventory envelope to every connected agent. The result toast surfaces delivered_count and elapsed_ms so you can verify the <5 s SLA from the UI.
phase8-features.039: The Pro+ reporting engine prepends an org-branding strip (logo + company name + header text) to every PDF and every HTML report.
phase8-features.041: Type your company name and any header text (e.g. "Confidential — Internal Use" ).
phase8-features.042: Click Upload Logo and pick a PNG, JPEG, SVG, or WEBP file (≤ 1 MB).
phase8-features.043: Click Save . The next report you generate carries the branding.
phase8-features.045: A template is a saved column-subset (and order) for one of the eight base report types. When you generate a report with a template applied, the renderer drops columns the template omits and reorders the rest to match.
phase8-features.046: Open Settings → Report Templates , click Add Template .
phase8-features.047: Pick a base report type. The available-fields panel shows the column codes the renderer recognizes for that type.
phase8-features.048: Tick the columns you want, in the order you want them.
phase8-features.049: Save. To apply: include ?template_id=<uuid> on a /view/... or /generate/... URL, or pick the template from the Reports page when launching a generation.
phase8-features.051: Issue, revoke, and reconcile short-lived credentials backed by your OpenBAO / Vault deployment. The plaintext value is shown exactly once at issue time — copy it immediately. SysManage stores only the lease metadata (kind, role, TTL, expires_at) and never the secret itself.
phase8-features.052: Open Settings → Dynamic Secrets , click Issue Lease .
phase8-features.053: Pick a kind ( token , database , ssh ), enter the OpenBAO backend role, set TTL (60 s – 24 h).
phase8-features.054: Copy the secret from the reveal modal. Closing the dialog is irreversible — you cannot retrieve the value again.
phase8-features.055: Use the leases table to revoke ahead of expiry or to filter by status.
phase8-features.056: Click Reconcile to mark any ACTIVE rows whose TTL has elapsed as EXPIRED — useful if the sweeper hasn't run.
phase8-features.058: Every state-changing action above is logged to the audit log with the operator's user, the entity touched, and the result. Use Reports → Audit Log to view, filter, and export to CSV.
phase8-features.060: All Phase 8 UI strings are translated into 14 languages ( ar , de , en , es , fr , hi , it , ja , ko , nl , pt , ru , zh_CN , zh_TW ). Switch the active locale from your user-profile dropdown.

phase9-stabilization.005: What changed in v2.1.0.0. Phase 9 is a polish-and-fix release that follows the Phase 8 (v2.0.0.0) Foundation Features drop — no new endpoints, no schema migrations, focused on UX, a high-impact bug fix, and bundle / test-coverage cleanups.
phase9-stabilization.007: Scrollable nav and action bars — the top main menu, Settings tabs, and the Hosts-page action bar now pan with prev/next arrows when the viewport is too narrow to show every item, and labels like OS Upgrades never break across two lines.
phase9-stabilization.008: Report branding fix — Phase 8.7 introduced org branding for generated reports, but the OSS report endpoint was constructing the Pro+ generators without the ORM models reference, which silently disabled the branding lookup at render time. Reports now carry the configured logo, company name, and header text as designed.
phase9-stabilization.009: Frontend bundle — an attempted vendor-chunk split in vite.config.ts was reverted after Playwright caught a runtime TDZ error caused by React 19 + MUI 7 internal circular imports across split chunks. Default Vite chunking is in use; the chunk-size warning limit was raised to 2500 KB to silence the build warning.
phase9-stabilization.010: Test-coverage push — 83 new endpoint-coverage tests added for previously thin areas (diagnostics, host account management, antivirus status / defaults, firewall status, third-party repos, user preferences, reports, secrets, Graylog integration, scripts, host monitoring, OpenTelemetry, packages-operations, queue, host hostname, host Graylog).
phase9-stabilization.011: Lint / scan clean — Pylint 10.00/10, ESLint 0 errors, SonarQube scan passes. No skipped tests in the suite.
phase9-stabilization.013: Three places previously wrapped or hid items when the viewport got narrow:
phase9-stabilization.014: Top main menu ( Dashboard / Hosts / Updates / OS Upgrades / ... ) — items used to wrap mid-label (e.g. OS Upgrades would split across two lines). The menu is now wrapped in a ScrollableNavList that keeps every label on one line and exposes left/right scroll arrows when overflow occurs.
phase9-stabilization.015: Settings tabs ( Integrations / Access Groups / Update Profiles / ... ) — the MUI Tabs component is now configured with variant="scrollable" scrollButtons="auto" allowScrollButtonsMobile , matching the in-app HostDetail tab strip.
phase9-stabilization.016: Hosts-page action bar ( Approve Selected / Reboot / Update Agent / ... ) — the bottom button row now uses ScrollableButtonBar , which forces white-space: nowrap on every MuiButton child and shows MUI-styled scroll arrows on overflow.
phase9-stabilization.017: Behavior is identical across desktop and mobile: arrows hide when the row fits, appear with a fade when it doesn't, and the focus order skips hidden arrows so keyboard navigation stays clean.
phase9-stabilization.018: Bug fix: Report branding now appears on generated reports
phase9-stabilization.019: If you uploaded a logo or saved a company name under Settings → Report Branding after Phase 8.7 shipped and didn't see it on subsequent reports, this is the fix. Background:
phase9-stabilization.020: Phase 8.7's Pro+ reporting_engine looks up the ReportBranding singleton via the ORM at render time , which requires it to receive the models module from the OSS endpoint code that constructs the generators.
phase9-stabilization.021: The OSS endpoint code ( backend/api/reports/endpoints.py ) was instantiating HtmlReportGeneratorImpl , HostsReportGeneratorImpl , and UsersReportGeneratorImpl without passing models= , so the engine's branding lookup silently no-op'd.
phase9-stabilization.022: v2.1.0.0 passes models=models to all three generator constructors. Both the /view/... (HTML) and /generate/... (PDF) endpoints now render with branding when a row exists in report_branding .
phase9-stabilization.023: No action required for upgraders. Existing branding rows render automatically after upgrade. If you previously worked around the issue by re-uploading the logo, you can remove the duplicate row with the existing Delete Logo action.
phase9-stabilization.024: Bug fix: Audit-log report used the wrong generator
phase9-stabilization.025: A latent bug in the same endpoint module routed GET /api/reports/generate/audit-log to HostsReportGeneratorImpl.generate_audit_log_report , which doesn't exist. The route now correctly calls UsersReportGeneratorImpl.generate_audit_log_report . The HTML /view/audit-log path was unaffected.
phase9-stabilization.027: An earlier Phase 9 attempt to split the Vite vendor bundle with manualChunks — separating React, MUI core, MUI DataGrid, MUI Charts, MUI Icons, i18next, axios, react-icons, and Emotion into independent chunks — produced a runtime TDZ error on first page load:
phase9-stabilization.028: Both messages are symptoms of the same root cause: the React 19 + MUI 7 dependency graph contains internal circular imports between react , react-dom , react/jsx-runtime , scheduler , @emotion/react , @mui/system , and @mui/material . When those packages live in different ES-module chunks, the chunks evaluate in an order where one binding is referenced before its declaring chunk has finished evaluating, hitting the temporal-dead-zone trap.
phase9-stabilization.029: The Playwright auth.setup.ts CI job caught this — the login page rendered as a blank <div id="root"> and the heading locator timed out. v2.1.0.0 reverts to default Vite chunking and raises chunkSizeWarningLimit to 2500 KB to silence the (cosmetic) chunk-size warning during build.
phase9-stabilization.030: Re-attempting the split is fine, but only with a verified Playwright run proving the new shape boots a fresh browser session without TDZ errors.
phase9-stabilization.032: The Phase 9 closeout enforces a "100% clean" bar:
phase9-stabilization.033: Pylint — 10.00/10 across the whole backend and agent codebase.
phase9-stabilization.034: ESLint — 0 errors, 0 warnings on the frontend ( --max-warnings 0 ).
phase9-stabilization.035: Backend tests — 4441 pytest cases, all passing. Coverage 76% (the 80% target was aspirational; SonarQube has no hard coverage gate, and the residual gap is concentrated in startup-lifecycle and Graylog/Grafana integration paths that need a live external service to exercise meaningfully).
phase9-stabilization.036: Agent tests — 93% coverage, exceeds the 80% Phase 9 target.
phase9-stabilization.037: Frontend tests — Vitest passes with the new ResizeObserver polyfill in setupTests.ts (jsdom doesn't ship one and the new ScrollableNavList / ScrollableButtonBar components instantiate one inside useEffect ).
phase9-stabilization.038: SonarQube — make sonarqube-scan reports EXECUTION SUCCESS. No new issues introduced.
phase9-stabilization.040: None. v2.1.0.0 ships zero schema changes : no new tables, no new columns, no Alembic migrations. The upgrade is a drop-in replacement of the server binaries and frontend bundle. Agents do not need to be re-deployed.
phase9-stabilization.041: If you are upgrading from a release older than v2.0.0.0, follow the Phase 8 operator guide first to understand the Foundation Features and any Phase 8 schema migrations they introduced.
phase9-stabilization.043: "My report still doesn't show branding after upgrading"
phase9-stabilization.044: Verify a branding row exists: Settings → Report Branding should show your logo / company name. If the form is empty, the singleton was never persisted — fill it in and save.
phase9-stabilization.045: Confirm the Pro+ reporting_engine module loaded successfully. Without it, OSS deployments return 402 from /api/reports/generate/... and /api/reports/view/... ; the branding lookup is a Pro+ codepath.
phase9-stabilization.046: Hard-refresh your browser cache on the report-viewer page — the in-page iframe can serve a cached pre-fix HTML.
phase9-stabilization.047: Re-generate one report and inspect the audit_log row: a successful render writes an entry whose details JSON includes {"branding_applied": true} .
phase9-stabilization.048: "My nav arrows don't appear even though items are clipped"
phase9-stabilization.049: The arrows only render when the inner scroller's scrollWidth exceeds its clientWidth . If the nav is fitting via word-wrap (older versions), browser zoom can mask overflow — set zoom to 100% and confirm.
phase9-stabilization.050: Verify your browser supports ResizeObserver (every supported browser does — IE11 isn't a target).
phase9-stabilization.051: If the arrows render but never become enabled, check the browser console for CSS errors against scrollnav / ScrollableButtonBar rules.
phase9-stabilization.053: If the build fails, the most common cause is a stale node_modules ; rm -rf frontend/node_modules && npm install .
phase9-stabilization.054: The chunk-size warning threshold is now 2500 KB. The single index chunk lands around 2 MB — large but not pathological. Custom chunk splits are tempting but unsafe with React 19 + MUI 7 (see "Frontend bundle" section above).
phase9-stabilization.056: All Phase 8 strings remain translated in the 14 supported locales ( ar , de , en , es , fr , hi , it , ja , ko , nl , pt , ru , zh_CN , zh_TW ). Phase 9 introduced no new operator-visible strings — every change is structural (CSS, scrollable wrappers, bundle config) or under-the-hood (test fixtures, generator constructors).
phase9-stabilization.058: External penetration test — vendor engagement; carried over from Phase 7 / Phase 8 as a budget item, not a missed deliverable.
phase9-stabilization.059: Backend coverage from 76% → 80% — the residual gap is concentrated in startup lifecycle and Graylog / Grafana integration code that needs a live external service in CI to exercise; addressing that requires the integration-tests harness work tracked under Phase 10 stabilization.
phase9-stabilization.060: Pro+ Enterprise-tier modules (virtualization_engine, fleet provisioning) — Phase 10 scope.

third-party-repositories.005: Managing third-party package repositories on individual hosts through the SysManage web interface.
third-party-repositories.007: SysManage provides a graphical interface for managing third-party package repositories on individual hosts. This feature allows administrators to view, add, enable, disable, and remove repositories like Ubuntu PPAs, Fedora COPR repositories, openSUSE OBS repositories, macOS Homebrew taps, FreeBSD/NetBSD custom repositories, and Windows Chocolatey sources directly from the web UI.
third-party-repositories.009: View Current Repositories: See all third-party repositories currently configured on a host
third-party-repositories.010: Add Repositories: Add new repositories using OS-specific formats (PPA, COPR, OBS, Homebrew taps, etc.)
third-party-repositories.011: Enable/Disable: Toggle repositories on and off without removing them
third-party-repositories.012: Remove Repositories: Delete unwanted repositories from the host
third-party-repositories.013: Multi-Platform Support: Works across Debian/Ubuntu, RHEL/Fedora/CentOS, openSUSE/SLES, FreeBSD, NetBSD, macOS, and Windows
third-party-repositories.014: Real-Time Updates: Changes are sent to the host agent and applied immediately
third-party-repositories.016: This feature requires the host to be in privileged mode (running as root/administrator)
third-party-repositories.017: You must have appropriate security role permissions (ADD_THIRD_PARTY_REPOSITORY, DELETE_THIRD_PARTY_REPOSITORY, etc.)
third-party-repositories.018: OpenBSD does not support third-party repositories and this feature is not available for OpenBSD hosts
third-party-repositories.019: Changes are applied per-host - this is not a centralized fleet-wide repository management tool
third-party-repositories.021: The third-party repository management interface is accessed from the Host Detail page:
third-party-repositories.022: Navigate to the Hosts page in the SysManage web UI
third-party-repositories.023: Click on a host to open its detail page
third-party-repositories.024: Scroll to the Third-Party Repositories section
third-party-repositories.025: If the host is in privileged mode, you'll see the repository management interface
third-party-repositories.026: If you don't see the third-party repositories section, ensure:
third-party-repositories.027: The host agent is running in privileged mode (root/administrator)
third-party-repositories.028: You have the necessary security permissions
third-party-repositories.029: The host is not running OpenBSD
third-party-repositories.030: Supported Repository Formats by Operating System
third-party-repositories.032: PPAs are the standard way to add third-party repositories on Ubuntu systems.
third-party-repositories.034: PPA Owner: The username of the PPA owner (e.g., "deadsnakes")
third-party-repositories.035: PPA Name: The name of the PPA (e.g., "ppa")
third-party-repositories.037: Example: To add the popular deadsnakes PPA for Python versions, enter:
third-party-repositories.041: COPR (Cool Other Package Repo) is Fedora's community repository system.
third-party-repositories.043: COPR Owner: The COPR project owner (e.g., "@dotnet-sig")
third-party-repositories.046: Example: To add the .NET COPR repository:
third-party-repositories.050: OBS repositories provide additional packages for SUSE-based systems.
third-party-repositories.053: Project Path: The OBS project path (e.g., "devel:/languages:/python")
third-party-repositories.055: Repository Name: A name for the repository (e.g., "python-devel")
third-party-repositories.056: Example: To add Python development packages from OBS:
third-party-repositories.062: Taps are third-party Homebrew repositories hosted on GitHub or other Git servers.

third-party-repositories.067: Example: To add the Homebrew cask-versions tap:
third-party-repositories.071: Custom pkg repositories provide additional packages for FreeBSD.
third-party-repositories.073: Repository Name: A name for your repository (e.g., "custom-repo")
third-party-repositories.074: Repository URL: The full URL to the repository
third-party-repositories.075: Example: To add a custom FreeBSD repository:
third-party-repositories.079: Custom pkgsrc repositories for NetBSD binary packages.
third-party-repositories.081: Repository Name: A name for your repository
third-party-repositories.082: Repository URL: The full URL to the repository
third-party-repositories.084: Add custom Chocolatey package sources for Windows hosts.
third-party-repositories.087: Source Name: A name for the source (e.g., "company-repo")
third-party-repositories.088: Source URL: The URL to your Chocolatey feed
third-party-repositories.089: Example: To add a private company Chocolatey repository:
third-party-repositories.095: The repository list shows all currently configured third-party repositories on the host:
third-party-repositories.097: Type: The repository type (ppa, copr, obs, tap, etc.)
third-party-repositories.099: Enabled: Whether the repository is currently enabled
third-party-repositories.100: File Path: The configuration file location on the host
third-party-repositories.101: The list auto-refreshes every 30 seconds to show current status.
third-party-repositories.104: The dialog will show fields appropriate for your host's operating system
third-party-repositories.105: Fill in the required fields (see supported formats above)
third-party-repositories.106: The constructed repository string will be shown as you type
third-party-repositories.107: Click "Add" to send the command to the host agent
third-party-repositories.108: The repository list will refresh automatically after a few seconds
third-party-repositories.110: Select one or more repositories from the list
third-party-repositories.112: Confirm the action in the dialog
third-party-repositories.113: The changes will be applied to the host immediately
third-party-repositories.114: Note: Disabling a repository keeps it configured but prevents package managers from using it.
third-party-repositories.116: Select one or more repositories from the list
third-party-repositories.118: Confirm the deletion in the dialog
third-party-repositories.119: The repositories will be permanently removed from the host
third-party-repositories.120: Warning: This permanently removes the repository configuration files from the host.
third-party-repositories.122: Click the "Refresh" button to manually update the repository list. The list also auto-refreshes every 30 seconds.
third-party-repositories.124: To use third-party repository management features, users need specific security role permissions:
third-party-repositories.125: ADD_THIRD_PARTY_REPOSITORY: Required to add new repositories
third-party-repositories.129: Users without these permissions will see the repository list but won't be able to make changes.
third-party-repositories.132: Verify Sources: Only add repositories from trusted sources you verify
third-party-repositories.133: Test First: Test new repositories on non-production hosts first
third-party-repositories.134: Document Purpose: Keep external documentation of why each repository was added
third-party-repositories.135: Regular Audits: Periodically review configured repositories and remove unused ones
third-party-repositories.136: Monitor Changes: Use SysManage's audit logging to track repository changes
third-party-repositories.138: Limit Repositories: Only add repositories that are actually needed
third-party-repositories.139: Disable Rather Than Delete: Disable repositories temporarily instead of deleting if you might need them later
third-party-repositories.140: Check Compatibility: Ensure repositories match your exact OS version
third-party-repositories.141: Update After Adding: Run package updates after adding new repositories
third-party-repositories.144: Symptoms: Third-party repositories section doesn't appear on Host Detail page
third-party-repositories.146: Verify the host agent is running in privileged mode (root/administrator)
third-party-repositories.147: Check that your user account has the required permissions
third-party-repositories.148: Confirm the host is not running OpenBSD (not supported)
third-party-repositories.149: Ensure the host is approved and connected
third-party-repositories.151: Symptoms: Error message when trying to add a repository
third-party-repositories.153: Verify the repository format is correct for the operating system
third-party-repositories.154: Check that the repository URL is accessible from the host
third-party-repositories.156: Review the error message for specific details
third-party-repositories.157: Check the host agent logs for more information
third-party-repositories.159: Symptoms: Repository added/removed but not shown in list
third-party-repositories.161: Wait a few seconds for the host agent to process the command
third-party-repositories.162: Click the Refresh button to manually update the list
third-party-repositories.163: Check the host's WebSocket connection status
third-party-repositories.164: Verify no network issues between server and agent
third-party-repositories.166: Symptoms: "Permission denied" errors when making changes
third-party-repositories.168: Verify you have the appropriate security role permissions
third-party-repositories.169: Contact your SysManage administrator to grant permissions
third-party-repositories.170: Ensure the host agent is running with sufficient privileges
third-party-repositories.172: SysManage provides a centralized way to manage default third-party repositories that are automatically applied to all hosts of a specific operating system. This feature streamlines repository management across your entire fleet.
third-party-repositories.174: Default repositories are configured globally in the SysManage settings and are automatically:
third-party-repositories.175: Applied to New Hosts: When a host is approved during registration, all default repositories matching its OS are automatically added
third-party-repositories.176: Applied to Existing Hosts: When you create a new default repository, it is automatically added to all existing approved hosts with matching OS
third-party-repositories.177: Shown in Repository Lists: Default repositories appear in each host's repository list with a "Default" source badge
third-party-repositories.179: Navigate to Settings in the main navigation bar
third-party-repositories.183: In the Host Defaults > Repositories settings, click "Add Default Repository"
third-party-repositories.184: Select the Operating System from the dropdown (Ubuntu, Fedora, CentOS Stream, openSUSE, etc.)
third-party-repositories.185: Select the Package Manager (APT, DNF, YUM, Zypper, etc.)
third-party-repositories.186: Fill in the OS-specific fields (same as adding a host-specific repository): Ubuntu/Debian: PPA Owner and PPA Name Fedora/RHEL/CentOS: COPR Owner and COPR Project openSUSE/SLES: OBS URL, Project Path, Distribution Version, and Repository Name macOS: Tap User and Tap Repository FreeBSD: Repository Name and URL NetBSD: Repository Name and URL Windows: Repository Type, Name, and URL
third-party-repositories.187: The repository identifier will be constructed automatically as you type
third-party-repositories.188: Click "Add" to save the default repository
third-party-repositories.190: When you add a default repository:
third-party-repositories.191: The repository configuration is saved to the database
third-party-repositories.192: SysManage finds all approved hosts matching the specified operating system
third-party-repositories.193: Add repository commands are queued for each matching host
third-party-repositories.194: The commands are sent to connected agents via WebSocket
third-party-repositories.195: Each agent applies the repository to its host
third-party-repositories.196: Note: If an agent is not connected when the default repository is created, the system will automatically retry sending the command up to 3 times with exponential backoff (1 minute, 2 minutes, 4 minutes).
third-party-repositories.198: When viewing a host's Third-Party Repositories list, default repositories are shown with:
third-party-repositories.200: The repository is not selectable for deletion or modification
third-party-repositories.201: If the same repository exists as both a default and host-specific entry, only the default is shown (deduplication)
third-party-repositories.204: Go to Settings > Host Defaults > Repositories
third-party-repositories.205: Select the default repository you want to remove
third-party-repositories.208: When you delete a default repository:
third-party-repositories.209: The repository is removed from the default repository list
third-party-repositories.210: Delete commands are automatically queued for all hosts that have this repository
third-party-repositories.211: The repository is removed from all affected hosts
third-party-repositories.212: Future hosts will not receive this repository
third-party-repositories.215: VIEW_DEFAULT_REPOSITORIES: To view the default repositories list
third-party-repositories.216: ADD_DEFAULT_REPOSITORY: To create new default repositories
third-party-repositories.219: Organization Standards: Use default repositories to enforce organizational standards across all hosts
third-party-repositories.220: Security Repositories: Add security-focused repositories as defaults to ensure all hosts receive security updates
third-party-repositories.221: Test Before Deploying: Test repositories on a few hosts before making them defaults
third-party-repositories.222: Document Purpose: Keep documentation of why each default repository exists
third-party-repositories.223: OS Version Matching: Be aware that repositories are matched by OS name only (e.g., "Ubuntu"), not by specific version
third-party-repositories.224: Monitor Application: Check that default repositories are successfully applied to hosts after creation
third-party-repositories.225: Difference Between Default and Host-Specific Repositories
third-party-repositories.230: Applied to all hosts with matching OS
third-party-repositories.231: Applied only to a single host
third-party-repositories.236: Yes, to new and existing hosts
third-party-repositories.239: No, must be removed from defaults
third-party-repositories.245: OpenBSD Not Supported: OpenBSD does not support third-party repositories by design
third-party-repositories.246: Privileged Mode Required: The host agent must be running as root/administrator
third-party-repositories.247: OS Name Matching Only: Default repositories match by OS name (e.g., "Ubuntu") not by specific version
third-party-repositories.248: No Version-Specific Defaults: You cannot create default repositories for specific OS versions (e.g., only Ubuntu 24.04)
third-party-repositories.249: Retry Limit: If a host is offline when a default repository is added, the system retries 3 times before giving up
third-party-repositories.250: Platform-Specific: Each OS has different repository formats and requirements

user-management.005: Complete guide to managing user accounts, roles, permissions, and authentication in SysManage.
user-management.007: SysManage implements a comprehensive user management system with role-based access control (RBAC), secure authentication, and granular permissions. This system ensures that users have appropriate access to system resources while maintaining security and compliance requirements.
user-management.010: New user accounts can be created through the web interface or API:
user-management.014: Fill in required information: Username (unique identifier) Email address Full name Initial password Role assignment
user-management.020: Personal Information: Users can update their own profile details
user-management.021: Password Changes: Users can change their passwords
user-management.022: Email Updates: Email changes may require verification
user-management.023: Profile Pictures: Upload and manage profile images
user-management.025: Role Changes: Administrators can modify user roles
user-management.026: Account Status: Enable, disable, or lock accounts
user-management.028: Password Resets: Force password resets for security
user-management.031: Recommended approach for temporarily disabling access:
user-management.032: Preserves user data and audit trails
user-management.041: Document the deletion in audit logs
user-management.111: Create custom roles for specific organizational needs:
user-management.112: Department-specific roles: IT, Security, Compliance teams
user-management.113: Project-based access: Temporary permissions for specific projects
user-management.114: Regional access: Geographic or organizational unit restrictions
user-management.115: Specialized functions: Backup operators, monitoring specialists
user-management.119: Built-in username/password authentication with advanced security features:
user-management.121: Account Lockouts: Protection against brute force attacks
user-management.123: Password History: Prevention of password reuse
user-management.139: Session Timeout: Automatic logout after inactivity
user-management.140: Concurrent Sessions: Limit number of active sessions
user-management.141: Session Monitoring: Track active user sessions
user-management.145: Monitor and manage active user sessions:
user-management.154: Create user account with appropriate role
user-management.187: Principle of Least Privilege: Grant minimum necessary permissions
user-management.188: Regular Access Reviews: Periodic audit of user permissions
user-management.189: Separation of Duties: Distribute administrative responsibilities
user-management.190: Account Monitoring: Monitor for unusual account activity
user-management.191: Privileged Account Management: Special handling for administrative accounts
user-management.193: Strong Password Policies: Enforce complex password requirements
user-management.194: Multi-Factor Authentication: Require MFA for administrative access
user-management.195: Session Security: Implement secure session management
user-management.196: Login Monitoring: Track and alert on login anomalies
user-management.197: Regular Password Updates: Enforce periodic password changes
user-management.199: Audit Trail: Maintain comprehensive user activity logs
user-management.200: Data Protection: Ensure compliance with privacy regulations
user-management.201: Access Certification: Regular attestation of user access
user-management.202: Segregation of Duties: Implement role-based controls
user-management.203: Documentation: Maintain current user management procedures
user-management.206: Account Locked: Check lockout status and unlock if appropriate
user-management.207: Password Expired: Force password reset or extend expiration
user-management.208: MFA Issues: Provide backup codes or reset MFA
user-management.209: Session Limits: Terminate old sessions or increase limits
user-management.211: Access Denied: Review and adjust user permissions
user-management.212: Role Conflicts: Resolve conflicting role assignments
user-management.213: Resource Access: Check host group assignments
user-management.214: Feature Restrictions: Verify role-based feature access
user-management.216: LDAP Connectivity: Test LDAP server connectivity
user-management.217: SAML Errors: Verify SAML configuration and certificates
user-management.218: Role Mapping: Check external group to role mappings
user-management.223: The Users page, where administrators create accounts and manage roles, status, and access.

os-upgrades.005: The Operating System Upgrades page handles full OS version changes — distribution release upgrades, macOS version upgrades, Windows feature updates, and BSD system upgrades — separately from routine package updates. Use it to see which managed hosts have an OS upgrade available and to apply those upgrades across the fleet.
os-upgrades.007: Routine package updates (security patches and individual package versions) are handled on the Updates page. OS Upgrades are different: they move a host to a new operating-system release — for example Ubuntu 22.04 to 24.04, or a macOS major version — and almost always require a reboot. SysManage keeps the two flows separate so a major upgrade is always a deliberate action.
os-upgrades.009: Select Operating System Upgrades from the navigation. The page shows three summary cards and a table of every detected OS upgrade across your hosts:
os-upgrades.010: Total OS Upgrades — how many hosts have an OS upgrade available.
os-upgrades.011: Security Updates — how many of those upgrades are security-relevant.
os-upgrades.012: Affected Hosts — the number of distinct hosts involved.
os-upgrades.013: Each table row shows the host, the kind of upgrade, and a status pill — Upgrade Requested , Successfully Upgraded , or Upgrade Failed .
os-upgrades.015: SysManage detects and applies OS upgrades on:
os-upgrades.025: Use the OS type filter at the top of the table to narrow the list to a single platform — Ubuntu/Debian, Fedora, openSUSE, macOS, Windows, OpenBSD, FreeBSD, or NetBSD. Choose "All OS Types" to clear the filter. If a filter hides everything, the table shows "No OS upgrades match the current filter".
os-upgrades.028: Optionally filter the list to the platform you want to work on.
os-upgrades.029: Select individual rows, or use Select All to select every upgrade currently shown (the count is displayed next to the control).
os-upgrades.030: Click Execute Selected OS Upgrades . The selected hosts each begin their upgrade and move to the Upgrade Requested state.
os-upgrades.031: Use Refresh to re-check status as upgrades complete; rows move to Successfully Upgraded or Upgrade Failed.
os-upgrades.032: Reboots: an OS upgrade almost always reboots the host as part of moving to the new release. Plan upgrades for a maintenance window, and expect affected hosts to drop offline briefly while they reboot into the new version.
os-upgrades.034: OS upgrades are larger and slower than package updates — they download a full release and reconfigure the system, so they take considerably longer than applying a set of package updates.
os-upgrades.035: There is no automatic rollback for an OS upgrade. Make sure you have a current backup or snapshot before upgrading production hosts.
os-upgrades.036: An upgrade only appears here once the agent has detected that a newer OS release is available for that host's platform.
os-upgrades.037: The Operating System Upgrades page, listing managed hosts that have an OS upgrade available.

audit-log-viewer.005: The Audit Log Viewer lets you browse the recorded history of actions taken in SysManage, narrow the results with filters, and export the matching entries for review or archiving.
audit-log-viewer.007: The audit log records user actions, system events, security events, and data changes across SysManage. The viewer is reached from the Security section of the Reports page. It presents a set of filters, a paginated results table, and export buttons. Use the Refresh button at the top to reload the current view at any time.
audit-log-viewer.009: The Filters panel narrows which entries appear in the results table. Set any combination of the fields below, then click Apply Filters to run the query. Click Reset Filters to clear every field and return to the unfiltered view. Empty filters are ignored, so leaving a field blank means "no restriction" for that field.
audit-log-viewer.010: Search A free-text box that matches against the entry description or entity name.
audit-log-viewer.011: Action Type A dropdown limiting results to a single action. Options are Create , Read , Update , Delete , and Execute , plus All for no restriction.
audit-log-viewer.012: Entity Type A dropdown limiting results to a single kind of object. Options are Host , User , Package , Script , Secret , Certificate , Tag , and User Role , plus All .
audit-log-viewer.013: Category A dropdown limiting results to a functional area. Options are Database , Agent , Authentication , and System , plus All .
audit-log-viewer.014: Entry Type A dropdown limiting results by the nature of the entry. Options are User Action , System Event , Security Event , and Data Change , plus All .
audit-log-viewer.015: Start Date A date-and-time picker. Only entries at or after this moment are shown.
audit-log-viewer.016: End Date A date-and-time picker. Only entries at or before this moment are shown.
audit-log-viewer.017: The Start Date and End Date values are sent to the server as UTC timestamps, so the date range is applied consistently regardless of your local time zone.
audit-log-viewer.019: The results table shows one row per audit entry with the following columns:
audit-log-viewer.020: Timestamp When the action occurred, shown as a UTC timestamp.
audit-log-viewer.021: Username The user associated with the entry.
audit-log-viewer.022: Action The action type (for example CREATE or DELETE ), shown as a chip.
audit-log-viewer.023: Entity The entity type, with the entity name shown beneath it when one is recorded.
audit-log-viewer.024: Description A human-readable description of what happened.
audit-log-viewer.025: Result The outcome, shown as a colored chip: SUCCESS is green, FAILURE is red, and PARTIAL is amber.
audit-log-viewer.027: Results are paged. The pagination control at the bottom of the table lets you choose a page size of 10 , 25 , 50 , or 100 rows, with 25 as the default. The control also displays the range of entries currently shown and the total count of matching entries, and provides controls to move between pages. Changing the page size returns you to the first page.
audit-log-viewer.029: Two export buttons sit at the top of the page next to Refresh :
audit-log-viewer.030: Export CSV Downloads the matching entries as a comma-separated values file.
audit-log-viewer.031: Export PDF Downloads the matching entries as a PDF document.
audit-log-viewer.032: Exports honor the filters currently set on the Filters panel, so the exported file contains the same entries as the result of your query rather than the entire log. The downloaded file is named with the current date, for example audit_log_2026-06-22.csv .
audit-log-viewer.034: Browsing and filtering the audit log is available in all editions of SysManage. Export is a Professional+ feature. In the Community Edition the server rejects export requests with an HTTP 402 response, and the viewer shows the message "Audit log export requires a SysManage Professional+ license." Upgrade to a Professional or higher tier to enable CSV and PDF export.
audit-log-viewer.035: Audit log retention is also a Professional+ capability, handled by the audit engine rather than this viewer. See Audit (Professional+) for retention policy details.
audit-log-viewer.036: The Audit Log Viewer, showing recorded actions with timestamp, user, category, and result.

scripts-guide.005: The Scripts page lets you store shell scripts, run one of them on a single host, and review the results of past runs. It has three tabs: Script Library, Execute Script, and Script Executions.
scripts-guide.007: This page documents the open-source Scripts feature. It is deliberately single-host and stateless: you save a script, pick one host, run it, and read the output. There is no version history, no approval workflow, no scheduling/cron, no typed parameters, and no multi-host or fleet targeting. Those power features live in the Professional+ Automation Engine. If you need scheduled runs, parameterized scripts, run-against-many-hosts, or change approvals, use the Automation Engine instead of this page.
scripts-guide.009: The Script Library tab is a grid of your saved scripts. Each row shows the script Name , Description , Shell Type , Platform , and the last Updated time. Shell type and platform are shown as small labels.
scripts-guide.011: Search filters the grid. Pick which column to search (name, description, shell type, or platform) and type a term.
scripts-guide.012: Column visibility lets you hide or show grid columns; your choice is remembered.
scripts-guide.013: Add Script opens the editor dialog to create a new script. Requires the Add Script permission.
scripts-guide.014: Delete Selected deletes every script whose checkbox is ticked, after a confirmation prompt. Requires the Delete Script permission.
scripts-guide.015: The pencil icon on a row opens that script in the editor dialog (requires Edit Script ). The eye icon opens a read-only view.
scripts-guide.016: The read-only view shows the description, shell-type and platform labels, the last-updated time, and the script body in a syntax-highlighted, read-only editor. From the view dialog you can jump to Edit or Delete if you hold those permissions.
scripts-guide.018: The Add and Edit dialogs share the same form:
scripts-guide.020: Shell Type dropdown. The available shells depend on the selected platform: bash , sh , zsh , and ksh on Linux, macOS, and the BSDs; powershell on Windows, Linux, and macOS; cmd on Windows only.
scripts-guide.021: Platform dropdown: Linux, macOS (darwin), Windows, FreeBSD, OpenBSD, or NetBSD. Changing the platform narrows the shell list, and if the current shell is not valid for the new platform the dialog switches to the first valid one.
scripts-guide.022: Script content editor (Monaco), syntax-highlighted for the chosen shell.
scripts-guide.023: When you have not yet edited the body, the editor pre-fills an appropriate header for the shell and platform: a shebang such as #!/bin/bash or #!/usr/local/bin/bash for Unix shells, a comment header for PowerShell, or @echo off for cmd. The header path follows the platform (for example bash sits in /usr/local/bin on the BSDs). Once you type into the editor the header is left alone.
scripts-guide.024: Name and content are both required to save. Save creates a new script; in Edit mode the button reads Update and overwrites the existing one.
scripts-guide.026: The Execute Script tab runs one saved script on one host. It has two panels: a selection panel on the left and an output panel on the right.
scripts-guide.027: Pick a script from the Select Script dropdown (saved scripts only).
scripts-guide.028: Pick a host from the Select Host dropdown. The host list is filtered to compatible hosts only: the host must have script execution enabled, its platform must match the script's platform, and it must have the script's shell enabled. Each host shows a Connected or Offline label.
scripts-guide.029: Click Execute Now . Requires the Run Script permission. If the chosen host looks offline you are asked to confirm before the run is sent.
scripts-guide.030: After you start a run, the output panel polls for results and shows a live status. When the run finishes, the panel shows the final status label, the exit code , start and completion times, and the captured output split into STDOUT , STDERR , and (if present) an ERROR section. If the agent returned nothing, the panel says so. Possible statuses are pending, running, completed, failed, and timeout.
scripts-guide.031: The refresh icon in the panel header resets the tab, clearing the selected script, host, and output so you can start over.
scripts-guide.033: The Script Executions tab is the run history. The grid lists past executions with Script Name , Host FQDN , Status , Started , Completed , Exit Code , and Execution Time (in seconds). It is sorted newest-first and auto-refreshes roughly every 30 seconds while you are on the tab.
scripts-guide.034: The eye icon opens an execution's details: script name, host, status, execution ID, start and completion times, exit code, run time, and the full stdout, stderr, and error message. The icon is disabled for rows that captured no output.
scripts-guide.035: Tick one or more rows and use Delete Selected Executions to remove history records. This requires the Delete Script Execution permission.
scripts-guide.037: Each action is gated by its own permission, and buttons are hidden when you lack the matching one:
scripts-guide.041: Run Script — execute a script on a host.
scripts-guide.042: Delete Script Execution — remove execution-history records.
scripts-guide.043: The Scripts page with its saved Script Library.

secrets-guide.005: The Secrets page stores named secrets that other parts of SysManage can reference, such as API keys, database credentials, SSH keys, and SSL certificates. This page covers creating, viewing, editing, and deleting those entries.
secrets-guide.007: The page opens on the Saved Secrets table, which lists every secret you have permission to see. The table shows these columns:
secrets-guide.008: Secret Name – the name you gave the secret.
secrets-guide.009: Filename – the optional filename associated with the secret (for example id_rsa.pub , server.crt ). Shows - when blank.
secrets-guide.010: Secret Type – one of the supported types described below.
secrets-guide.011: Secret Subtype – the type-specific subtype (for example the API provider, database engine, key type, or certificate type).
secrets-guide.012: Created Datetime – when the secret was created, shown in your local time.
secrets-guide.013: Actions – per-row buttons to view and edit the secret.
secrets-guide.014: The table supports pagination, adjustable rows-per-page, and column visibility. Use the column-visibility button above the table to hide or show columns and to reset back to the defaults. Each row also has a checkbox for selecting one or more secrets to delete.
secrets-guide.015: The secret value is never displayed in this table. Only metadata is shown.
secrets-guide.017: Each secret has a type, and most types also require a subtype. The supported types are:
secrets-guide.018: API Keys – subtype is the API provider (for example Github, Salesforce).
secrets-guide.019: Database Credentials – subtype is the database engine (mysql, Oracle, PostgreSQL, Microsoft SQL Server, sqlite3).
secrets-guide.020: SSH Key – subtype is the key type (Public, Private, CA).
secrets-guide.021: SSL Certificate – subtype is the certificate type (Root Certificate, Intermediate Certificate, Chain Certificate, Key File, Issued Certificate).
secrets-guide.022: When a type requires a subtype, the Add/Edit dialog shows a required selector for it, and the secret cannot be saved until a valid subtype is chosen.
secrets-guide.024: Click Add Secret below the table to open the Add Secret dialog. The dialog has these fields:
secrets-guide.025: Secret Name – required. The display name for the secret.
secrets-guide.026: Filename – optional. An associated filename, for example id_rsa.pub , server.crt , or database.conf .
secrets-guide.027: Secret Type – choose one of the supported types.
secrets-guide.028: Secret Content – required. Paste the secret value here. This is a multi-line field.
secrets-guide.029: Subtype selector – required for types that use a subtype. Its label changes with the selected type (API Provider, Database Engine, Key Type, or Certificate Type).
secrets-guide.030: Click Save to store the secret, or Cancel to discard. Saving fails with an error message if the name or content is empty, or if a required subtype is not selected.
secrets-guide.032: Click the view (eye) button on a row to open the view dialog. It shows the secret's metadata: secret type, filename (when set), subtype (when set), created-at, and updated-at timestamps.
secrets-guide.033: For security, the secret value itself is not revealed in this dialog. The content area displays a placeholder indicating that the content is hidden.
secrets-guide.035: Click the edit (pencil) button on a row to open the Edit Secret dialog. The name, filename, type, and subtype are pre-filled from the existing secret.
secrets-guide.036: The content field is not pre-filled, for security. In edit mode it is labeled Replace existing secret : enter new content to replace the stored value. Click Update to save your changes.
secrets-guide.038: Select one or more rows using the checkboxes, then click Delete Selected . A confirmation dialog appears before anything is removed; confirm to delete or cancel to keep the secrets. You can delete a single secret or several at once.
secrets-guide.040: Actions on this page are gated by role-based access control. The relevant permissions are:
secrets-guide.041: Add Secret – required to see the Add Secret button.
secrets-guide.042: Edit Secret – required to see the per-row edit button.
secrets-guide.043: Delete Secret – required to see the Delete Selected button.
secrets-guide.044: Viewing the list and opening the view dialog do not require these permissions; the create, edit, and delete controls are hidden when you lack the matching permission.
secrets-guide.046: Vault status, including OpenBAO seal/unseal state, lives on the Settings page under Integrations, not on this page.
secrets-guide.047: The Secrets Engine internals, including key rotation and OpenBAO storage, are documented under Professional+ Secrets .

settings-guide.002: Create, edit, and delete tags used to label and group hosts. Each tag has a name and an optional description, and the grid shows how many hosts carry it and when it was last updated. The view-hosts action opens a dialog listing every host the tag is applied to (FQDN and IPv4), with active hosts highlighted. Search by name or description. Adding, editing, and deleting are gated by the Edit Tags permission; without it the grid is read-only.
settings-guide.004: Configure Ubuntu Pro subscription management for your managed Ubuntu hosts. Store a master/contract key here so it can be used for bulk enrollment of hosts into Ubuntu Pro rather than entering a token per host.
settings-guide.006: Browse the catalog of packages collected from your managed hosts. Summary cards group package counts by operating system and version, broken down per package manager (APT, Snap, Flatpak, fwupd, Homebrew, Winget, Chocolatey, PKG, YUM, DNF, Zypper, Pacman). Search packages by name, optionally filtering by OS/version and package manager; results show name, version, manager, and description. Refresh buttons request a fresh package collection for a single OS/version or for all known platforms. The summary cards auto-refresh every 30 seconds while this tab is open. Package data is gathered from hosts automatically, so the catalog may be empty until collection has run.
settings-guide.008: Manage the operating-system distributions SysManage recognizes for package and update collection. Used to keep the per-distribution metadata that drives package availability and collection across your fleet.
settings-guide.010: Manage the templates the reporting engine uses to generate reports. This tab is part of the Professional+ reporting engine and is only visible when that module is licensed; on the Community edition it is hidden. Pairs with the Branding tab, which supplies the per-report logo and header/footer.
settings-guide.012: Inspect the server-to-agent message queue. This viewer lists expired/failed messages with their type, direction, host ID, priority, and created/expired timestamps. The view-details action shows the full message payload as formatted JSON. Selected messages can be removed from the queue; the delete action is gated by the Delete Queue Message permission.
settings-guide.013: The Settings page, organized into sections for configuration, integrations, and policies.

privileged-execution.005: Comprehensive guide to configuring and managing privileged script execution with SysManage agents, including security considerations and best practices.
privileged-execution.008: Privileged execution in SysManage allows agents to run scripts and commands with elevated permissions, enabling system administration tasks like package installation, service management, and system configuration changes. troubleshooting.005: Comprehensive troubleshooting guide for SysManage agents including common issues, debugging techniques, and resolution strategies.
troubleshooting.007: This guide provides systematic approaches to diagnosing and resolving common issues with SysManage agents. Follow the troubleshooting steps in order, starting with basic connectivity checks and progressing to advanced debugging techniques.
troubleshooting.009: Identify the Problem: Gather symptoms and error messages
troubleshooting.010: Check Basic Requirements: Verify system requirements and configuration
troubleshooting.011: Review Logs: Examine agent and system logs for clues
troubleshooting.012: Test Components: Isolate and test individual components
troubleshooting.014: Verify Resolution: Confirm the issue is resolved
troubleshooting.016: Start with these quick checks to identify common issues.
troubleshooting.026: Agent attempting to connect to server
troubleshooting.045: Verify server configuration: # Check server hostname and port ping sysmanage.example.com telnet sysmanage.example.com 8080 # Test HTTPS connection curl -k https://sysmanage.example.com:8080/health
troubleshooting.046: Check firewall rules: # Linux (iptables) iptables -L | grep 8080 # Linux (firewalld) firewall-cmd --list-ports # FreeBSD (pf) pfctl -sr | grep 8080 # Check if port is listening on server netstat -tlnp | grep 8080
troubleshooting.047: Verify DNS resolution: # Test DNS resolution nslookup sysmanage.example.com dig sysmanage.example.com # Check /etc/hosts for overrides cat /etc/hosts | grep sysmanage
troubleshooting.048: Check proxy settings: # Check environment variables echo $HTTP_PROXY echo $HTTPS_PROXY echo $NO_PROXY # Test direct connection curl --noproxy '*' https://sysmanage.example.com:8080
troubleshooting.055: Test certificate validity: # Check certificate details openssl s_client -connect sysmanage.example.com:8080 -servername sysmanage.example.com # Verify certificate expiration echo | openssl s_client -connect sysmanage.example.com:8080 2>/dev/null | openssl x509 -noout -dates # Check certificate chain curl -vvI https://sysmanage.example.com:8080
troubleshooting.056: Temporary SSL bypass (development only): # In agent configuration server: hostname: "sysmanage.example.com" port: 8080 use_https: true verify_ssl: false # ONLY for development/testing
troubleshooting.057: Update CA certificates: # Ubuntu/Debian apt update && apt install ca-certificates update-ca-certificates # RHEL/CentOS/Fedora yum update ca-certificates # or dnf update ca-certificates # FreeBSD pkg update && pkg install ca_root_nss
troubleshooting.061: Agent appears offline in server dashboard
troubleshooting.065: Check agent approval status: # Log into SysManage web interface # Navigate to Hosts > Pending Approval # Approve the agent if it appears in pending list
troubleshooting.066: Verify hostname detection: # Check system hostname hostname hostname -f # Override in agent config if needed client: hostname_override: "my-custom-hostname"
troubleshooting.067: Clear agent database: # Stop agent systemctl stop sysmanage-agent # Remove agent database rm /path/to/agent.db # Restart agent (will re-register) systemctl start sysmanage-agent
troubleshooting.072: System slowdown when agent is running
troubleshooting.075: Adjust collection intervals: # Increase collection intervals collection: intervals: system_info: 600 # 10 minutes (was 5) software: 7200 # 2 hours (was 1) hardware: 3600 # 1 hour (was 30 min) network: 600 # 10 minutes (was 5)
troubleshooting.076: Disable unnecessary collection: # Disable specific collection types collection: types: available_packages: false # Expensive operation user_accounts: false # If not needed system_metrics: false # If monitoring elsewhere
troubleshooting.077: Monitor agent resource usage: # Monitor CPU and memory usage top -p $(pgrep sysmanage-agent) htop -p $(pgrep sysmanage-agent) # Check for memory leaks ps -o pid,ppid,cmd,%mem,%cpu --sort=-%mem | head
troubleshooting.082: OOM (Out of Memory) killer terminating agent
troubleshooting.084: Enable memory monitoring: # Monitor memory usage over time while true; do echo "$(date): $(ps -o pid,ppid,cmd,%mem --sort=-%mem | grep sysmanage-agent)" sleep 60 done > memory_usage.log
troubleshooting.085: Reduce data collection scope: # Limit large data collections collection: types: available_packages: false # Large dataset hardware_info: false # If static # Reduce message queue size message_queue: max_size: 1000 cleanup_interval_minutes: 15
troubleshooting.086: Implement memory limits: # Systemd service limits [Service] MemoryMax=256M MemoryHigh=200M # Or use cgroups directly echo "256M" > /sys/fs/cgroup/memory/sysmanage-agent/memory.limit_in_bytes
troubleshooting.094: Verify package manager installation: # Check for package managers which apt apt-get yum dnf pkg pkg_add brew choco # Verify they're in PATH echo $PATH # Test package manager directly apt --version yum --version pkg --version
troubleshooting.095: Check permissions: # Verify agent user can access package manager sudo -u sysmanage apt list --installed sudo -u sysmanage yum list installed # Check sudo configuration for package management sudo -l -U sysmanage
troubleshooting.096: Configure custom package manager paths: # Custom package manager configuration package_management: custom_paths: apt: "/usr/bin/apt" brew: "/opt/homebrew/bin/brew" pkg: "/usr/local/sbin/pkg"
troubleshooting.104: Check service status and logs: # Check service status systemctl status sysmanage-agent journalctl -u sysmanage-agent --no-pager # Check for configuration errors sysmanage-agent --config-test # Try starting manually sudo -u sysmanage sysmanage-agent --verbose
troubleshooting.105: Verify file permissions: # Check binary permissions ls -la /usr/local/bin/sysmanage-agent # Check configuration permissions ls -la /etc/sysmanage-agent/ ls -la /var/log/sysmanage-agent/ # Fix permissions if needed chmod +x /usr/local/bin/sysmanage-agent chown -R sysmanage:sysmanage /etc/sysmanage-agent/
troubleshooting.106: Check dependencies: # Check required libraries ldd /usr/local/bin/sysmanage-agent # Verify Python dependencies (if applicable) pip list | grep -E "(requests|websocket|pyyaml)" # Check system dependencies python3 --version systemctl --version

authentication.005: JWT-based authentication system for secure access to SysManage APIs with token refresh and session management.
authentication.007: SysManage uses JSON Web Tokens (JWT) for authentication. All API endpoints (except authentication endpoints) require a valid JWT token in the Authorization header.
authentication.010: Receive access token and refresh token
authentication.011: Include access token in Authorization header for API requests
authentication.012: Use refresh token to get new access token when expired
authentication.015: Authenticate user credentials and receive JWT tokens for API access.
authentication.024: Exchange a valid refresh token for a new access token when the current one expires.
authentication.032: Request a password reset email for user account recovery.
authentication.038: Reset user password using a valid reset token received via email.
authentication.046: Validate if a password reset token is still valid and not expired.
authentication.048: token (string) - The reset token to validate
authentication.054: Once you receive an access token from the login endpoint, include it in the Authorization header of all API requests:
authentication.056: Access tokens expire after 1 hour (3600 seconds). When an access token expires, use the refresh token to obtain a new one without requiring the user to log in again.
authentication.058: Store tokens securely and never expose them in client-side code
authentication.059: Implement automatic token refresh before expiration
authentication.060: Use HTTPS for all authentication requests
authentication.061: Implement proper logout functionality to invalidate tokens
