monitoring.087: Diagnostic collections can be resource-intensive - schedule during low-usage periods
monitoring.088: Failed queue messages indicate potential system issues requiring attention
monitoring.089: Regular health checks help identify issues before they become critical
monitoring.090: Historical metrics data is retained for 90 days by default
monitoring.091: Alert thresholds can be customized through configuration
monitoring.092: Email configuration is required for alert notifications

packages.005: Cross-platform package management for installing, updating, and removing software packages across your infrastructure.
packages.007: The Package Management API provides unified access to various package managers across different operating systems including apt (Debian/Ubuntu), yum/dnf (RHEL/CentOS/Fedora), pkg (FreeBSD), pkg_add (OpenBSD), pkgin (NetBSD), and Homebrew (macOS).
packages.010: Get package summary statistics across all managed hosts.
packages.013: Get list of supported package managers.
packages.016: Get supported operating system versions for package management.
packages.020: Search for packages across your infrastructure.
packages.022: q (string) - Search query (package name or description)
packages.023: os (string, optional) - Filter by operating system
packages.024: version (string, optional) - Filter by OS version
packages.025: manager (string, optional) - Filter by package manager
packages.031: Get count of packages matching search criteria.
packages.034: os (string, optional) - Filter by operating system
packages.035: version (string, optional) - Filter by OS version
packages.038: Get packages available through a specific package manager.
packages.040: manager_name (string) - Package manager name (apt, yum, dnf, etc.)
packages.047: Install a package on a specific host.
packages.055: Uninstall one or more packages from a specific host using UUID-based tracking.
packages.057: host_id (string) - UUID of the target host
packages.060: package_names (array) - List of package names to uninstall
packages.061: package_manager (string, optional) - Specific package manager to use ("auto" for automatic detection)
packages.063: assume_yes (boolean, optional) - Assume yes to all prompts (default: true)
packages.064: remove_dependencies (boolean, optional) - Remove unused dependencies (default: false)
packages.070: Get package operation history for a specific host, including both installations and uninstallations.
packages.072: host_id (string) - UUID of the target host
packages.074: operation_type (string, optional) - Filter by operation type: "install", "uninstall", or "all" (default: "all")
packages.082: Get available package updates across all hosts.
packages.085: Get available updates for a specific host.
packages.087: host_id (string) - UUID of the target host
packages.090: Execute package updates on specified hosts.
packages.094: Get update execution log for a specific host.
packages.096: host_id (string) - UUID of the target host
packages.100: Get available operating system upgrades across all hosts.
packages.103: Execute operating system upgrades on specified hosts.
packages.108: Refresh package cache for a specific OS version.
packages.115: Package operations are executed asynchronously - use execution IDs to track progress
packages.116: Some operations require root/administrator privileges on target hosts
packages.117: Always test operations on non-production systems first
packages.118: Package availability depends on configured repositories
packages.119: Use UUID-based tracking for grouped operations to ensure atomicity
packages.121: ⚠️ Irreversible Operations: Package uninstallation cannot be undone through the API
packages.122: Dependency Management: Uninstalling packages may automatically remove dependencies
packages.123: System Stability: Removing system packages can cause instability or service disruption
packages.124: Backup Recommended: Create system snapshots before uninstalling critical packages
packages.125: Confirmation Required: Web interface requires explicit confirmation for uninstall operations
packages.126: Audit Trail: All uninstallation operations are logged with full details and user attribution
packages.128: OS upgrades should be scheduled during maintenance windows
packages.129: Backup systems before major updates or OS upgrades
packages.130: Monitor system resources during large package operations
packages.132: Implement proper role-based access controls for package management
packages.133: Review package sources and repositories for security
packages.134: Monitor package operations for unauthorized changes
packages.135: Use approval workflows for critical system modifications

phase8-features.005: REST endpoints introduced in Phase 8 (v2.0.0.0): access groups, registration keys, scheduled update profiles, package compliance profiles, broadcast messaging, report branding, custom report templates, and dynamic secret leases.
phase8-features.007: All endpoints below require JWT bearer authentication and are mounted under /api . Endpoints prefixed /api/v1/reports/... are served by the Pro+ reporting_engine module; OSS deployments expose only the storage / template-CRUD subset.
phase8-features.009: Hierarchical RBAC scoping for hosts and users, plus pre-shared registration keys for unattended agent enrollment.
phase8-features.011: List every access group (flat). Each row carries parent_id ; tree assembly is the client's job. Returns 200 with an array of {id, name, description, parent_id, created_at, updated_at} .
phase8-features.013: Create a new group. Body: {name, description?, parent_id?} . Returns 200 on success, 404 if parent_id doesn't exist, 400 on cycle / depth violation. Tree depth is capped at 10.
phase8-features.017: Update name / description / parent. Cycle detection re-runs against the new parent.
phase8-features.019: Delete. Children are reparented to the deleted group's parent (or to root) per the FK ON DELETE SET NULL rule.
phase8-features.021: List every registration key without secrets. The plaintext key is only ever returned at create time.
phase8-features.023: Create. Body: {name, access_group_id?, auto_approve?, max_uses?, expires_at?} . Response includes the plaintext key field exactly once — it is never recoverable.
phase8-features.025: Mark revoked. Idempotent. Existing host enrollments are not affected.
phase8-features.027: Delete the key row. (Use revoke instead if you want to keep the audit trail.)
phase8-features.036: REQUIRED / BLOCKED package definitions evaluated against host inventory, optionally with version constraints.
phase8-features.040: Create. Body: {name, description?, enabled?, constraints[]} where each constraint is {package_name, package_manager?, constraint_type, version_op?, version?} . constraint_type is REQUIRED or BLOCKED ; version_op is one of = , == , >= , <= , > , < , != , ~= .
phase8-features.044: Update. Constraint list is replaced atomically — pass the full desired list.
phase8-features.048: Evaluate the host's cached software_package inventory against the profile, persist the result in HostPackageComplianceStatus , and return it. Fast but only as fresh as the most recent inventory upload.
phase8-features.050: Ask the agent to evaluate compliance against its live local package state and report back via the WS command-result channel. Slower round-trip but always-current.
phase8-features.052: Latest compliance status for one host across all profiles. Each row contains {status, violations[], last_scan_at} .
phase8-features.054: Fan out one envelope to every connected agent (or every agent matching a tag / platform filter) in a single round-trip.
phase8-features.056: Body: {broadcast_action, message?, parameters?, tag_id?, platform?} . Returns {broadcast_id, broadcast_action, delivered_count, elapsed_ms, target_filter} . SLA: under 5 s for fleets up to 100 hosts.
phase8-features.057: Common actions: refresh_inventory (re-collect software inventory), banner + message (display a notification on the agent host).
phase8-features.059: Singleton org branding (logo + company name + header text) injected into every PDF / HTML report by the Pro+ reporting engine.
phase8-features.061: Read current branding. Logo bytes are not included; has_logo indicates whether one is configured.
phase8-features.063: Update company_name and header_text . Empty strings clear the field.
phase8-features.065: Upload (or replace) the org logo. multipart/form-data with a file field. Allowed: PNG, JPEG, SVG, WEBP. Max size 1 MB.
phase8-features.067: Stream the logo bytes. Used by the frontend preview AND embedded as a data URL by the reporting engine.
phase8-features.069: Remove the configured logo (text fields untouched).
phase8-features.071: Admin-defined column subsets / orderings layered on top of the eight base report types.
phase8-features.075: Create. Body: {name, description?, base_report_type, selected_fields[], enabled?} . selected_fields entries must be valid codes for the chosen base_report_type ; unknown codes are rejected with 400 .
phase8-features.083: List the eight base report types ( registered-hosts , hosts-with-tags , users-list , firewall-status , antivirus-opensource , antivirus-commercial , user-rbac , audit-log ) that templates can be built on.
phase8-features.085: Catalog of available {code, label} pairs for the base type. The frontend uses this to populate the field-picker UI.
phase8-features.086: Applying a template at render time: add a ?template_id=<uuid> query param to GET /api/v1/reports/view/{report_type} or GET /api/v1/reports/generate/{report_type} . Unknown / disabled templates fall back to the default column set.
phase8-features.088: Short-lived OpenBAO/Vault-backed credentials with automatic expiry. The plaintext value is returned exactly once at issue time and never persisted in the SysManage DB.
phase8-features.090: Issue a new lease. Body: {name, kind, backend_role, ttl_seconds, note?} . kind is token , database , or ssh ; ttl_seconds is clamped to [60, 86400] . Response: {lease, secret} — surface secret to the operator once and discard.
phase8-features.096: Revoke immediately and delete from OpenBAO. Idempotent.
phase8-features.098: Sweeper hook — transitions ACTIVE rows whose expires_at has passed into EXPIRED. Safe to call repeatedly.
phase8-features.100: Catalog of supported lease kinds and the TTL range.
phase8-features.102: Every state-changing endpoint above writes one or more rows to the audit_log table via AuditService.log , including the operator's user ID, the entity touched, and the action result. Use GET /api/audit-log/list to query, or GET /api/audit-log/export?fmt=csv to stream a CSV (Phase 8.4).
phase8-features.104: Every operator-visible response string returned by the endpoints above is wrapped with the server's gettext shim and translated into the 14 supported locales: ar , de , en , es , fr , hi , it , ja , ko , nl , pt , ru , zh_CN , zh_TW .

reports.005: Generate and access comprehensive system reports, inventory data, and analytics through programmatic interfaces.
reports.007: The Reports API provides access to various system reports including host inventories, package status, security summaries, and performance analytics. Reports can be generated in multiple formats and scheduled for regular delivery.
reports.010: All endpoints require valid JWT authentication with appropriate permissions.
reports.013: Host Inventory: Complete hardware and software inventory
reports.014: Package Status: Installed packages and available updates
reports.015: System Performance: Performance metrics and trends
reports.016: Capacity Planning: Resource utilization and growth projections
reports.018: Vulnerability Assessment: Security vulnerabilities and patches
reports.019: Compliance Status: Security compliance and policy adherence
reports.020: User Activity: User access patterns and administrative actions
reports.021: Security Events: Security incidents and alerts
reports.023: System Health: Overall system status and uptime
reports.024: Maintenance Summary: Maintenance activities and schedules
reports.025: Backup Status: Backup completion and integrity reports
reports.026: Change Log: System changes and modifications
reports.031: Retrieve a list of all available report types and their descriptions.
reports.037: Generate a new report with specified parameters and format.
reports.043: Check the generation status of a specific report.
reports.052: Returns the report file with appropriate Content-Type header based on format.
reports.056: Get real-time host inventory data in JSON format.
reports.058: host_ids - Comma-separated list of host UUIDs
reports.061: format - Response format: json, csv (default: json)
reports.067: Get comprehensive package status across all hosts.
reports.072: security_only - Show only security updates (default: false)
reports.078: Get security vulnerability assessment across all hosts.
reports.080: severity - Filter by severity: critical, high, medium, low
reports.088: Get system performance summary and trends.
reports.092: metrics - Specific metrics: cpu, memory, disk, network
reports.098: Manage scheduled report generation and delivery.
reports.109: JSON: Structured data for API consumption
reports.110: CSV: Comma-separated values for spreadsheet import
reports.111: Excel: Microsoft Excel format with multiple sheets
reports.112: PDF: Formatted reports for presentation and printing
reports.113: HTML: Web-viewable reports with interactive elements
reports.119: Automatic table of contents and page numbering
reports.121: Multiple worksheets for different data types
reports.126: Raw data export for analysis tools

third-party-repositories.005: Centralized management of third-party package repositories across multiple operating systems and platforms.
third-party-repositories.007: The Third-Party Repositories API provides centralized management of package repositories across Debian, Ubuntu, RHEL-based, SUSE-based, FreeBSD, NetBSD, Windows, and macOS systems. Repositories are automatically distributed to matching hosts based on OS name and version.
third-party-repositories.009: Automatic OS Detection: Repositories are automatically sent to all hosts matching the specified OS and version
third-party-repositories.010: GPG Key Management: Automatic GPG key import and verification for Linux systems
third-party-repositories.011: Cross-Platform Support: Support for all major package management systems
third-party-repositories.012: User Attribution: Track which administrator added each repository
third-party-repositories.015: Add a new third-party repository configuration. The repository will be automatically distributed to all hosts matching the specified OS name and version.
third-party-repositories.033: Complete repository configuration in OS-specific format
third-party-repositories.037: URL to GPG signing key for verification (Linux systems)
third-party-repositories.041: GPG key content directly (alternative to URL)
third-party-repositories.048: Get details of a specific repository configuration.
third-party-repositories.051: Remove a repository configuration. This will remove the repository from all affected hosts and update their package cache.
third-party-repositories.055: Add Docker Repository to Ubuntu Hosts
third-party-repositories.056: Add EPEL Repository to RHEL Hosts
third-party-repositories.068: HTTPS Only: Always use HTTPS repository URLs to prevent man-in-the-middle attacks
third-party-repositories.069: GPG Keys: Provide GPG keys for all Linux repositories to ensure package verification
third-party-repositories.070: Verify Sources: Only add repositories from trusted, verified sources
third-party-repositories.071: Admin Only: Repository management requires administrative privileges
third-party-repositories.073: Test First: Test repository configurations on non-production systems before adding
third-party-repositories.074: Document Purpose: Maintain external documentation of why each repository was added
third-party-repositories.075: Monitor Application: Check affected_hosts in API responses to verify successful distribution
third-party-repositories.076: Version Control: Track API calls in version control or configuration management systems

users.005: Complete user account management, role-based access control, and administrative functions through programmatic interfaces.
users.007: The User Management API provides comprehensive functionality for managing user accounts, roles, permissions, and access controls. It supports role-based access control (RBAC), multi-factor authentication (MFA), and detailed user activity tracking.
users.010: All endpoints require valid JWT authentication with administrative privileges for most operations.
users.013: Administrator: Full system access and user management privileges
users.014: Operator: Host management and monitoring capabilities
users.015: Viewer: Read-only access to system information
users.016: Guest: Limited access to basic system status
users.018: Host Management: Create, modify, delete hosts and agents
users.019: Package Management: Install, update, remove packages
users.020: User Administration: Manage user accounts and permissions
users.021: System Configuration: Modify system settings and configuration
users.022: Monitoring Access: View metrics, alerts, and system health
users.023: Report Generation: Create and access system reports
users.025: Local Authentication: Username and password stored locally
users.026: LDAP Integration: Active Directory and LDAP authentication
users.028: Multi-Factor Authentication: TOTP, SMS, and hardware token support
users.033: Retrieve a list of all users with their basic information and roles.
users.036: limit - Number of users per page (default: 50, max: 100)
users.039: search - Search by username, email, or full name
users.045: Retrieve detailed information about a specific user.
users.051: Create a new user account with specified role and permissions.
users.057: Update user information, role, permissions, or status.
users.063: Delete a user account. This action is irreversible.
users.065: transfer_ownership - Transfer owned resources to another user
users.066: backup_data - Create backup of user data before deletion
users.072: Change a user's password. Users can change their own password, administrators can change any password.
users.078: Initialize MFA setup for a user account.
users.084: Complete MFA setup by verifying the authentication code.
users.090: Retrieve detailed permissions for a specific user.
users.096: Update specific permissions for a user, overriding role defaults.
users.102: Retrieve user activity log including logins, actions, and system changes.
users.104: start_date - Start date for activity log (ISO 8601)
users.105: end_date - End date for activity log (ISO 8601)
users.107: limit - Number of entries to return (default: 100)
users.113: Retrieve the current authenticated user's profile information.
users.119: Update the current user's preferences and settings.
users.126: Retrieve all available user roles and their permissions.
users.132: Create a custom role with specific permissions and restrictions.

websockets.005: Real-time bidirectional communication for live updates, command execution, and status monitoring.
websockets.007: SysManage uses WebSockets for real-time communication between the server, web interface, and agents. This enables live status updates, real-time command execution feedback, and instant notifications of system events.
websockets.009: Real-time Updates - Live host status, package installation progress, and system metrics
websockets.010: Bidirectional Communication - Send commands and receive responses in real-time
websockets.011: Event Streaming - Subscribe to specific events like host status changes
websockets.012: Authentication - JWT-based authentication for secure connections
websockets.013: Automatic Reconnection - Built-in reconnection logic for network interruptions
websockets.017: WebSocket connections require authentication via JWT token passed as a query parameter:
websockets.020: All WebSocket messages use JSON format with a standardized structure:
websockets.027: response - Response to a command or request
websockets.031: Receive real-time updates about host status changes.
websockets.035: Monitor package installation, update, and removal operations.
websockets.039: Real-time command execution feedback and results.
websockets.048: Monitor user login/logout and activity events.
websockets.060: Implement automatic reconnection with exponential backoff
websockets.062: Store JWT tokens securely and refresh before expiration
websockets.063: Monitor connection health with periodic ping/pong messages
websockets.066: Use message IDs for request/response correlation
websockets.067: Implement message queuing for offline scenarios
websockets.070: Subscribe only to channels you need
websockets.071: Use filters to reduce message volume
websockets.072: Unsubscribe from channels when no longer needed
websockets.073: Implement client-side message throttling if necessary
websockets.075: Never expose JWT tokens in client-side logs
websockets.076: Validate all incoming data on the client side

airgap.005: Endpoint reference for moving package and repository data into and out of disconnected (air-gapped) environments. Covers the collector that builds signed ISOs, the bundle builder, repository mirroring, removable-device ingest, and the trusted-key keyrings that establish trust between a collector and a repository. All endpoints require Bearer-token auth except the explicitly token-authenticated download streams noted below.
airgap.007: Recurring collection jobs that build signed ISOs on a cron-style cadence. The tick endpoint is the driver hook an external scheduler calls to fire any schedules that are due.
airgap.009: POST /api/v1/airgap/collector/schedules — create a new collection schedule
airgap.013: POST /api/v1/airgap/collector/schedules/tick — driver hook; fire all due schedules
airgap.015: One-shot collection runs. A run produces one or more disc ISOs and a set of signed manifests. ISOs can be streamed with header auth, or downloaded natively via a short-lived minted token so a browser or native tool can pull the stream without a Bearer header.
airgap.016: POST /api/v1/airgap/collector/runs — create a one-shot collection run
airgap.019: GET /api/v1/airgap/collector/runs/{run_id}/discs — list disc ISOs for multi-disc runs
airgap.021: POST /api/v1/airgap/collector/runs/{run_id}/iso-token — mint a token for native ISO download
airgap.023: DELETE /api/v1/airgap/collector/runs/{run_id} — delete a run and its data
airgap.024: GET /api/v1/airgap/collector/runs/{run_id}/manifests — list signed manifests for a run
airgap.025: GET /api/v1/airgap/collector/manifests/{manifest_id}/download — stream the signed ISO referenced by a manifest
airgap.027: The bundle builder produces a self-contained ISO for installing or updating a fully disconnected server. Build jobs run in Docker and return a job ID; resource and Docker readiness can be checked before kicking off a build. Like collector runs, the ISO can be streamed with header auth or via a minted download token.
airgap.028: GET /api/airgap-bundles/docker-status — check Docker installation and readiness
airgap.029: GET /api/airgap-bundles/resource-status — check RAM, swap, and disk for a build
airgap.030: POST /api/airgap-bundles — trigger a new bundle build (returns a job ID)
airgap.034: POST /api/airgap-bundles/{bundle_id}/download-token — mint a token for native ISO download
airgap.036: DELETE /api/airgap-bundles/{bundle_id} — remove the bundle and its on-disk artifacts
airgap.038: On the repository (disconnected) side, ISOs arrive on removable media. These endpoints enumerate candidate drives, persist the chosen import device, probe its readiness, and queue ingest of its contents into the repository.
airgap.040: PUT /api/v1/airgap/import-device — persist the chosen import drive (or clear it)
airgap.041: GET /api/v1/airgap/import-device/status — probe device readiness for import
airgap.042: POST /api/v1/airgap/repository/ingest-device — queue ingest from the selected drive
airgap.045: Read-only views of repository state: per-host compliance buckets that classify packages by transfer/apply status, the list of mirrored repositories with their freshness, and a global freshness label for the air-gapped mirror set.
airgap.047: GET /api/v1/airgap/repository/repositories — list air-gap repositories with freshness
airgap.048: GET /api/v1/airgap/repository/freshness — global air-gap mirror freshness label
airgap.050: Trust between a collector and a repository is established by exchanging public keys. The repository side imports collector public keys into its trusted keyring; signed ISOs are only accepted from trusted collectors.
airgap.051: GET /api/v1/airgap/collector-key — get this server's collector public key and fingerprint
airgap.053: POST /api/v1/airgap/trusted-collectors — import a collector public key
airgap.054: DELETE /api/v1/airgap/trusted-collectors/{name} — remove a trusted collector key
airgap.056: Local mirrors of upstream package repositories, with snapshot/restore and per-platform configuration. The tick endpoint is the cron driver hook that fires any mirrors due for sync; the setup-status and setup-install endpoints detect and install the mirror tooling on a target host. Endpoints carry the /api prefix directly rather than a versioned path.
airgap.065: GET /api/mirror-repositories/{mirror_id}/snapshots — list snapshots for a mirror
airgap.066: POST /api/mirror-repositories/tick — cron driver hook; fire due syncs
airgap.071: GET /api/mirror-platform-configs / POST /api/mirror-platform-configs — platform config list / upsert
airgap.072: GET /api/mirror-platform-configs/{cfg_id} / PUT / DELETE — platform config read / update / delete
airgap.073: GET /api/mirror-known-versions — list known OS versions (optionally by platform)
airgap.074: GET /api/host-defaults/mirrors — list default mirror assignments per (platform, version, os_family)
airgap.075: PUT /api/host-defaults/mirrors/{platform}/{version_key}/{os_family} — assign / unassign a default mirror

control-plane.005: Endpoint reference for the multi-tenancy control plane: the surface that manages tenants, their database placement and provisioning, enrollment tokens that bind agents to a tenant, and the users and grants that authorize access. In the open-source core this surface is a thin stub; the working logic is a Professional+ feature (the licensed multitenancy engine, MULTITENANT_SAAS tier). The routes documented below are the engine surface, mounted under /api/control-plane when multi-tenancy is enabled and the engine is loaded. Every endpoint is gated by a JWT bearer token at the router level.
control-plane.007: The OSS core ships only a stub router. When multi-tenancy is configured ( multitenancy.enabled ) but the licensed engine is not loaded, every path under the prefix is served by a single catch-all that returns HTTP 501 Not Implemented, indicating the licensed engine is required.
control-plane.008: ANY /api/control-plane/{path} — catch-all stub; returns 501 when the licensed multitenancy engine is not loaded
control-plane.009: When the engine is loaded it replaces this stub with the full router below. The route table is defined inside the compiled engine module; the paths shown here reflect that surface.
control-plane.011: A single read of overall control-plane state: tenant count, whether multi-tenancy is enabled, and whether self-service provisioning is turned on.
control-plane.012: GET /api/control-plane/status — control-plane status (tenant count, feature flags)
control-plane.014: Tenant lifecycle. Listing accepts a status filter; deletion requires a confirmation slug and an explicit flag to drop the underlying database.
control-plane.018: DELETE /api/control-plane/tenants/{tenant_id} — delete a tenant (confirmation slug + drop_database flag)
control-plane.020: Placement records where a tenant's database lives (host, port, dbname, region, tier, OpenBAO role). Provisioning then stands up that database and its OpenBAO identity. Manual provisioning requires placement to be set first; auto-provision is the self-service path that creates placement and provisions in one step.
control-plane.021: GET /api/control-plane/tenants/{tenant_id}/placement / PUT — read or upsert tenant database placement
control-plane.026: Tenant-scoped tokens that bind an enrolling agent to a specific tenant. Created and revoked per tenant; consumed at agent registration.
control-plane.027: GET /api/control-plane/tenants/{tenant_id}/enrollment-tokens — list a tenant's enrollment tokens
control-plane.031: Per-tenant allowlist of email domains, used to scope which users may belong to a tenant.
control-plane.033: POST /api/control-plane/tenants/{tenant_id}/email-domains — add an allowed email domain
control-plane.036: Control-plane users and the grants that bind a user to a tenant with a role. User and grant listings accept email and user_id filters respectively.
control-plane.040: POST /api/control-plane/grants — create a user-tenant grant (role assignment)

federation.005: Endpoint reference for federating multiple SysManage servers. The open-source core ships the identity surface (key exchange, certificate pinning, role selection) so peers can establish trust. The substantive coordinator and site surfaces are Professional+ features implemented in the licensed federation engines; when those engines are not loaded the routes are mounted as stubs that report they are unlicensed. All coordinator/site endpoints require Bearer-token auth except enrollment completion and the site-to-coordinator ingest endpoints, which authenticate with a site token instead.
federation.007: The identity surface is thin and lives in the OSS core. It handles key exchange between peers, exposes the federation TLS certificate for pinning (unauthenticated, since it is public), and lets an operator select this server's federation role. The valid roles are none , coordinator , and site .
federation.008: GET /api/v1/federation/identity-key — this server's federation public identity key and fingerprint
federation.009: GET /api/v1/federation/trusted-peers — list trusted federation peer keys
federation.010: POST /api/v1/federation/trusted-peers — import a peer's public key into the trusted keyring
federation.011: DELETE /api/v1/federation/trusted-peers/{name} — remove a trusted peer key
federation.012: GET /api/v1/federation/tls-cert — this server's federation TLS certificate (unauthenticated, public)
federation.013: GET /api/v1/federation-role / PUT /api/v1/federation-role — read or set the federation role
federation.015: The coordinator enrolls and manages the sites it federates. Enrollment completion is token-authenticated so a new site can finish the handshake before it holds operator credentials.
federation.016: GET /api/v1/federation/sites / POST /api/v1/federation/sites — list or enroll federation sites
federation.018: GET /api/v1/federation/sites/{site_id} / PATCH — get or update a site
federation.019: POST /api/v1/federation/sites/{site_id}/suspend / resume — suspend or resume a site
federation.023: POST /api/v1/federation/sites/{site_id}/repush-policies — repush policies to a site
federation.025: Cross-site visibility. The host directory searches hosts across all federated sites; the rollups aggregate host, compliance, and vulnerability data into coordinator-level dashboards and reports.
federation.026: GET /api/v1/federation/hosts — search hosts across federation sites
federation.027: GET /api/v1/federation/hosts/{host_id} — host details from the federated directory
federation.034: Policies are authored at the coordinator, assigned to sites, and pushed out. Commands are dispatched to sites and tracked centrally.
federation.035: GET /api/v1/federation/policies / POST — list or create policies
federation.036: GET /api/v1/federation/policies/{policy_id} / PATCH / DELETE — get, update, or deactivate a policy
federation.037: POST /api/v1/federation/policies/{policy_id}/assign — assign a policy to sites
federation.038: POST /api/v1/federation/policies/{policy_id}/push — push a policy to enrolled sites
federation.039: POST /api/v1/federation/commands/dispatch — dispatch a command to sites

agent-deployment-protocol.005: Server-to-agent message protocol for the three open-source deployment primitives every Pro+ engine builds on: deploy_files , execute_command_sequence , and service_control .
agent-deployment-protocol.007: The architectural decision made in Phase 3 split the deployment pipeline in two:
agent-deployment-protocol.008: Server side (Pro+ Cython modules) generates fully-baked configuration — firewall rule sets, AV deployment plans, OTEL collector configs, VM definitions. This is where the proprietary value lives.
agent-deployment-protocol.009: Agent side (open-source) just deploys files, runs commands, and controls services. It has no knowledge of what any particular file means.
agent-deployment-protocol.010: The three messages documented here are the entire interface between the two halves. They are deliberately generic — adding a new Pro+ feature does not require any agent code change.
agent-deployment-protocol.011: Agent implementation lives in sysmanage-agent/src/sysmanage_agent/operations/generic_deployment.py (deploy and command sequencing) and sysmanage-agent/src/sysmanage_agent/core/agent_utils.py (service control). All three return a structured result dict; none of them ever raise out of the handler.
agent-deployment-protocol.013: Atomic file deployment. Writes one or more files to the local filesystem using the temp-file-plus-rename pattern, with optional SHA-256 verification and backup/rollback support.
agent-deployment-protocol.023: Absolute destination path. Parent dirs are created (mode 0755) if missing.
agent-deployment-protocol.027: Full file contents. The agent appends a trailing newline if the content does not end in one (POSIX text-file convention).
agent-deployment-protocol.043: If supplied, the agent verifies BOTH the pre-write content (to catch transport corruption) AND the on-disk file post-rename (to catch filesystem-level mutation). Mismatch triggers rollback if a backup exists, otherwise removes the bad file.
agent-deployment-protocol.047: Default false . When true, an existing target is copied to <path>.sysmanage.bak before the new content is written. The backup is left in place on success (admin can clean up); on post-write hash mismatch the backup is restored.
agent-deployment-protocol.049: success is true if at least one file was deployed. Per-file outcomes are in deployed_files (each carries its own success ) and errors .
agent-deployment-protocol.051: Run a sequence of steps in order. Stops at the first failed step. A "step" is a discriminated union of three types: shell (run a command), deploy_file (one-off file deployment, same semantics as deploy_files ), and wait_condition (poll a command's output until it matches a regex).
agent-deployment-protocol.061: Runs via subprocess. Step succeeds iff exit code is 0.
agent-deployment-protocol.064: same optional fields as in deploy_files
agent-deployment-protocol.065: Single-file atomic deploy with the same SHA-256 + backup semantics.
agent-deployment-protocol.069: Re-runs command on the interval until its stdout matches the match regex, or times out. Useful for waiting on services to become ready.
agent-deployment-protocol.071: If progress_message_type is supplied, the agent emits one progress message per step before executing it. The message envelope uses the supplied type so callers can route progress without colliding with other in-flight commands. Default type is command_sequence_progress .
agent-deployment-protocol.073: If a step fails, the sequence stops; completed_steps reflects the count of successful steps and errors contains the failure message. The remaining steps are not attempted.
agent-deployment-protocol.075: Start, stop, restart, enable, or disable one or more services. The agent picks the appropriate service manager based on the host platform — systemctl on most modern Linux distros, rc-service + rc-update on Alpine/Gentoo (OpenRC), launchctl on macOS, or sc.exe on Windows. The same message works across distros without the caller knowing which manager runs the host.
agent-deployment-protocol.081: Bring the service up now. No-op if already running.
agent-deployment-protocol.083: Take the service down now. No-op if already stopped.
agent-deployment-protocol.087: Mark the service to start on boot. Does NOT start it now.
agent-deployment-protocol.089: Mark the service to NOT start on boot. Does NOT stop it now.
agent-deployment-protocol.091: The handler refuses to operate when the agent is not running in privileged mode ( is_running_privileged() returns false). In unprivileged mode the response is {"success": false, "error": "Service control requires privileged mode"} — the caller is expected to surface that to the operator and request elevated re-deployment.
agent-deployment-protocol.093: success is true iff EVERY service in the list completed successfully. Per-service outcomes are in results .
agent-deployment-protocol.095: These three messages are the open-source contract every Pro+ release ships against. Backwards-incompatible changes to any of the request or response shapes documented above require:
agent-deployment-protocol.096: A new command_type string (e.g., deploy_files_v2 ) — never an in-place schema change.
agent-deployment-protocol.097: A migration path documented in the agent release notes.
agent-deployment-protocol.098: A version-detection probe so older Pro+ engines can fall back to the previous wire format.
agent-deployment-protocol.099: Adding optional new fields to existing messages is safe and does not require versioning, provided the agent treats unknown fields as no-ops.
agent-deployment-protocol.103: Pro+ callers: av_management_engine , firewall_orchestration_engine , and container_engine all generate plans that ultimately call these three messages.

communication-protocols.005: Advanced communication infrastructure enabling secure, real-time, and reliable data exchange
communication-protocols.011: SysManage implements a multi-layer communication architecture designed for reliability, security, and performance.
communication-protocols.012: Mutual TLS Encryption: All communications are secured using TLS 1.3 with mutual authentication. Both server and agents present X.509 certificates for verification, ensuring secure identity verification and encrypted data transmission.
communication-protocols.013: Dual Protocol Support: WebSocket connections for real-time bidirectional communication and HTTP/REST for request-response operations. WebSocket provides persistent connections for immediate command execution and status updates.
communication-protocols.014: Structured Messaging: JSON-based message format with typed schemas, message IDs, timestamps, and operation metadata. Supports command messages, response messages, status updates, and error notifications.
communication-protocols.015: Delivery Guarantees: Persistent message queuing with automatic retry logic, exponential backoff, and dead letter queues. Connection health monitoring with automatic reconnection and message persistence during disconnections.
communication-protocols.019: Optimized for low latency with connection pooling, efficient serialization, and minimal overhead protocols.
communication-protocols.020: Intelligent reconnection logic with exponential backoff, connection health monitoring, and seamless failover.
communication-protocols.021: Persistent message queuing ensures delivery even during network interruptions or agent disconnections.
communication-protocols.022: Efficient message routing to specific hosts, host groups, or broadcast scenarios with delivery confirmation.
communication-protocols.023: Live connection status, message delivery tracking, and performance metrics with alerting capabilities.
communication-protocols.024: Detailed audit logs for all communications, message tracking, and debugging with retention policies.

data-synchronization.005: Data synchronization strategies, conflict resolution, and consistency models in SysManage distributed systems.
data-synchronization.007: SysManage implements a sophisticated data synchronization system to maintain consistency across distributed components while handling network partitions, agent disconnections, and concurrent updates gracefully.
data-synchronization.009: Eventually Consistent: System converges to consistency over time
data-synchronization.011: Partition Tolerant: Continues operation during network issues
data-synchronization.012: Agent Autonomy: Agents can operate independently when disconnected
data-synchronization.017: SysManage uses eventual consistency with vector clocks for tracking causality:
data-synchronization.020: For critical operations like certificate management and user authentication, SysManage uses strong consistency with two-phase commit:
data-synchronization.024: SysManage detects conflicts using a combination of techniques:
data-synchronization.041: Agents push changes to the server immediately when connected:
data-synchronization.044: Periodic pull synchronization to catch missed updates:
data-synchronization.047: For efficient bulk synchronization and verification:
data-synchronization.051: Agents maintain local state and operation queues during disconnection:
data-synchronization.054: When agents reconnect, potential conflicts must be identified and resolved:
data-synchronization.058: Change Batching: Group related changes for efficient transmission
data-synchronization.059: Delta Compression: Only send differences between versions
data-synchronization.060: Data Compression: GZIP compression for large payloads
data-synchronization.061: Deduplication: Remove duplicate operations in queue
data-synchronization.065: Change Log Caching: Cache recent changes for faster conflict detection
data-synchronization.066: Hash Caching: Cache Merkle tree hashes for quick comparison
data-synchronization.067: Network Response Caching: Cache immutable data locally
data-synchronization.068: Computation Memoization: Cache expensive operations like conflict resolution
data-synchronization.090: Idempotency: Design operations to be safely retryable
data-synchronization.091: Immutability: Use immutable data structures where possible
data-synchronization.092: Event Sourcing: Store events rather than just current state
data-synchronization.093: Graceful Degradation: System should work even with sync failures
data-synchronization.095: Monitor Sync Health: Set up alerts for sync failures and delays
data-synchronization.096: Conflict Analysis: Regularly analyze conflict patterns
data-synchronization.097: Capacity Planning: Monitor queue sizes and growth trends
data-synchronization.098: Testing: Test sync behavior under various failure scenarios

database-design.005: Enterprise-grade PostgreSQL architecture with advanced indexing, relationships, and performance optimization

database-design.012: SysManage implements a comprehensive relational schema designed for scalability, performance, and data integrity.
database-design.013: Authentication & Authorization: Complete user management system with secure password hashing, JWT tokens, role-based access control, user groups, and profile management including image uploads and account locking mechanisms.
database-design.014: Infrastructure Tracking: Comprehensive host information including hardware specifications, operating system details, network interfaces, storage devices, certificate management, and real-time status monitoring.
database-design.015: Software Inventory: Complete package tracking system including installed packages, available packages, update operations, dependency management, and package operation history with detailed execution logs.
database-design.016: Automation Framework: Script repository with version control, execution tracking, parameter management, result storage, and comprehensive audit trails for all script operations.
database-design.017: Communication Infrastructure: Persistent message queuing system with delivery tracking, retry logic, expiration handling, and comprehensive message lifecycle management.
database-design.018: Flexible Categorization: Dynamic tagging system for hosts and resources with hierarchical organization, color coding, and advanced filtering capabilities.

database-design.033: Package operation history including installs, updates, removals with execution status and logs.

database-schema.005: Comprehensive documentation of SysManage database design, tables, relationships, and data management patterns.
database-schema.007: SysManage uses PostgreSQL as its primary database, designed for high performance, data integrity, and scalability. The schema is organized into logical domains with clear separation of concerns and well-defined relationships.
database-schema.035: agents → certificates: Each agent has one certificate for mTLS authentication
database-schema.036: agents → agent_groups: Agents can be organized into hierarchical groups
database-schema.037: hosts → agents: One-to-one relationship between host inventory and agent
database-schema.038: tasks → agents: Tasks are executed on specific agents
database-schema.039: tasks → users: Tasks track who created and assigned them
database-schema.040: user_roles → users/roles: Many-to-many relationship for RBAC
database-schema.042: ON DELETE CASCADE: When an agent is deleted, all related hosts, packages, services, and metrics are automatically removed
database-schema.043: ON DELETE SET NULL: When a user is deleted, their tasks are orphaned but preserved for audit purposes
database-schema.044: ON DELETE RESTRICT: Roles cannot be deleted if they're assigned to users
database-schema.047: PostgreSQL's JSONB type is extensively used for flexible, schema-free data:
database-schema.049: task.parameters: Dynamic task configuration and input parameters
database-schema.050: host.*_info: Hardware and system information that varies by platform
database-schema.051: roles.permissions: Flexible permission definitions with resource-level granularity
database-schema.052: metrics.labels: Prometheus-style metric labels for dimensional data
database-schema.055: All timestamps use TIMESTAMP WITH TIME ZONE for global consistency:
database-schema.056: created_at/updated_at: Standard audit fields on all major tables
database-schema.062: Critical status fields use CHECK constraints for data integrity:
database-schema.063: Agent Status: online, offline, maintenance, error
database-schema.064: Task Status: pending, running, completed, failed, cancelled, timeout
database-schema.066: Alert Severity: critical, high, medium, low, info
database-schema.067: Package Status: installed, available, upgradeable, broken
database-schema.071: Unique constraints: Natural keys (hostname, email, serial_number)
database-schema.072: Foreign key indexes: All foreign key columns for join performance
database-schema.073: Status indexes: Critical for filtering active/online entities
database-schema.074: Timestamp indexes: Essential for time-based queries and cleanup
database-schema.076: GIN indexes: JSONB columns for fast JSON queries
database-schema.077: Partial indexes: Active records only (WHERE is_active = true)
database-schema.078: Composite indexes: Multi-column queries (agent_id, status, timestamp)
database-schema.081: High-volume tables are partitioned by timestamp:
database-schema.082: metrics: Monthly partitions with automatic rotation
database-schema.087: Agent Dashboard: Join agents, hosts, latest metrics
database-schema.088: Package Inventory: Aggregate packages by name/version across agents
database-schema.089: Task Monitoring: Active tasks with progress and ETA
database-schema.090: Alert Summary: Open alerts grouped by severity and type
database-schema.093: SysManage uses Alembic (SQLAlchemy's migration tool) for database schema management:
database-schema.097: Incremental Changes: Small, focused migrations for easier rollback
database-schema.098: Data Preservation: Always include data migration scripts when needed
database-schema.099: Index Management: Add indexes concurrently to avoid locking
database-schema.100: Constraint Validation: Use NOT VALID constraints for large table modifications
database-schema.101: Testing: Test migrations on production-like data sets
database-schema.112: Full Backup: Daily pg_dump with compression
database-schema.114: Point-in-Time: Recovery capability for last 30 days
database-schema.115: Logical: Schema and reference data exports

database-schema.118: User Accounts & Roles: Access control and permissions
database-schema.119: Agent Configuration: Agent registration and grouping
database-schema.125: Query Performance: Slow query log analysis

design-principles.005: Core design principles, architectural patterns, and engineering decisions that guide SysManage development.
design-principles.007: SysManage is built on a foundation of proven software engineering principles that prioritize security, scalability, maintainability, and operational excellence. Every architectural decision is guided by real-world operational requirements and enterprise-grade reliability standards.
design-principles.009: Security is not an afterthought but a fundamental design constraint that influences every component and interaction.
design-principles.011: Architecture anticipates growth from small deployments to enterprise-scale, with clear scaling paths at every layer.
design-principles.013: Every feature considers the operational burden on administrators, prioritizing automation and self-healing capabilities.
design-principles.015: Cross-platform design ensures consistent functionality across diverse operating systems and environments.
design-principles.018: SysManage operates under the assumption that no component, network, or user can be inherently trusted.
design-principles.020: Mutual TLS (mTLS): Every agent-server communication is authenticated and encrypted using client certificates
design-principles.021: Certificate Rotation: Automatic certificate renewal with configurable expiration periods
design-principles.022: Least Privilege: Role-based access control with granular permissions and time-limited tokens
design-principles.023: Defense in Depth: Multiple security layers from network to application to data
design-principles.026: Performance Impact: Encryption overhead vs. security assurance
design-principles.027: Complexity: Certificate management complexity vs. authentication strength
design-principles.028: Usability: Security barriers vs. ease of deployment
design-principles.030: System components communicate through events and message queues, enabling loose coupling and asynchronous processing.
design-principles.033: Scalability: Independent scaling of event producers and consumers
design-principles.034: Resilience: Automatic retry and dead letter handling for failed events
design-principles.035: Observability: Complete audit trail of all system events
design-principles.036: Flexibility: Easy addition of new event handlers without code changes
design-principles.051: Configuration and state changes are treated as immutable events with full audit trails and rollback capabilities.
design-principles.053: Configuration as Code: All configuration stored in version-controlled files
design-principles.054: Audit Trails: Every change recorded with who, what, when, and why
design-principles.055: Rollback Capability: Point-in-time recovery for configuration and data
design-principles.056: Validation Gates: Automated testing before configuration deployment
design-principles.059: Every feature is accessible through well-designed APIs before UI implementation, ensuring programmatic access and integration capabilities.
design-principles.061: RESTful Principles: Consistent resource-oriented design with standard HTTP methods
design-principles.062: OpenAPI Specification: Machine-readable API documentation with code generation
design-principles.063: Versioning Strategy: Backward-compatible evolution with deprecation policies
design-principles.064: Error Handling: Standardized error responses with actionable information
design-principles.068: Deprecation Process: 6-month notice period for breaking changes
design-principles.069: Backward Compatibility: Support for N-1 API versions
design-principles.070: Feature Flags: Gradual rollout of new API features
design-principles.072: Every component produces structured logs, metrics, and traces to enable comprehensive monitoring and debugging.
design-principles.092: Separation of read and write operations for optimized performance and scalability.
design-principles.095: Performance: Optimized read and write models
design-principles.096: Scalability: Independent scaling of read/write workloads
design-principles.097: Flexibility: Multiple read models for different use cases
design-principles.098: Event Sourcing: Complete audit trail and replay capability
design-principles.100: Agent Inventory: Write to normalized schema, read from denormalized views
design-principles.101: Task Management: Commands for task operations, optimized queries for dashboards
design-principles.102: Metrics Collection: High-throughput writes, aggregated reads
design-principles.104: Automatic failure detection and recovery for external dependencies and agent communication.
design-principles.107: Agent Communication: Prevent cascading failures when agents are unreachable
design-principles.108: Database Connections: Handle database unavailability gracefully
design-principles.109: External APIs: Protect against third-party service failures
design-principles.110: Package Repositories: Fail fast when repositories are unavailable
design-principles.112: Manage complex workflows across multiple agents and services with compensation logic.
design-principles.115: Rollback: Undo changes in reverse order
design-principles.116: Retry: Automatic retry with exponential backoff
design-principles.117: Manual Intervention: Flag for administrator attention
design-principles.118: Partial Success: Continue with successful subset
design-principles.132: Alternative Considered: Go (chosen Python for rapid development and library ecosystem)
design-principles.144: Alternative Considered: Vue.js (chosen React for ecosystem maturity)
design-principles.156: Alternative Considered: MongoDB (chosen PostgreSQL for consistency and ACID guarantees)
design-principles.173: Reduced server load compared to HTTP polling
design-principles.177: Fallback to HTTP for unreliable connections
design-principles.182: Ability to replay and debug issues
design-principles.186: Applied only to critical operations (task execution, configuration changes, security events) to avoid complexity and storage overhead for routine data.
design-principles.194: Use for truly dynamic data only
design-principles.195: Define clear schemas in application code
design-principles.197: Validate JSON structure at application layer
design-principles.208: Caching layers for frequently accessed data
design-principles.239: Encryption at rest and in transit
design-principles.289: Extensible plugin system for custom functionality without core modifications.
design-principles.291: Extensive configuration options to adapt behavior without code changes.
design-principles.293: Structured approach to API evolution maintaining backward compatibility.
design-principles.295: Optional components that can be enabled/disabled based on requirements.
design-principles.297: To understand how these principles are implemented:

load-balancing.005: Load balancer configuration, health checks, session affinity, and scaling patterns for SysManage distributed deployments.
load-balancing.007: SysManage is designed to support horizontal scaling through load balancing. The architecture enables multiple server instances to share the load while maintaining session continuity and data consistency.
load-balancing.009: Stateless Design: Application servers maintain no session state
load-balancing.010: Database Centralization: Shared PostgreSQL cluster for consistency
load-balancing.011: WebSocket Affinity: Sticky sessions for real-time connections
load-balancing.012: Health-Based Routing: Automatic failure detection and recovery

load-balancing.033: Nginx Lua Script for Dynamic Routing
load-balancing.061: Symptoms: Some servers overloaded while others idle
load-balancing.075: Symptoms: False positive health check failures
load-balancing.083: Health Check Tuning: Set appropriate intervals and timeouts
load-balancing.084: Session Affinity: Use sticky sessions only when necessary
load-balancing.085: Graceful Shutdowns: Implement proper connection draining
load-balancing.086: SSL Termination: Terminate SSL at load balancer for performance
load-balancing.088: Monitoring: Monitor load distribution and health status
load-balancing.089: Capacity Planning: Plan for peak traffic scenarios
load-balancing.090: Disaster Recovery: Test failover scenarios regularly
load-balancing.091: Security: Implement rate limiting and DDoS protection

message-flow.001: Figure 1: Complete message flow and state diagram showing bidirectional communication between agents and server
message-flow.004: All real-time communication between agents and the server occurs over WebSocket connections secured with mutual TLS (mTLS). The WebSocket layer provides:
message-flow.005: Real-time bidirectional communication for immediate message delivery
message-flow.006: Automatic reconnection logic with exponential backoff
message-flow.007: Connection state management and heartbeat monitoring
message-flow.010: Both agents and server maintain persistent message queues to ensure no data is lost during network interruptions or system restarts:
message-flow.012: Local SQLite database stores messages when connection is unavailable
message-flow.013: Automatic replay of queued messages upon reconnection
message-flow.014: Priority-based processing ensures critical messages are sent first
message-flow.015: Retry logic with configurable maximum attempts
message-flow.017: PostgreSQL message_queue table provides enterprise-grade persistence
message-flow.018: Host-specific and broadcast queuing for targeted or fleet-wide operations
message-flow.020: Transaction integrity ensures consistent state management
message-flow.025: Messages can also transition to FAILED state if maximum retry attempts are exceeded, or EXPIRED if they exceed the configured time-to-live.
message-flow.049: Messages are processed according to priority levels, ensuring critical operations are handled first:
message-flow.055: The heartbeat mechanism is critical for monitoring agent health and maintaining connection state. This detailed diagram shows the complete flow of a heartbeat message from generation to processing:
message-flow.056: Figure 2: Detailed heartbeat message flow from agent through queuing systems to final processing
message-flow.058: Timer Event: The message_sender() loop triggers heartbeats based on ping_interval configuration
message-flow.059: Message Creation: create_heartbeat_message() generates a heartbeat with agent status, hostname, IPs, and timestamp
message-flow.060: Local Queueing: Message is queued in SQLite database ( agent.db ) via queue_outbound_message()
message-flow.061: Background Processing: The process_outbound_queue() thread picks up high-priority heartbeats
message-flow.062: WebSocket Transmission: Messages are sent via secure WebSocket with mTLS encryption
message-flow.064: WebSocket Reception: FastAPI WebSocket endpoint receives the message
message-flow.065: Message Routing: _route_message() identifies the heartbeat type
message-flow.066: Database Queueing: Message is queued in PostgreSQL with INBOUND direction
message-flow.067: Async Processing: Background MessageProcessor service processes queued messages
message-flow.068: Heartbeat Handling: handle_heartbeat() updates host's last_access timestamp and status
message-flow.069: Acknowledgment: Server sends ACK message back to agent
message-flow.071: A separate background service runs every 60 seconds to check for hosts that haven't sent heartbeats within the configured timeout period (default: 5 minutes). Hosts exceeding this threshold are automatically marked as "down" in the system.
message-flow.074: State Transitions: up → down when timeout exceeded
message-flow.075: Recovery: Automatic status update to "up" on next successful heartbeat

performance-stability.005: Enterprise-grade performance engineering with comprehensive monitoring, optimization, and reliability measures
performance-stability.012: SysManage implements multiple layers of stability measures ensuring reliable operation in enterprise environments.
performance-stability.013: Fault Isolation: Automatic circuit breakers prevent cascade failures by temporarily blocking operations to failed services. Includes configurable thresholds, failure detection, and automatic recovery mechanisms.
performance-stability.014: Resilient Communication: Intelligent retry mechanisms with exponential backoff, jitter, and maximum attempt limits. Handles transient failures while preventing system overload during recovery.
performance-stability.015: Resource Protection: Advanced rate limiting protects against abuse and ensures fair resource allocation. Implements token bucket algorithms with per-user and global limits.
performance-stability.016: Service Continuity: System continues operating with reduced functionality during partial failures. Critical operations remain available while non-essential features degrade gracefully.
performance-stability.017: Resource Efficiency: Optimized database and network connection pooling prevents resource exhaustion and improves performance under load. Includes connection health monitoring and automatic pool resizing.
performance-stability.018: Proactive Detection: Comprehensive health checks monitor all system components with automated alerting. Includes dependency checks, performance baselines, and predictive failure detection.
performance-stability.020: Non-blocking I/O operations with FastAPI async/await patterns, ensuring maximum throughput and resource utilization.
performance-stability.021: Multi-layer caching strategy with Redis for session data, application-level caching, and database query result caching.
performance-stability.022: Persistent WebSocket connections, HTTP/2 support, and optimized connection pooling for minimal latency.
performance-stability.023: Strategic indexing, query optimization, connection pooling, and efficient data access patterns for sub-10ms queries.
performance-stability.024: Efficient memory management, CPU optimization, and minimal agent footprint on managed systems.
performance-stability.025: Compressed data transmission, efficient serialization, and minimal message overhead for network efficiency.
performance-stability.029: SysManage is designed to scale horizontally across multiple deployment scenarios
performance-stability.032: CPU, memory, disk, and network utilization across all managed hosts with real-time alerting.

performance-stability.034: Query performance, connection pool utilization, lock analysis, and index optimization recommendations.
performance-stability.035: Connection quality, latency measurements, bandwidth utilization, and connectivity status monitoring.
performance-stability.036: Authentication events, authorization failures, certificate expiration, and security incident detection.
performance-stability.037: Operation success rates, user activity patterns, system utilization trends, and capacity planning data.

system-architecture.005: Modern distributed architecture designed for scalability, reliability, and enterprise-grade performance
system-architecture.011: SysManage employs a layered architecture pattern ensuring separation of concerns, maintainability, and scalability.
system-architecture.012: React TypeScript Frontend: Modern single-page application with real-time WebSocket integration, responsive design, and comprehensive system management interfaces. Features include host management, package operations, script execution, and system monitoring dashboards.
system-architecture.013: FastAPI REST Endpoints: High-performance API layer with automatic OpenAPI documentation, request validation, authentication middleware, and comprehensive error handling. Supports both synchronous REST operations and asynchronous WebSocket communications.
system-architecture.014: Service Components: Core business logic handling host management, package operations, script execution, message queuing, and system orchestration. Implements complex workflows like package dependency resolution, batch operations, and state management.
system-architecture.015: PostgreSQL Database: Enterprise-grade relational database with advanced features including JSON columns, full-text search, complex indexing strategies, and ACID compliance. Handles user data, host information, package inventories, and audit logs.
system-architecture.016: WebSocket + mTLS Protocol: Real-time bidirectional communication with agents using WebSocket over mutual TLS. Implements persistent message queuing, automatic retry mechanisms, and connection health monitoring for reliable distributed operations.
system-architecture.017: Cross-Platform Agents: Lightweight Python agents running on managed hosts, supporting Windows, Linux, and macOS. Handle local operations, system monitoring, package management, and secure communication with the server.
system-architecture.028: Modular design with clearly defined service boundaries, enabling independent development, testing, and deployment of system components.
system-architecture.029: Optimized for speed with async operations, connection pooling, efficient database queries, and minimal resource footprint on managed hosts.
system-architecture.030: Multiple security layers including mTLS encryption, JWT authentication, role-based access control, input validation, and comprehensive audit logging.
system-architecture.031: Designed to scale horizontally with load balancing support, stateless services, efficient caching, and distributed architecture patterns.
system-architecture.032: Resilient design with graceful degradation, automatic retry mechanisms, circuit breakers, and comprehensive error handling and recovery.
system-architecture.033: Clean code architecture with comprehensive testing, detailed documentation, consistent coding standards, and automated CI/CD pipelines.

system-overview.005: Comprehensive overview of SysManage system architecture, components, and data flow patterns.
system-overview.007: SysManage is built as a distributed system with a central server coordinating multiple lightweight agents across your infrastructure. The architecture emphasizes security, scalability, and reliability while maintaining simplicity for both deployment and operation.
system-overview.011: The central coordination hub that manages all agents, processes requests, and maintains the global state of your infrastructure.
system-overview.019: Lightweight agents that run on managed hosts to collect information, execute tasks, and maintain communication with the server.
system-overview.021: Cross-platform support (Linux, Windows, macOS, FreeBSD, OpenBSD, NetBSD)
system-overview.027: Modern React-based web application providing a comprehensive management interface for administrators and operators.
system-overview.029: Responsive design for desktop and mobile
system-overview.035: Comprehensive API built with FastAPI providing programmatic access to all SysManage functionality.
system-overview.047: SysManage uses a robust mTLS (mutual TLS) authentication system:
system-overview.048: Server Certificate Authority: Issues and manages agent certificates
system-overview.049: Agent Certificates: Unique certificates for each agent with automatic rotation
system-overview.050: Certificate Validation: Continuous validation of certificate status and revocation
system-overview.051: Secure Enrollment: Automated certificate enrollment with approval workflows
system-overview.067: SysManage is designed to scale horizontally across multiple dimensions:
system-overview.069: Stateless Design: Servers maintain no session state, enabling easy horizontal scaling
system-overview.070: Load Balancing: Support for standard load balancers with session affinity for WebSocket connections
system-overview.071: Database Clustering: PostgreSQL clustering support for high availability
system-overview.072: Shared Storage: Certificate and configuration storage can be shared across instances
system-overview.074: Lightweight Footprint: Minimal resource usage per agent
system-overview.075: Efficient Communication: Optimized protocols to minimize network overhead
system-overview.076: Connection Pooling: Intelligent connection management and pooling
system-overview.077: Regional Distribution: Support for geographically distributed deployments
system-overview.122: Vite: Build tool and development server
system-overview.123: React Query: Data fetching and state management
system-overview.129: JWT: JSON Web Tokens for API auth
system-overview.135: Nginx: Reverse proxy and load balancing

multi-tenancy.005: How SysManage isolates many tenants on one deployment — a control plane plus a dedicated database per tenant — and how that powers the Enterprise SaaS edition.
multi-tenancy.006: Isolation by separation, not by column
multi-tenancy.007: Most multi-tenant products put every customer's rows in one shared database and filter by a tenant_id column. SysManage does the opposite: each tenant gets its own physically separate PostgreSQL database . A missing WHERE tenant_id = ? can never leak one customer's data to another, because the data was never in the same database to begin with. Multi-tenancy is opt-in and licensed (the Enterprise SaaS tier); when it is disabled, SysManage runs as an ordinary single-database server.
multi-tenancy.009: The same SysManage codebase runs in two shapes. The diagram below contrasts the classic single-tenant deployment (Community, Professional, and on-prem Enterprise) with the multi-tenant topology behind Enterprise SaaS.
multi-tenancy.010: Click the diagram to open the full-size version in a new tab.
multi-tenancy.012: One SysManage server talks to one PostgreSQL database. Hosts, users, inventory, and audit all live together. This is the Community Edition and the on-prem Professional and Enterprise deployments, and it is the only topology an air-gapped appliance ever uses.
multi-tenancy.013: Default Nothing about multi-tenancy is active unless you turn it on.
multi-tenancy.015: A control plane fronts many tenants. Control-plane metadata lives in a registry database; each tenant's operational data lives in its own database. The server resolves the caller to their tenant and routes every query to that tenant's database. OpenBAO brokers a short-lived, least-privilege credential per tenant.
multi-tenancy.016: Enterprise SaaS Licensed and enabled per deployment.
multi-tenancy.018: A multi-tenant deployment is split into a small control plane and any number of tenant silos :
multi-tenancy.020: The control plane is the SysManage server itself plus the licensed multitenancy_engine . It owns the tenant registry — which tenants exist and which database each one is placed in — and it is the only component that decides, per request, which tenant database to use. It also holds the small amount of genuinely cross-tenant state: the catalog of tenants and their placements.
multi-tenancy.022: Each tenant is a silo: its own database, reached with its own credentials. A tenant silo contains everything host-scoped — hosts, packages, hardware inventory, compliance results, the agent message queue, and the tenant's own audit-relevant operational data. One tenant can never see another tenant's silo because it never holds a connection to it.
multi-tenancy.024: SysManage organizes tables into three partitions , distinguished by table-name prefix, so it is unambiguous at the schema level where each table belongs:
multi-tenancy.032: The tenant catalog and per-tenant database placements.
multi-tenancy.036: Canonical reference data that is identical for every tenant (for example the CVE/NVD catalog).
multi-tenancy.040: All host-scoped operational data — one copy per tenant.
multi-tenancy.042: Because the partitions can live in physically separate databases, a foreign key can never point across a partition boundary — PostgreSQL cannot enforce a constraint into another database. Where a logical reference is needed across partitions (for example a row that records which server-global user created it), SysManage stores a soft reference : the GUID is kept, but no database-level foreign key is declared. This keeps each database self-contained and independently migratable.
multi-tenancy.044: Each partition has its own Alembic migration chain — registry, shared, and tenant — with separate version tables. The registry and shared chains run once against the control-plane databases; the tenant chain is applied to every tenant database. Schema is open-source and identical for all tenants; only the routing logic that decides which database to touch is licensed.
multi-tenancy.046: Routing is the heart of the data plane. Every request carries an active tenant — established from the authenticated session and JWT — and a single routing seam turns that into the right database connection:
multi-tenancy.047: User requests. Middleware binds the active tenant for the request; data-plane endpoints resolve their database session from it, so a tenant user only ever reads and writes their own database.
multi-tenancy.048: Server-wide background work. Jobs that must touch every host — the heartbeat sweep, the store-and-forward queue processors, the repository-mirror and upgrade-profile schedulers — fan out across the bootstrap database and every provisioned tenant database, one isolated session each.
multi-tenancy.049: Agent traffic. Agent reports flow through a durable, per-tenant message queue. Each inbound message is resolved to its host's tenant and written into that tenant's database; a bound host's data always lands in its own silo.
multi-tenancy.051: If the router cannot resolve a tenant for a piece of work, it logs loudly with full context and stops — it never silently falls back to a default database. Misrouting tenant data is treated as a correctness bug, not something to paper over.
multi-tenancy.053: Not everything is per-tenant. SysManage draws a deliberate line:
multi-tenancy.056: User accounts, authentication, roles/RBAC, MFA, password-reset and external-IdP state, the tenant registry, licensing.
multi-tenancy.057: Hosts and their inventory, packages, compliance results, the agent message queue, and the tenant's operational data.
multi-tenancy.058: Login is not per-tenant — a person authenticates once against the server — but the moment they act on managed data, that data is served from the active tenant's database. Keeping identity server-global while keeping managed data per-tenant is what lets one operator administer many tenants without ever mixing their fleets.
multi-tenancy.059: Binding a host to a tenant
multi-tenancy.060: A host belongs to exactly one tenant, decided at registration. An operator issues a tenant-scoped enrollment token from the control-plane UI and drops it into that host's agent configuration. When the agent registers, the server validates and consumes the token and records the host→tenant binding in the registry. From then on the agent is oblivious to tenancy: it just reports, and the server routes its data to the right silo. See the multi-tenant security and deployment guides for the operator workflow.
multi-tenancy.062: New tenants can be provisioned self-service from the control-plane UI. A least-privilege provisioner identity creates the tenant's database and registers it — the server never holds database-superuser rights. At run time, the tenant's database credentials are brokered by OpenBAO : short-lived, dynamically issued, and scoped to a single tenant. The full security treatment — why this design is hard to attack — is covered in the Multi-Tenant Security guide.
multi-tenancy.064: Multi-tenancy is entirely opt-in. With it disabled, none of the routing, registry, or per-tenant machinery is active and SysManage behaves exactly like the single-tenant topology — the same code, the same one database. The multi-tenant routing engine is part of the licensed Enterprise SaaS tier ( MULTITENANT_SAAS ); the database schema and partitioning themselves remain open-source. Without the licensed engine, a deployment configured for multi-tenancy fails loudly with a clear licensing error rather than misrouting.
multi-tenancy.067: Why per-database isolation, OpenBAO-brokered credentials, and the identity boundary make this design exceptionally hard to attack.
multi-tenancy.070: The multi-tenant, hosted edition: what it includes, who it is for, and how it compares to Community, Professional, and Enterprise.
multi-tenancy.073: Standing up a multi-tenant deployment: the registry and tenant databases, OpenBAO, the three migration chains, and provisioning.
multi-tenancy.076: The underlying schema, entity relationships, and persistence patterns that the partitions are built on.
