multi-tenancy.005: Standing up a multi-tenant SysManage deployment — the databases, OpenBAO, the migration chains, and tenant provisioning — for development and production.
multi-tenancy.007: Multi-tenancy is the Enterprise SaaS deployment model: a control plane plus an isolated database per tenant. It is opt-in and licensed — with it off, SysManage installs exactly as the single-tenant guide describes ( Server Installation ). Read the architecture first so the pieces below make sense.
multi-tenancy.009: A multi-tenant deployment introduces databases beyond the single application database of a normal install:
multi-tenancy.015: The tenant catalog and per-tenant database placements. This is the database sysmanage.yaml points at.
multi-tenancy.018: Canonical reference data identical for every tenant (collapses onto the bootstrap database until the dedicated shared engine is configured).
multi-tenancy.021: Each tenant's host-scoped operational data, in its own database.
multi-tenancy.022: Three things make this work: PostgreSQL (one or more instances), OpenBAO (to broker per-tenant database credentials), and the licensed multitenancy engine (the routing logic). Without the engine loaded, a deployment configured for multi-tenancy fails loudly with a licensing error rather than misrouting.
multi-tenancy.024: The fastest way to develop against multi-tenancy locally. By default a dev checkout runs collapsed — multi-tenancy off, all three migration chains applied to one database — which is the normal single-tenant experience. Turn it on explicitly:
multi-tenancy.025: Install the dev environment and PostgreSQL as in the Server Installation guide (clone, make install-dev , create the sysmanage role and database).
multi-tenancy.026: Enable multi-tenancy in sysmanage-dev.yaml . Point the bootstrap/registry block at your registry database, turn the feature on, and configure the local OpenBAO (the dev config already ships a vault: block): registry: host: localhost port: 5432 name: sysmanage_registry user: sysmanage password: DEV_Change_Me_ABC123! multitenancy: enabled: true self_service_provisioning: true vault: # OpenBAO brokers per-tenant database credentials url: http://localhost:8200 bootstrap: config_file: ./openbao.hcl data_path: ./data/openbao
multi-tenancy.027: Run the migrations. make migrate drives scripts/sysmanage_migrate.py — the same tool operators run in production. With multi-tenancy on it automatically starts OpenBAO for the per-tenant fan-out, applies the registry, shared, and tenant chains, then stops OpenBAO again: make migrate
multi-tenancy.028: Provision the bootstrap provisioner identity — a scoped Postgres role plus OpenBAO policy so the server can create tenants without holding superuser: make provision-bootstrap ARGS='--bao-token $BAO_TOKEN'
multi-tenancy.029: Start the stack and create a tenant. make start , then create a tenant from the control-plane UI (Settings → Tenants). With self_service_provisioning on, the server provisions the tenant's database and OpenBAO credentials for you.
multi-tenancy.030: Fan the tenant chain out to the newly created tenant database(s): make migrate-tenants
multi-tenancy.033: Production follows the same shape, with real databases, a hardened OpenBAO, and least-privilege provisioning.
multi-tenancy.034: Provision the registry database and a login role. On your PostgreSQL instance, create the sysmanage role and the registry database it owns (see the Server Installation database step). The server connects as this role; it is not a superuser.
multi-tenancy.035: Stand up OpenBAO and configure its database secrets engine so it can issue short-lived, per-tenant PostgreSQL credentials. OpenBAO is the broker that keeps the server from ever holding a long-lived, shared database password. (Build and start it with the repository's scripts/build-openbao.sh and scripts/start-openbao.sh , or your own managed instance.)
multi-tenancy.036: Configure /etc/sysmanage.yaml — point the registry block at the registry database, enable multi-tenancy, and configure the vault: registry: host: db-registry.internal port: 5432 name: sysmanage_registry user: sysmanage password: <from your secret store> multitenancy: enabled: true self_service_provisioning: true vault: url: https://openbao.internal:8200 # token / auth configured per your OpenBAO setup Licensing: per-tenant routing requires the licensed multitenancy engine (the MULTITENANT_SAAS tier). Without it, the server refuses to run multi-tenant rather than silently misrouting — this is intentional.
multi-tenancy.037: Run the migrations with the operator tool. make migrate (or scripts/sysmanage_migrate.py directly) applies the registry, shared, and tenant chains and shows per-database progress; with multi-tenancy on it brings OpenBAO up for the fan-out: make migrate
multi-tenancy.038: Provision the least-privilege provisioner identity so self-service tenant creation works without cluster-superuser rights: make provision-bootstrap ARGS='--bao-token $BAO_TOKEN'
multi-tenancy.039: Create your tenants from the control-plane UI (Settings → Tenants). Each tenant gets its own database and its own OpenBAO-brokered credentials. Place each tenant's database where it needs to live — by host, region, or jurisdiction.
multi-tenancy.040: Fan the tenant chain out to every provisioned tenant database (OpenBAO must be running): make migrate-tenants
multi-tenancy.041: Bind hosts to tenants. Issue a tenant-scoped enrollment token per tenant (Tenants → token), drop it into that host's agent configuration under security.enrollment_token , and register the agent. From then on the host's data lands in its tenant's database automatically.
multi-tenancy.043: Schema changes use an expand-contract discipline so a migration is always safe to apply while the previous version of the server is still running. Each partition (registry, shared, tenant) has its own linear, idempotent Alembic chain with a separate version table, and the operator tool applies them across the bootstrap database and every tenant database, reporting progress. The same sysmanage_migrate.py you run in development is what runs in production — there is no separate, riskier production path.
multi-tenancy.045: Confirm a bound host's data really lands in its tenant database rather than the bootstrap one — switch the active tenant in the UI and compare host counts, or query the tenant databases directly:
multi-tenancy.046: Each tenant's hosts appear only in that tenant's database. If the router cannot resolve a tenant for a piece of work, it logs loudly with full context rather than guessing — check the server logs.

multi-tenancy.052: Why per-database isolation and OpenBAO credentials are hard to attack.

host-details-walkthrough.005: Renames the host record on the server. The agent's local hostname is unaffected.
host-details-walkthrough.008: Add / remove free-form tags used by Hosts list filters and fleet group dynamic criteria.
host-details-walkthrough.011: Sends the agent a reboot command. On Pro+ Professional tier hosts with running children, triggers safe-reboot orchestration (stop children, reboot, restart children).
host-details-walkthrough.014: Sends the agent a poweroff command.
host-details-walkthrough.017: Triggers in-place upgrade of the agent .deb / .rpm / .pkg / .apk on the host.
host-details-walkthrough.020: Pushes a public key into the agent user's ~/.ssh/authorized_keys ; lets server admins SSH into the host through whatever account the agent runs as.
host-details-walkthrough.023: Reissues the host's mTLS client certificate (rotation).
host-details-walkthrough.026: Quick ad-hoc shell / PowerShell / batch run against this single host. For library / multi-host / scheduled runs see the Pro+ Automation Engine.
host-details-walkthrough.029: Pro+: attaches this host to a Graylog input (log forwarder sidecar).
host-details-walkthrough.031: Deploy OpenTelemetry / Start / Stop / Restart OTEL
host-details-walkthrough.035: Removes the host record + history from the server. The agent on the machine isn't touched (re-registers on next start unless uninstalled).
host-details-walkthrough.038: Per-host service start / stop / restart lives on the Server Roles tab, not in a separate Services tab. When the agent fingerprints a server role (web server, database, mail, DNS, etc.) it also records the backing system service. Roles that map to a service can be controlled from this tab.
host-details-walkthrough.039: Each row in the roles table has a checkbox. The checkbox is only selectable for roles that have a service name; roles with no service ("None") cannot be selected. Use the header checkbox to select or clear all controllable roles at once. Select the roles you want to act on, then use the Service Control Actions buttons below the table:
host-details-walkthrough.052: Each button appears only if you hold the matching permission. After a start / stop / restart is requested the page refreshes the role list a few seconds later so the new service status is reflected.
host-details-walkthrough.053: Privileged mode is required. The checkboxes and the Start / Stop / Restart buttons are disabled unless the agent on the host is running in privileged mode. When it is not, a "Privileged mode required for service control" note is shown and no services can be controlled.
host-details-walkthrough.054: On Ubuntu hosts, the Ubuntu Pro tab has its own separate Services panel. The pencil (Edit services) button there puts the panel into edit mode where you toggle individual Ubuntu Pro services (ESM, livepatch, FIPS, USG) on or off and Save; this only appears when the host is attached to a Pro contract and the agent is privileged. That is Pro service enablement, not general system-service start/stop.
host-details-walkthrough.056: Most host data refreshes on a schedule and when the host re-registers, but several tabs have a button that asks the agent to send fresh data right now. These are useful after you've changed something on the host and don't want to wait for the next scheduled upload. Each button requires the host to be active (online).
host-details-walkthrough.062: Requests a fresh certificate scan from the agent. The certificate list reloads a few seconds after the request completes.
host-details-walkthrough.065: Requests a fresh server-role detection run. The roles table reloads a few seconds after the request completes.
host-details-walkthrough.068: The broadest refresh. In one click it asks the agent for diagnostics, system info, user / access data, software inventory, and a package-updates check. On hosts that support child hosts it also triggers a virtualization capability check. The button shows "Requesting..." while in flight and records a "Last requested" timestamp.
host-details-walkthrough.071: Asks the agent to re-enumerate child hosts (VMs / containers) and reload the child-host list.
host-details-walkthrough.072: All of these are asynchronous requests to the agent: the button confirms the request was sent, then the relevant view reloads once the agent has reported back. If a host is offline the request cannot be delivered.
host-details-walkthrough.073: The Host Details page — the Operating System and host summary panes.
host-details-walkthrough.074: The Hardware tab: CPU, memory, storage devices, and network interfaces.
host-details-walkthrough.075: The Software tab: the host's installed package inventory.

package-uninstallation-guide.005: Learn how to safely remove software packages from your managed hosts using SysManage's web interface.
package-uninstallation-guide.007: SysManage provides a user-friendly web interface for uninstalling software packages from your managed hosts. This guide will walk you through the process of safely removing packages while understanding the implications and safeguards in place.
package-uninstallation-guide.009: How to locate and uninstall packages from the web interface
package-uninstallation-guide.010: Understanding the confirmation process and safety measures
package-uninstallation-guide.012: Best practices for safe package removal
package-uninstallation-guide.015: Package uninstallation is irreversible and can impact system stability. Always:
package-uninstallation-guide.016: Test uninstallations in non-production environments first
package-uninstallation-guide.017: Create system backups before removing critical packages
package-uninstallation-guide.019: Have rollback plans for critical systems
package-uninstallation-guide.022: Begin by accessing the specific host where you want to uninstall packages:
package-uninstallation-guide.023: From the main dashboard, click on "Hosts" in the navigation menu
package-uninstallation-guide.024: Locate your target host in the hosts list
package-uninstallation-guide.025: Click on the hostname or "View Details" to open the host detail page
package-uninstallation-guide.027: Use the search functionality or filters to quickly find specific hosts in large environments.
package-uninstallation-guide.029: Once on the host detail page, navigate to the software inventory:
package-uninstallation-guide.030: Look for the tab navigation near the top of the page
package-uninstallation-guide.032: Wait for the package list to load completely
package-uninstallation-guide.033: The Software Inventory tab displays all packages currently installed on the host, including:
package-uninstallation-guide.034: Package Name: The name of the installed software
package-uninstallation-guide.037: Description: Brief description of the package's purpose
package-uninstallation-guide.039: Step 3: Locate the Package to Uninstall
package-uninstallation-guide.040: Find the specific package you want to remove:
package-uninstallation-guide.042: Use the search box at the top of the package list
package-uninstallation-guide.043: Type the package name or keywords
package-uninstallation-guide.044: Results will filter in real-time as you type
package-uninstallation-guide.047: Use the sorting options to organize by name, version, or other fields
package-uninstallation-guide.048: Use pagination controls for large package lists
package-uninstallation-guide.050: Review the package description and dependencies before proceeding. Consider whether the package is:
package-uninstallation-guide.053: Part of a larger software suite
package-uninstallation-guide.056: Once you've located the package, begin the uninstallation process:
package-uninstallation-guide.057: Find the "Uninstall" button in the Actions column for your target package
package-uninstallation-guide.059: A confirmation dialog will immediately appear
package-uninstallation-guide.062: You have the correct package selected
package-uninstallation-guide.063: You understand the impact of removing this package
package-uninstallation-guide.064: You have appropriate backups if needed
package-uninstallation-guide.065: This is the right host for the operation
package-uninstallation-guide.067: The confirmation dialog is a critical safety step:
package-uninstallation-guide.069: Title: "Uninstall Package" clearly identifies the action
package-uninstallation-guide.070: Warning Message: Explains that the action will remove the package from the system
package-uninstallation-guide.071: Package Details: Shows the exact package name being removed
package-uninstallation-guide.074: To Cancel: Click "Cancel" or click outside the dialog to abort
package-uninstallation-guide.075: To Proceed: Click the red "Uninstall" button to confirm
package-uninstallation-guide.077: The confirmation dialog cannot be bypassed and serves as your final checkpoint. Once you click "Uninstall," the operation cannot be undone through the interface.
package-uninstallation-guide.079: After confirming, monitor the uninstallation progress:
package-uninstallation-guide.081: A success message appears: "Package uninstallation has been queued"
package-uninstallation-guide.083: You're returned to the Software Inventory view
package-uninstallation-guide.085: Navigate to the "Software Changes" tab (previously "Software Installs")
package-uninstallation-guide.086: Look for your uninstallation request in the operations list
package-uninstallation-guide.087: Monitor the "Status" column for progress updates
package-uninstallation-guide.090: pending: Operation is queued but not yet started
package-uninstallation-guide.091: in_progress: Agent is actively removing the package
package-uninstallation-guide.097: In the "Software Changes" tab, verify the status shows "completed"
package-uninstallation-guide.098: Review any error messages if the status shows "failed"
package-uninstallation-guide.099: Check the timestamps to confirm operation timing
package-uninstallation-guide.102: Search for the previously uninstalled package
package-uninstallation-guide.103: Confirm it no longer appears in the installed packages list
package-uninstallation-guide.104: Refresh the page if necessary to see updated inventory
package-uninstallation-guide.108: System logs show no related errors

package-uninstallation-guide.117: Cleanup of development tools from production systems

package-uninstallation-guide.153: Inventory cache needs to be refreshed

package-uninstallation-guide.164: Check if the uninstalled package was a critical dependency
package-uninstallation-guide.166: Consider reinstalling the package if necessary
package-uninstallation-guide.168: Always research package dependencies before removal
package-uninstallation-guide.170: Use dependency checking tools when available
package-uninstallation-guide.171: Maintain detailed documentation of system dependencies

webui-overview.002: Explore the SysManage web interface with detailed screenshots and feature explanations.
webui-overview.004: The main dashboard provides a comprehensive view of your infrastructure at a glance.
webui-overview.007: View and manage all connected systems from a single interface. Monitor status, approve new agents, and access detailed host information.
webui-overview.009: Track available updates across all platforms. View security updates, system patches, and application updates in one unified view.
webui-overview.011: Manage user accounts, permissions, and access controls. Set up role-based authentication and monitor user activity.
webui-overview.013: Monitor security status, view vulnerability scans, and manage security policies across your infrastructure.
webui-overview.016: Click on any host to access detailed information including system specifications, installed software, update history, and real-time metrics.
webui-overview.018: The updates page provides a comprehensive view of all available updates across your infrastructure, with filtering and bulk update capabilities.
webui-overview.020: The interface is designed for ease of use with intuitive navigation, responsive design, and comprehensive search capabilities.
webui-overview.021: Responsive Design: Works seamlessly on desktop, tablet, and mobile devices
webui-overview.022: Real-time Updates: Live data updates via WebSocket connections
webui-overview.023: Global Search: Quickly find hosts, packages, or users across your infrastructure
webui-overview.024: Multi-language Support: Interface available in 14 languages
webui-overview.025: Dark/Light Themes: Choose your preferred interface theme
webui-overview.026: Getting Started with the Web Interface
webui-overview.027: Login: Access the web interface at https://your-server:8443
webui-overview.028: Dashboard: Review the main dashboard for an overview of your infrastructure
webui-overview.029: Approve Agents: Navigate to Hosts to approve pending agent connections
webui-overview.030: Explore Features: Use the navigation menu to explore different management areas
webui-overview.031: Customize: Adjust settings, language preferences, and notification options
webui-overview.032: Note: Screenshots will be added as the documentation evolves. The interface continues to receive updates and improvements based on user feedback.
webui-overview.034: The dashboard displays a set of summary cards, each showing a gauge with a single key number. The gauge color reflects status: green is healthy, yellow indicates a partial or warning condition, and red indicates a problem. The cards refresh automatically every 30 seconds. Click any card to jump to the relevant management page.
webui-overview.035: Hosts: Shows the total number of registered hosts. The gauge color reflects host status, where green means all approved hosts are up, yellow means some are up and some are down, and red means all approved hosts are down. Clicking the card opens the Hosts page.
webui-overview.036: Updates: Shows the number of hosts that have available package updates, measured against the total host count. The gauge turns red when one or more hosts have updates pending. Clicking the card opens the Updates page.
webui-overview.037: Security: Shows the number of available security updates across your infrastructure. The gauge turns red when any security updates are outstanding. Clicking the card opens the Updates page with the security-only filter already applied.
webui-overview.038: Reboot Needed: Shows the number of approved hosts that require a reboot. The gauge turns red when one or more hosts need to be rebooted. Clicking the card opens the Hosts page.
webui-overview.039: Antivirus: Shows the percentage of hosts covered by antivirus, from 0 to 100. The gauge is green at 80% or above, yellow between 50% and 80%, and red below 50%. Clicking the card opens the Hosts page.
webui-overview.040: OpenTelemetry: Shows the percentage of hosts covered by OpenTelemetry, from 0 to 100. The gauge uses the same color thresholds as the Antivirus card. Clicking the card opens the Hosts page.
webui-overview.042: You can choose which cards appear on your dashboard. Click the settings (gear) icon in the upper-right corner of the dashboard to open the Dashboard Settings dialog.
webui-overview.043: The dialog lists each available card (Hosts, Updates, Security, Reboot Needed, Antivirus, and OpenTelemetry) with a checkbox. Check a card to show it on the dashboard, or uncheck it to hide it. Use the Check All and Check None buttons to toggle every card at once. Click Save to apply your selection, or Cancel to discard changes. Your preferences are stored per user, so they persist across sessions.
webui-overview.044: The Map view plots managed hosts by geographic location.

profile-and-account.001: The Profile & Account page: personal details, password, multi-factor authentication, and API tokens.

automation-engine.002: POST /api/v1/automation/scripts — create or update; auto-bumps version and snapshots history
automation-engine.005: DELETE /api/v1/automation/scripts/{id} — remove script + all version history
automation-engine.007: POST /api/v1/automation/approvals/{id}/approve — promote linked execution to queued
automation-engine.013: Settings → Update Profiles schedules recurring package-update runs with cron and rollout rules.
automation-engine.014: A host's Automation tab lists recent script executions, pending approvals, and the saved-script library.

av-management-engine.002: POST /api/v1/av/policies — create or overwrite a policy by name
av-management-engine.004: POST /api/v1/av/policies/{name}/apply — resolve and queue the policy for a host list
av-management-engine.010: Settings → Antivirus sets the default antivirus package for each operating system across the fleet.
av-management-engine.011: A host's Security tab shows its antivirus and firewall status with deploy and remove controls.

firewall-orchestration-engine.002: firewalld — RHEL, CentOS, Rocky, Alma, Oracle, Fedora, AmazonLinux
firewall-orchestration-engine.003: pf — OpenBSD, FreeBSD (writes /etc/pf.conf via deploy_files, pfctl -nf validates, pfctl -f loads)
firewall-orchestration-engine.004: ipfw — FreeBSD alternative (kldload + sysrc preamble, rule numbering from 100/+10)
firewall-orchestration-engine.005: npf — NetBSD (writes /etc/npf.conf , npfctl validate then reload )
firewall-orchestration-engine.006: Windows Firewall — netsh advfirewall , RDP 3389 always preserved, source uses remoteip=
firewall-orchestration-engine.007: macOS Application Firewall — socketfilterfw , app-based rather than port-based; port-only rules surface in unsupported
firewall-orchestration-engine.008: An open allow on the same (port, proto, direction) as a source-restricted allow (the open rule shadows the restricted one)
firewall-orchestration-engine.009: An allow and a deny on the same triple
firewall-orchestration-engine.010: Two distinct source CIDRs on the same triple
firewall-orchestration-engine.016: Settings → Firewall Roles defines reusable port-rule sets that can be assigned to and deployed across hosts.

fleet-engine.003: GET /api/v1/fleet/groups/{id}/hosts — resolve effective membership against the live inventory
fleet-engine.005: POST /api/v1/fleet/rolling — create with batch_size + batch_delay + failure_threshold_pct
fleet-engine.012: fleet_config_deployment — reserved for fleet-wide config deploy
fleet-engine.013: A host's Fleet tab shows the groups it belongs to and any bulk operations or rolling deployments targeting it.

health-analysis.013: The Health tab analyses a host and reports a grade, score, detected issues, and recommendations.

observability-engine.001: POST /api/v1/observability/otel/deploy — deploy the collector to one or more hosts
observability-engine.002: POST /api/v1/observability/otel/{action} — lifecycle: start / stop / restart / uninstall
observability-engine.004: POST /api/v1/observability/graylog/attach — attach a host (or set of hosts) to a Graylog input
observability-engine.006: GET /api/v1/observability/graylog/status — per-host attach state + source list
observability-engine.007: POST /api/v1/observability/grafana/connect — register a Grafana instance and provision dashboards
observability-engine.009: Deploy OpenTelemetry , Start OpenTelemetry Service , Stop OpenTelemetry Service , Restart OpenTelemetry Service
observability-engine.010: Connect Host to Graylog , Enable Graylog Integration
observability-engine.012: Settings → Integrations deploys and connects the observability stack — OpenTelemetry, Graylog, and Grafana.

upgrade-guide.002: vuln_engine — CVE database + per-host vulnerability scanning
upgrade-guide.004: alerting_engine — Email / webhook / Slack / Teams alert routing
upgrade-guide.006: audit_engine — Audit log retention + SIEM export
upgrade-guide.007: secrets_engine — OpenBAO-backed secret deployment to hosts
upgrade-guide.008: container_engine — Full WSL / LXD container lifecycle (open source has read-only)
upgrade-guide.009: proplus_core — Pro+ settings + license management UI
upgrade-guide.010: av_management_engine — Centralised ClamAV / ClamWin deployment, named policies, scan scheduling, commercial-AV detection (CrowdStrike, SentinelOne, Defender, etc.)
upgrade-guide.011: firewall_orchestration_engine — Multi-platform firewall config generation (UFW, firewalld, pf, ipfw, npf, Windows Firewall, macOS), role assignment, fleet deployment, conflict detection, compliance reporting

virtualization-engine.001: POST /api/host/{id}/virtualization/create-child — create a KVM / bhyve / VMM / LXD / WSL child host
virtualization-engine.006: POST /api/host/{id}/virtualization/{kvm|bhyve|vmm|lxd|wsl}/enable — bootstrap the hypervisor on the parent
virtualization-engine.007: GET /api/host/{id}/virtualization/status — capability + ready state per hypervisor
virtualization-engine.008: POST /api/host/{id}/reboot — safe reboot orchestration (stops children, reboots parent, restarts children)
virtualization-engine.009: Enable KVM , Enable bhyve , Enable VMM , Enable LXD , Enable WSL — bootstrap the hypervisor on a parent host.
virtualization-engine.010: Create Child Host — open the create dialog and dispatch the create plan.
virtualization-engine.011: Start Child Host , Stop Child Host , Restart Child Host , Delete Child Host — per-action lifecycle controls.
virtualization-engine.012: Configure Child Host — edit child host settings (hostname, distribution, etc.).
virtualization-engine.013: Reboot Host — trigger the safe-parent-reboot flow.
virtualization-engine.014: The Child Hosts tab manages the VMs and containers running on a hypervisor host.

vulnerability-scanning.011: The Vulnerabilities overview lists every host with its CVE counts by severity and an overall risk grade.
vulnerability-scanning.012: The Vulnerabilities tab on a host's detail page surfaces that host's CVE findings inline.
vulnerability-scanning.013: Settings → CVE Database controls how often the CVE feeds refresh and shows recent ingestion runs.
vulnerability-scanning.014: Drilling into a host shows each CVE with the affected package, installed and fixed versions, and remediation guidance.

enterprise-saas.005: The multi-tenant, hosted edition — manage many fleets from one platform, with each tenant's data in its own physically isolated database.
enterprise-saas.007: Run a fleet-management business, not just a fleet.
enterprise-saas.008: Enterprise SaaS takes everything in SysManage Enterprise and delivers it multi-tenant and hosted — so a managed-service provider, a platform team, or a multi-division enterprise can manage dozens or hundreds of separate fleets from a single deployment, while guaranteeing that no tenant can ever see another's data. The isolation isn't a policy you trust; it's a database boundary you can point at.
enterprise-saas.010: SysManage meets you wherever you are — from a free open-source server to a fully multi-tenant SaaS platform. Enterprise SaaS is the newest tier, sitting alongside the established on-premises offerings.
enterprise-saas.013: The full open-source platform. Single server, single database, self-hosted.
enterprise-saas.016: AI health analysis, container management, and advanced monitoring for growing teams. On-premises.
enterprise-saas.019: The full Professional+ feature set — compliance, vulnerability scanning, federation, secrets, automation. On-premises.
enterprise-saas.022: Everything in Enterprise, delivered multi-tenant and hosted, with a dedicated isolated database per tenant.
enterprise-saas.023: Community, Professional, and Enterprise are single-tenant deployments. Enterprise SaaS is the multi-tenant edition — see the architecture for how the two topologies differ.
enterprise-saas.026: Every tenant lives in its own PostgreSQL database. There is no shared table, no tenant_id column to forget, and no connection from one tenant to another. When a customer asks “can anyone else see our data?”, the answer is a boundary you can point at, not a policy you ask them to trust. See the security treatment .
enterprise-saas.028: Each tenant's database credentials are leased on demand from OpenBAO — short-lived, auto-expiring, and scoped to one tenant. There is no shared master password to leak, and a captured credential is narrow and brief by construction.
enterprise-saas.030: Because a tenant is a single self-contained database, you can place it where it must live — a specific region or jurisdiction — and back it up, export it, or delete it completely without ever touching another tenant. Right-to-be-forgotten becomes a per-database operation.
enterprise-saas.032: Provision a new tenant from the control-plane UI in minutes. A least-privilege provisioner creates the tenant's database and registers it — no manual cluster surgery, and the server never holds superuser rights.
enterprise-saas.034: Administer every tenant from a single control plane. Operators switch tenant context to work inside a fleet; server-wide jobs fan out across all tenants. Identity is shared; data never is.
enterprise-saas.036: Enterprise SaaS includes the full Professional+ engine set — compliance, vulnerability scanning, reporting, automation, secrets, and more — running per tenant. Nothing is given up for the multi-tenant model.
enterprise-saas.038: Managed service providers (MSPs) managing the fleets of many separate customers, who need airtight isolation and per-customer billing, backup, and offboarding.
enterprise-saas.039: Platform and SaaS companies offering infrastructure management to their own users as a hosted product.
enterprise-saas.040: Large enterprises and holding companies with independent divisions, subsidiaries, or jurisdictions that must not share a data store.
enterprise-saas.041: Regulated organizations that need per-tenant data residency, independent backups, and clean, provable deletion.
enterprise-saas.050: Compliance, vulnerability scanning, federation, secrets, automation
enterprise-saas.061: The open-source schema and partitioning are the same across editions; the multi-tenant routing engine ( MULTITENANT_SAAS ) is what the Enterprise SaaS license unlocks.
enterprise-saas.062: Ready to run many fleets, securely?
enterprise-saas.063: Enterprise SaaS is licensed per deployment. Talk to us about provisioning, data-residency options, and migrating from an on-premises Enterprise install.
enterprise-saas.067: Control plane, per-tenant silos, partitioning, and the routing seam.
enterprise-saas.070: The defense-in-depth isolation model and why it is hard to attack.
enterprise-saas.073: Stand up a multi-tenant deployment and provision your first tenants.

authentication.005: Comprehensive guide to SysManage's JWT-based authentication system, role-based access control, multi-factor authentication, and session management.
authentication.007: SysManage implements a robust multi-layered authentication and authorization system designed for enterprise environments. The system combines JWT-based stateless authentication with comprehensive role-based access control (RBAC) to ensure secure access to infrastructure management capabilities.

mtls.005: Comprehensive guide to SysManage's mutual TLS implementation, certificate management, PKI infrastructure, auto-rotation, and secure agent communication.
mtls.007: SysManage implements mutual TLS (mTLS) for secure agent-to-server communication. This provides strong authentication, encryption in transit, and protection against various attack vectors including man-in-the-middle attacks, agent spoofing, and unauthorized access.
mtls.011: SysManage implements a two-tier PKI with offline root CA and online intermediate CA for enhanced security.

package-uninstall-security.005: Security considerations, risk assessment, and best practices for safe package uninstallation in enterprise environments.
package-uninstall-security.007: Package uninstallation in enterprise environments presents unique security challenges and risks. Unlike installation, uninstallation can inadvertently expose systems to vulnerabilities, break security controls, or compromise system integrity. This guide provides comprehensive security guidance for package uninstallation operations.

reporting.005: How to responsibly report security vulnerabilities and issues in SysManage.
reporting.007: We take security seriously. If you discover a security vulnerability, please follow our responsible disclosure process to help us address it quickly and safely.
reporting.010: Send an email to security@sysmanage.org with details about the vulnerability. Include:
reporting.016: We will acknowledge receipt of your report within 48 hours and provide an initial assessment within 7 days .
reporting.018: Our security team will investigate the issue and work on a fix. We may contact you for additional information or clarification.
reporting.020: Once fixed, we will notify you and coordinate the public disclosure timeline.
reporting.040: Please do not report the following as security vulnerabilities:
reporting.041: Issues requiring physical access to the system
reporting.043: Missing best practices without security impact
reporting.044: Issues in third-party software (report to respective vendors)
reporting.046: Brute force attacks without demonstrable impact
reporting.052: Keep the vulnerability confidential until resolved
reporting.065: For sensitive reports, use our PGP key:
reporting.071: We appreciate security researchers who help improve SysManage's security. Responsible reporters will be:
reporting.072: Acknowledged in our security advisories (with permission)
reporting.073: Listed in our Hall of Fame
reporting.077: Thank you to all security researchers who have helped improve SysManage's security through responsible disclosure.

multi-tenancy.005: How the Enterprise SaaS edition keeps every tenant's data provably separate — and why that separation holds even when something goes wrong.
multi-tenancy.006: The strongest isolation boundary is a different database
multi-tenancy.007: Shared-schema multi-tenancy — everyone's rows in one table, separated by a tenant_id column — is one forgotten WHERE clause away from a cross-tenant data breach. SysManage refuses that risk by construction: every tenant gets its own physically separate database . There is no shared table to filter, no tenant_id to forget, and no connection from one tenant's silo to another. Isolation is the default state, not something the application has to remember to enforce on every query.
multi-tenancy.009: Tenant isolation in SysManage is not one feature — it is a stack of independent layers, each of which would contain a breach on its own. An attacker has to defeat all of them, simultaneously, to cross a tenant boundary.
multi-tenancy.011: Each tenant's operational data lives in its own PostgreSQL database. A SQL injection or a missing tenant filter in one tenant's request can, at worst, reach that tenant's own data — the other tenants' data is in databases the request holds no connection to.
multi-tenancy.013: Database credentials are issued on demand by OpenBAO : short-lived, automatically expiring, and scoped to a single tenant. There is no long-lived, shared database password to steal. A leaked credential is useless to reach any other tenant and stops working on its own.
multi-tenancy.015: Tenants are created by a dedicated, least-privilege provisioner identity — not by a database superuser. The running server never holds the keys to the whole cluster, so compromising the server does not hand an attacker every tenant's database.
multi-tenancy.017: The routing seam that maps a request to a tenant database never guesses . If it cannot resolve a tenant, it logs with full context and refuses the operation rather than silently falling back to a default database. A misroute is treated as a correctness bug, not a soft failure.
multi-tenancy.019: Because partitions can live in separate databases, no foreign key crosses a tenant boundary. Cross-partition references are stored as soft references (a GUID, no enforced constraint), so each database is self-contained — there is no schema-level path from one tenant's data into another's.
multi-tenancy.021: Authentication, users, and roles are server-global; managed data is per-tenant. A person authenticates once, but every action on managed data is served from their active tenant's database. Authorization is evaluated on the control plane, independently of which tenant database is in play.
multi-tenancy.022: Why it is hard to attack
multi-tenancy.023: The clearest way to judge an isolation model is to walk the attacks against it.
multi-tenancy.024: “I'll inject SQL or drop a tenant filter to read another customer's hosts.”
multi-tenancy.025: Your request is bound to your tenant's database connection. The other tenants' data is in other databases. There is no query — malicious or accidental — that can reach across, because the connection simply isn't there. Contained to your own data.
multi-tenancy.026: “I'll steal the database password from config or memory.”
multi-tenancy.027: There is no shared, long-lived password to steal. Credentials are leased per tenant from OpenBAO, expire automatically, and are scoped to a single tenant's database. A captured credential is narrow and short-lived by construction. Blast radius is one tenant, briefly.
multi-tenancy.028: “I'll compromise the SysManage server process itself.”
multi-tenancy.029: The server does not hold superuser rights to the database cluster, and it does not cache every tenant's credentials. It leases narrowly scoped credentials as needed. Server compromise is serious, but it does not hand over the whole estate at once. No master key to seize.
multi-tenancy.030: “I'll point my agent at a tenant I don't belong to.”
multi-tenancy.031: A host is bound to exactly one tenant at registration via a tenant-scoped enrollment token that is validated and consumed server-side. The agent never chooses a database; the server routes its reports to the bound tenant's silo. Binding is server-enforced.
multi-tenancy.037: A tenant_id column; correctness depends on every query.
multi-tenancy.038: A separate database per tenant; isolation is the default.
multi-tenancy.041: Limited to the tenant whose request it is.
multi-tenancy.046: All tenants share one database and location.
multi-tenancy.047: Each tenant database can be placed independently — by host, region, or jurisdiction.
multi-tenancy.050: Operate on a single, self-contained database.
multi-tenancy.052: Per-tenant databases are not just a security property — they are an operational and regulatory one. Because a tenant's data is a single self-contained database, you can place it where it must live (a specific region or jurisdiction for data-residency rules), back it up and restore it independently , export it on request, and delete it completely when a customer leaves — all without touching, or risking, any other tenant. Right-to-be-forgotten and data-residency obligations become a per-database operation instead of a careful surgery on shared tables.
multi-tenancy.053: Part of a larger security posture
multi-tenancy.054: Multi-tenant isolation sits on top of the security SysManage already brings to every deployment. The same protections apply per tenant:
multi-tenancy.055: Mutual TLS for agent communication, with certificate-based identity. See mTLS .
multi-tenancy.056: Role-based access control evaluated on the control plane for every operation. See RBAC .
multi-tenancy.057: OpenBAO secrets management — the same vault that brokers tenant credentials manages certificates and SSH keys. See Secrets Management .
multi-tenancy.058: JWT authentication with token rotation. See Authentication .
multi-tenancy.059: Database security hardening that applies to every tenant database. See Database Security .

installation.001: Python 3.12+: Download from python.org ✅ Python 3.12, 3.13, or 3.14 recommended ✅ Check "Add Python to PATH" during installation
installation.004: Git for Windows: Download from git-scm.com
installation.005: Visual Studio Build Tools: Download from Microsoft
installation.008: Before starting installation, NetBSD users MUST:
installation.009: Install ALL required packages including jpeg (not just libjpeg-turbo)
installation.010: Set environment variables: export CFLAGS="-I/usr/pkg/include" LDFLAGS="-L/usr/pkg/lib -Wl,-R/usr/pkg/lib"
installation.011: Use gmake instead of make for all make commands
installation.012: Manually create PostgreSQL user if not present
installation.013: Increase /tmp size if needed: mount -t tmpfs -o -s=4g tmpfs /tmp
installation.014: See the NetBSD tab in Prerequisites above for complete setup instructions.
installation.016: PostgreSQL user missing: NetBSD may not create the postgres/pgsql user automatically. Create it with: sudo useradd -m -d /var/db/postgresql -s /bin/sh postgres
installation.017: PostgreSQL won't start: Ensure pgsql=YES is in /etc/rc.conf, then run sudo /etc/rc.d/pgsql start
installation.018: No space left on device during pip install: NetBSD's /tmp is limited to 1GB by default. Increase with: mount -t tmpfs -o -s=4g tmpfs /tmp or use export TMPDIR=$HOME/tmp
installation.019: gmake install-dev for development: Use gmake instead of make . The Makefile includes NetBSD-specific C tracer dependency checks (gcc13, py312-cffi)
installation.020: PostgreSQL socket not found: NetBSD PostgreSQL often uses TCP instead of Unix sockets. Always use -h localhost when connecting
installation.021: OpenTelemetry Collector build failures: NetBSD uses BSD versions of find/xargs/dirname which are incompatible with the OTEL Collector Makefile. Install GNU utilities: sudo pkgin install findutils coreutils The install-telemetry.py script will automatically detect and use gfind/gxargs/gdirname on NetBSD.
installation.022: Browser/WebDriver errors: Install browsers for UI testing: # Install Chromium (includes ChromeDriver) doas pkgin install chromium # Install Firefox (requires version-specific name) doas pkgin install firefox-138.0.4 # Install GeckoDriver for Firefox UI testing doas pkgin install geckodriver-0.36.0 # Verify installations which chromium firefox geckodriver Note: NetBSD Firefox packages often require explicit version numbers unlike other BSD systems. Due to Firefox WebDriver compatibility issues on NetBSD, the UI test suite automatically excludes Firefox tests and runs Chrome-only tests. This ensures clean test results without failures or skipped tests.
installation.023: Pillow build failures: Install image libraries and set paths: pkgin install jpeg libjpeg-turbo tiff png freetype2 lcms2 libwebp openjpeg zlib export CFLAGS="-I/usr/pkg/include" export LDFLAGS="-L/usr/pkg/lib -Wl,-R/usr/pkg/lib"
installation.024: Backend fails to start with "libjpeg.so.9 not found": This occurs when Pillow was installed before the JPEG library or with incorrect paths. The gmake install-dev target automatically rebuilds Pillow for NetBSD. For manual fix: . .venv/bin/activate export CFLAGS="-I/usr/pkg/include" export LDFLAGS="-L/usr/pkg/lib -Wl,-R/usr/pkg/lib" pip uninstall -y Pillow pip install --no-binary=:all: Pillow
installation.025: OpenBAO installation: NetBSD doesn't have pre-built OpenBAO packages. Options: Build from source (recommended): pkgin install go git gmake # NetBSD installs Go as go124 - create symlink: sudo ln -sf /usr/pkg/bin/go124 /usr/pkg/bin/go # Add ~/.local/bin to PATH: echo 'export PATH="$HOME/.local/bin:$PATH"' >> ~/.profile . ~/.profile # Then run: gmake install-dev # It will prompt to build OpenBAO from source Use Linux emulation: Try running Linux binaries under NetBSD's Linux compatibility Skip OpenBAO: It's optional - set vault.enabled=false in config Note: OpenBAO is optional for development. The system works perfectly without it.
installation.026: OpenBAO 'bao' command not found: Ensure ~/.local/bin is in your PATH: echo 'export PATH="$HOME/.local/bin:$PATH"' >> ~/.profile && . ~/.profile
installation.028: The steps above create the single application database used by the Community, Professional, and Enterprise editions. A multi-tenant Enterprise SaaS deployment also needs a registry database, OpenBAO, and an isolated database per tenant. See Deploying Multi-Tenancy for the full development and production database setup.

The Audit Log report is additionally gated by the VIEW_AUDIT_LOG permission and only appears for users who hold it.
reports.098: User Security Roles (RBAC): All users with their assigned security roles, organized by role group, showing the granular permission structure used for role-based access control across the system.
reports.099: Audit Log: Complete audit trail of user actions and system changes for compliance and security monitoring, covering database modifications, agent messages, and administrative operations. This report has its own dedicated viewer.
reports.100: Host Firewall Status: Firewall state for each managed host, including the firewall software in use, enabled/disabled status, open IPv4 and IPv6 ports, and operating system information.

reports.102: Commercial Antivirus Status: Commercial antivirus protection status across all hosts, including product name, version, signature version, real-time protection status, and service status.
reports.104: The Federation tab presents the federation report panel, which summarizes the state of federated SysManage servers and the hosts they manage. Use it to review connected peers and aggregated fleet information across a federation. Like the other reports, this view requires a Professional+ license.

migration_compat_banner.action_step2: From the project directory, run alembic upgrade head (or the equivalent Make target your installation uses).
migration_compat_banner.action_step4: The banner clears once the affected modules successfully reload at the new schema revision.
migration_compat_banner.appearance: Each affected module is listed individually with the alembic revision it requires and (if a row was readable) the current revision the database is at. Modules that fail this check are not loaded; the corresponding UI features remain unavailable until migrations catch up.
migration_compat_banner.description: SysManage displays a yellow banner across the top of the UI when one or more Pro+ modules cannot load because the database schema is older than the module requires. The banner is a fallback safety net — the normal upgrade procedure runs alembic upgrade head as part of installing a new release, and the banner only fires when that step was missed (e.g. when a Pro+ module is downloaded after an OSS upgrade but before migrations are applied).
migration_compat_banner.endpoint: The same data is exposed at GET /api/license/module-compat; a non-empty incompatibilities array indicates pending migrations. Monitoring systems and pre-flight checks should poll this endpoint to detect schema drift before users see the banner.
migration_compat_banner.title: Database migration banner

integrity_verification.description: The Audit Engine uses cryptographic hashing to ensure logs cannot be tampered with:

steps.chain: The hash includes the previous entry's hash, creating a chain
steps.detect: Any modification breaks the chain and is immediately detected
steps.hash: Each audit entry is hashed using SHA-256
steps.store: The hash is stored with the entry in the database
steps.verify: Verification recalculates all hashes and compares to stored values

integrity_verification.title: Integrity Verification

retention_policies.description: Configure automated retention policies to manage audit log storage:
retention_policies.title: Retention Policies

configuration.action: action — What to do with expired entries: delete permanently removes them, or archive moves them to the archive table for long-term storage.
configuration.action_types: action_types (optional) — Array of action types to target (e.g., ["login", "logout", "config_change"]). If omitted, the policy applies to all action types.
configuration.description: Each retention policy supports the following configuration options:
configuration.entity_types: entity_types (optional) — Array of entity types to target (e.g., ["host", "user", "secret"]). If omitted, the policy applies to all entity types.
configuration.retention_days: retention_days — Number of days to retain entries (1–3650). Entries older than this threshold are processed by the policy action.
configuration.title: Policy Configuration

siem_integration.description: Configure real-time streaming to enterprise SIEM platforms:
siem_integration.title: SIEM Integration

archival.description: When a retention policy uses the archive action, expired audit entries are moved from the main audit log table to a dedicated archive table. Archived entries retain all original fields and remain queryable through the archive API, but are separated from the active audit log to improve query performance.

how_it_works.copy: Matching entries are copied to the audit_archive table with all original fields preserved
how_it_works.evaluate: The retention policy evaluates entries older than the configured retention_days threshold
how_it_works.metadata: Archive metadata is added including the archive timestamp and source policy ID
how_it_works.remove: Original entries are removed from the active audit log table
how_it_works.title: How Archival Works
how_it_works.verify: Integrity hashes are preserved so archived entries can still be verified

querying.description: Archived entries can be queried using the same filter parameters available for the active audit log. Use date ranges, action types, users, and entity types to locate specific archived records.
querying.title: Querying Archived Entries

statistics.description: View summary statistics about your archive including total archived entries, storage breakdown by entity type and action type, and date range coverage.

retention_archive_api.description: Manage retention policies and query archived audit entries programmatically via the REST API:
retention_archive_api.title: API Access for Retention & Archive

approvals.description: Scripts marked requires_approval dispatch into the pending_approval state and create a paired ApprovalRequest. An authorized reviewer either approves (the linked execution promotes to queued and dispatches) or rejects with an optional reason (the linked execution flips to rejected). Approvals are scoped per-execution, not per-script, so reuse is explicit.
approvals.title: Approval Workflows

architecture.description: The engine is a Cython-compiled binary (automation_engine.so) loaded by the open-source server's module_loader at startup. It exports the same get_module_info() + get_automation_router() contract as the other Pro+ modules, so mount_automation_routes() wires it under /api/v1/automation alongside the 402 stub routes.
architecture.title: Architecture

deploy_plan.description: Every execution produces a deploy plan that the agent runs step-by-step: write the rendered script body to a tempfile, invoke the right interpreter, then clean up. The plan is wire-compatible with apply_deployment_plan so the same agent handler runs both Pro+ and ad-hoc paths:
deploy_plan.title: Deploy Plan Shape

execution.description: A single POST dispatches one rendered script body to many hosts. Each host gets its own ScriptExecutionHostResult tuple tracking start / completion timestamps, return code, and captured output. The overall ScriptExecution.status rolls up from the per-host states (all-succeeded → succeeded; any-failed → failed; etc.).
execution.title: Multi-host Execution

feature_codes.description: Endpoints in this engine are gated by these feature codes (returned 402 by the open-source server when the module isn't loaded):
feature_codes.title: Feature Codes

header.description: Server-side script automation: a versioned saved-script library, multi-host execution with per-host result tracking, multi-shell support, cron-style scheduling, and approval workflows for privileged scripts. Migrates ~328 lines of agent-side script orchestration to a Cython engine so the agent retains only a thin execution shim.
header.title: Automation Engine

automation_engine.meta_description: Saved-script library with version history, multi-host execution, multi-shell support, scheduled triggers, and approval workflows for privileged scripts.

opensource.adhoc: Community Edition: ad-hoc one-shot script runs against a single host via backend/services/script_plan_builder.py
opensource.approvals: Professional+: scripts flagged requires_approval queue an ApprovalRequest reviewers must clear before the execution dispatches
opensource.library: Professional+: persisted, versioned saved-script library (every edit snapshots the prior body for audit / rollback)
opensource.multihost: Professional+: queue one execution against many hosts; per-host result rolls up into an aggregate status
opensource.parameters: Professional+: typed parameter declarations (string / integer / boolean / float / secret) with validation + ${name} substitution
opensource.scheduling: Professional+: cron-triggered scheduled executions with last-run / next-run tracking
opensource.title: Open Source vs Professional+

overview.description: The Automation Engine owns every aspect of script execution: storing the script, tracking its version history, validating typed parameters, picking the right shell per host, dispatching to many hosts in one call, and capturing the per-host stdout/stderr. The agent's role shrinks to running the deploy plan the server hands it via the existing apply_deployment_plan handler.
overview.title: Overview

parameters.description: Each script declares a list of ScriptParameter entries with a typed signature (string, integer, boolean, float, secret), a required flag, and an optional default. Submitted values are type-coerced and required-checked before the script body's ${name} placeholders are substituted left-to-right. Unknown parameter names are rejected; unknown placeholders are left untouched.
parameters.title: Parameters & Substitution

scheduling.description: A ScheduledExecution binds a saved script + a host list + a cron expression. The engine validates the 5-field cron form on insert; the dispatcher polls and fires matching schedules, recording last_run and last_status. Schedules can be disabled in place without deleting the record.
scheduling.title: Scheduled Execution

scripts.description: A SavedScript bundles a name, shell, body, typed parameter declarations, timeout, and an optional requires_approval flag. Updating an existing script bumps its version and snapshots the previous body into the version-history table; older versions remain executable for rollback / audit replays.
scripts.title: Saved Scripts & Versioning

shells.description: Supported shells: bash, zsh, sh, ksh, powershell, cmd. The engine validates the script's declared shell against each target host's advertised shells (or per-platform defaults) before queueing; hosts that don't support the script's shell appear in the execution's skipped_hosts list rather than being dispatched and failing at the agent.
shells.title: Multi-shell Support

architecture.description: The engine is a Cython-compiled binary (av_management_engine.so) loaded by the open-source server's module_loader at startup. It exports the same get_module_info() + get_av_management_router() contract as the other Pro+ modules, so the open-source server's mount_av_management_routes() wrapper can mount it alongside the 402 stubs without conditionals.
architecture.title: Architecture

commercial.description: The agent's open-source commercial_antivirus_collection.py already detects CrowdStrike, SentinelOne, Defender, McAfee, Symantec, etc. and writes per-host status to the commercial_antivirus_status table. Professional+ adds a fleet aggregation endpoint that summarises which products are installed on how many hosts and surfaces hosts with realtime protection disabled.
commercial.title: Commercial AV Fleet Report

deploy_plan.description: Every operation produces a declarative deploy plan that the agent executes step-by-step (install packages → write configs → run commands → start services). The plan is wire-compatible with the open-source planner so the same agent handler runs both:
deploy_plan.title: Deploy Plan Shape

feature_codes.description: Endpoints in this engine are gated by these feature codes (returned 402 by the open-source server when the module isn't loaded):
feature_codes.title: Feature Codes

header.description: Centralised, policy-driven antivirus deployment for ClamAV (Linux/BSD/macOS) and ClamWin (Windows). Adds reusable AV policies, scheduled scans, definition-update cadence control, and a fleet-wide commercial-AV detection report on top of the open-source basics.
header.title: AV Management Engine

av_management_engine.meta_description: Centralised antivirus deployment, policy management, scan scheduling, and commercial AV detection across the fleet.

opensource.commercial: Professional+: fleet-wide commercial-AV detection report (CrowdStrike, SentinelOne, Defender, etc.)
opensource.deploy: Community Edition: per-host install / enable / disable / remove via backend/services/av_plan_builder.py
opensource.policies: Professional+: reusable named policies with cadence + scan schedule, applied to many hosts in one call
opensource.scheduling: Professional+: cron-style daily / weekly / monthly scheduled scans (Linux/FreeBSD via /etc/cron.d; Windows via schtasks)
opensource.title: Open Source vs Professional+

overview.description: The AV Management Engine generates server-side ClamAV / ClamWin configurations and ships them to agents as fully-baked deploy plans. The agent runs the plan via the generic apply_deployment_plan handler — no AV business logic lives on the agent. The engine layers an AvPolicy abstraction on top so an operator can define a single named policy (cadence + schedule + product) and apply it to any number of hosts.
overview.title: Overview

policies.description: An AvPolicy bundles the AV product, definition-update cadence, and scan schedule into a named, reusable unit. Apply it across as many hosts as you like; the engine resolves the per-host build options and queues an APPLY_DEPLOYMENT_PLAN message for each.
policies.title: AV Policies

architecture.description: The engine is a Cython-compiled binary (firewall_orchestration_engine.so) loaded by the open-source server's module_loader at startup. It exports the same get_module_info() + get_firewall_orchestration_router() contract as the other Pro+ modules. The agent retains zero firewall business logic after Phase 3 ships, except for the read-only firewall_collector.py which reports current state back.
architecture.title: Architecture

compliance.description: Compares each host's role-derived expected port set against what FirewallStatus.tcp_open_ports reports as actually open. A host is compliant iff it has a status row AND actual_ports ⊇ expected_ports.
compliance.title: Compliance Report

conflicts.description: Before any plan is queued, detect_rule_conflicts() walks the flattened rule list and surfaces inconsistent intent:
conflicts.identical: Identical rules listed twice are not reported — they just deduplicate.
conflicts.title: Rule Conflict Detection

flavors.description: A host's flavor is detected from platform + platform_release:
flavors.title: Supported Firewall Flavors

fleet.description: Apply a role-set to many hosts in a single call. Supply either explicit host_ids or a host_filter (platform / approval_status, combined with logical AND). Hosts on platforms with no builder are skipped with a reason.
fleet.title: Fleet Policy Deployment

header.description: Server-side, multi-platform firewall rule generation. Builds UFW, firewalld, pf, ipfw, npf, Windows Firewall, and macOS socketfilterfw plans from a unified PortRule schema. Adds role assignment, fleet policy deployment, rule conflict detection, and compliance reporting on top of the open-source basics.
header.title: Firewall Orchestration Engine

lockout.description: Every build_*_rules call always re-permits the management ports first — SSH (22), DNS (53), DHCP (67), plus any agent communication ports passed in host_info["agent_ports"], plus RDP (3389) on Windows. Removal builders skip preserved ports silently and surface them in skipped_preserved_ports so the operator can audit. This is non-bypassable; the engine refuses to emit a plan that would lock the agent out.
lockout.title: Lockout Protection

firewall_orchestration_engine.meta_description: Multi-platform firewall config generation, role assignment, fleet policy deployment, rule conflict detection, and compliance reporting.

opensource.basics: Community Edition: enable / disable / restart / deploy plus add / remove role ports per host via backend/services/firewall_plan_builder.py
opensource.compliance: Professional+: fleet compliance report comparing assigned roles vs actual firewall state
opensource.conflict: Professional+: pre-deploy rule conflict detection (allow/deny mismatch, source shadowing, multiple distinct sources)
opensource.fleet: Professional+: fleet-wide deployment with platform / approval-status filters
opensource.rich_rules: Professional+: source-restricted rules (CIDR), in/out direction, named-rule descriptions, in-engine validation
opensource.title: Open Source vs Professional+

overview.description: The Firewall Orchestration Engine generates per-platform firewall configurations server-side and ships them to the agent as deploy plans. The agent runs the plan via the generic apply_deployment_plan handler — no firewall business logic lives on the agent (only the read-only firewall_collector.py stays for status reporting). All seven supported flavors share a single PortRule schema with source restrictions, in/out direction, and action (allow/deny).
overview.title: Overview

architecture.description: The engine is a Cython-compiled binary (fleet_engine.so) loaded by the open-source server's module_loader at startup. It exports the same get_module_info() + get_fleet_router() contract as the other Pro+ modules, so mount_fleet_routes() wires it under /api/v1/fleet alongside the 402 stub routes.
architecture.title: Architecture

bulk.description: Eight operation types: run_script, deploy_file, service_control, install_package, remove_package, reboot, shutdown, and apply_deployment_plan. Each BulkOperation record tracks per-host status and rolls up to an aggregate.
bulk.title: Bulk Operations

feature_codes.description: Endpoints in this engine are gated by these feature codes (returned 402 by the open-source server when the module isn't loaded):
feature_codes.title: Feature Codes

groups.description: A HostGroup bundles an explicit host list, dynamic-criteria, and an optional parent group. Membership re-evaluates on every resolve_group_hosts call. The engine rejects parent assignments that would form a cycle, and reparents a deleted group's children to the deleted group's parent.
groups.title: Host Groups

header.description: Server-side fleet management: bulk operations across selectable host sets, host groups with parent/child hierarchies and dynamic-criteria membership, rolling deployments with batched rollout windows and failure thresholds, scheduled fleet operations, and per-operation progress tracking.
header.title: Fleet Engine

fleet_engine.meta_description: Bulk operations, host groups with parent/child hierarchy and dynamic-criteria membership, rolling deployments with failure thresholds, and per-operation progress tracking.

opensource.expand: Community Edition: simple expand-into-per-host plans via backend/services/bulk_op_planner.py (no persistence, no batching, explicit host_ids only)
opensource.groups: Professional+: persistent host groups with parent/child hierarchy and dynamic-criteria membership
opensource.rolling: Professional+: rolling deployments with batched rollout windows + automatic halt on excess failures
opensource.selector: Professional+: HostSelector filter DSL — by tag, platform, group, approval status, or arbitrary criteria with equals / contains / in / matches ops
opensource.title: Open Source vs Professional+
opensource.tracking: Professional+: persisted BulkOperation records, per-host result tracking, aggregated OperationProgress snapshots

overview.description: The Fleet Engine adds operation-at-scale primitives on top of the open-source single-host APIs: a HostSelector filter DSL that resolves to a list of host IDs, persisted BulkOperation records with per-host result tracking, RollingDeployment with configurable batch size and failure-threshold halt, and HostGroup with optional dynamic-criteria membership that re-evaluates on every selector resolution.
overview.title: Overview

rolling.description: A RollingDeployment partitions the resolved host set into batches of batch_size, dispatching one batch at a time. The engine halts when failures exceed failure_threshold_pct. Operators can pause / resume / cancel mid-flight.
rolling.title: Rolling Deployments

scheduling.description: A ScheduledFleetOperation binds an operation type + selector + cron expression. The selector resolves on each fire, so a schedule that targets tags=[prod] automatically picks up newly-tagged hosts.
scheduling.title: Scheduled Fleet Operations

selector.description: A HostSelector is a compound filter combining explicit host IDs with optional criteria (AND-joined). Convenience shortcuts (platforms, tags, groups, approval_status) compile into equivalent criteria before evaluation.
selector.title: Host Selector DSL

deploy_flow.step1: User selects target hosts (singly or via fleet selector).
deploy_flow.step2: User picks the backend (e.g. OTLP HTTP endpoint, OTLP gRPC, Prometheus remote-write).
deploy_flow.step3: Engine generates the per-platform deployment plan: download the otelcol binary, drop the rendered config.yml
deploy_flow.step4: Agent runs the plan via apply_deployment_plan
deploy_flow.step5: Status sweep runs every minute and refreshes the per-host OTEL state; the host details page renders it.
deploy_flow.title: OTEL Deploy Flow

header.description: Fleet-wide telemetry: OpenTelemetry collector deployment + lifecycle, Graylog log-forwarder sidecar attachment, and Grafana provisioning. Generates per-platform deployment plans (systemd / launchd / sysvinit / Windows services / FreeBSD rc.d) so a single server-side configuration rolls out consistently across mixed OS fleets.
header.title: Observability Engine

observability_engine.meta_description: Server-side OpenTelemetry collector deployment, Graylog log-forwarder sidecar, and Grafana provisioning. Telemetry routing across the fleet from one configuration.

opensource.attach: Professional+: attach hosts to a Graylog input with one click; configure source paths and TLS per host.
opensource.deploy: Professional+: deploy, start, stop, restart, and uninstall the OTEL collector across the fleet from one server-side configuration.
opensource.grafana: Professional+: connect Grafana, auto-provision the SysManage datasource + dashboards.
opensource.status: Community Edition: agent reports OTEL service status and Graylog attach state via existing status messages so the host details page shows the current connectivity.
opensource.title: Open Source vs Professional+

overview.description: The Observability Engine owns the deployment, configuration, and routing of three telemetry surfaces: the OpenTelemetry collector running on each agent (metrics + traces), the Graylog log-forwarder sidecar (syslog / file / journald shipping), and Grafana (dashboards + datasources auto-provisioned for SysManage's Postgres data). Per-host plans are generated from server-side templates so the agent never has to know how to configure these services.
overview.title: Overview

grafana.description: Connect a Grafana instance and the engine auto-provisions its Postgres datasource (read-only credentials), an admin folder, and a starter dashboard pack: host inventory, package compliance, vulnerability status, agent connectivity, and update / OS-upgrade activity. Custom dashboards added through Grafana's UI are not touched by re-provisioning.
grafana.title: Grafana Provisioning

graylog.description: A lightweight log-shipper sidecar that attaches to a Graylog input. Sources are configurable per host: syslog files, journald, application log files, or Windows Event Log. Detach + re-attach without restarting the SysManage agent.
graylog.title: Graylog Log Forwarder

otel.description: A per-host OTEL collector that scrapes node-level metrics (CPU, memory, disk, network), forwards traces from instrumented apps, and ships them to a configurable backend. The engine generates platform-specific install bundles — binary + config + service unit — for Linux (systemd), FreeBSD (rc.d), OpenBSD (rc.d), NetBSD (rc.d), macOS (launchd), and Windows (Windows service).
otel.title: OpenTelemetry Collector

troubleshooting.grafana_creds: Grafana provisioning uses the read-only Postgres credentials configured under observability.grafana.datasource
troubleshooting.graylog_tls: Graylog over TLS requires the agent host's CA bundle to trust the Graylog certificate; the attach dialog accepts a custom CA file path which the engine deploys to the agent.
troubleshooting.otel_status: If the host shows OTEL as not running after a successful deploy, the engine's status sweep takes up to ~60s to refresh; force a re-check via the host details page's refresh action.
troubleshooting.title: Troubleshooting

api.description: The Reboot Orchestration module provides three API endpoints:

orchestrated.description: Initiates an orchestrated reboot sequence. Stops all running child hosts, reboots the parent, and automatically restarts children after the agent reconnects. Requires a Professional+ license with the container_engine module.
orchestrated.error_400: Host is not active, or no running child hosts found.
orchestrated.error_402: Professional+ license required. Upgrade to access orchestrated reboot.
orchestrated.title: Orchestrated Reboot (Pro+)

pre_check.description: Returns information about running child hosts so the user can make an informed decision before rebooting. Available in the open-source Community Edition.
pre_check.title: Reboot Pre-Check (Open Source)

status.description: Returns the current status of a reboot orchestration, including timestamps for each phase and per-child restart status.
status.title: Orchestration Status

api.title: API Reference

frontend.completion_notification: A notification is shown when the orchestration completes or if any children fail to restart
frontend.description: When rebooting a parent host with running child hosts, the frontend provides an enhanced experience:
frontend.orchestrated_option: With Pro+, an "Orchestrated Reboot" button replaces the standard reboot for hosts with children
frontend.pre_check_dialog: The reboot dialog shows a pre-check summary of running child hosts
frontend.progress_polling: A progress indicator polls the orchestration status endpoint to show real-time state transitions
frontend.title: Frontend Behavior

header.description: Safe parent host reboot with automatic child host orchestration. Cleanly stops running containers before reboot, then automatically restarts them after the host comes back online.
header.title: Reboot Orchestration Module

reboot_orchestration.meta_description: Safe parent host reboot with automatic child host stop and restart orchestration for WSL and LXD containers.

opensource.description: The open-source Community Edition includes a pre-check endpoint that warns about running child hosts before a reboot. The Professional+ Reboot Orchestration module adds full automated orchestration:
opensource.orchestrated: Professional+: Automated stop, reboot, and restart orchestration
opensource.pre_check: Community Edition: Pre-check warning shows running child hosts before reboot
opensource.recovery: Professional+: Automatic child host recovery after parent reboot
opensource.title: Open Source vs Professional+
opensource.tracking: Professional+: Real-time orchestration status tracking with timestamps

overview.description: The Reboot Orchestration module provides a safe, automated workflow for rebooting parent hosts that have running child hosts (WSL or LXD containers). Instead of abruptly rebooting and risking data loss in containers, the orchestrator follows a controlled sequence to ensure all children are cleanly stopped before the reboot and automatically restarted afterward.
overview.title: Overview

requirements.agent: SysManage agent running on the parent host with active connectivity
requirements.children: Parent host must have at least one running child host (WSL or LXD)
requirements.license: Professional or Enterprise license with container_engine module enabled
requirements.permissions: User must have the REBOOT_HOST security role
requirements.title: Requirements

state_machine.description: Each orchestrated reboot follows a well-defined state machine. The orchestration progresses through these states:

failed.desc: One or more child hosts failed to restart; error details available
failed.label: failed

pending_restart.desc: Parent host is rebooting; waiting for agent to reconnect
pending_restart.label: pending_restart

rebooting.desc: All children stopped; reboot command issued to parent host
rebooting.label: rebooting

restarting.desc: Agent reconnected; start commands sent for previously-running children
restarting.label: restarting

shutting_down.desc: Stop commands sent to all running child hosts; waiting for confirmation
shutting_down.label: shutting_down

state_machine.title: Orchestration State Machine

timeouts.agent_reconnect: Agent reconnect: The orchestration transitions from "rebooting" to "restarting" when the parent's agent sends its first heartbeat after reboot
timeouts.audit_logging: Audit logging: All orchestration events are recorded in the audit log for compliance and troubleshooting
timeouts.description: The orchestration service includes built-in safeguards to handle failures at any stage:
timeouts.partial_failure: Partial failure: If some children fail to restart, the orchestration completes with an error message indicating which children failed
timeouts.shutdown_timeout: Shutdown timeout: If child hosts do not stop within the configured timeout (default 120 seconds), the reboot proceeds anyway
timeouts.title: Timeout and Error Handling
