create_flow.intro: A KVM cloud-image VM create runs end-to-end as one deployment plan. Steps are skip-guarded so re-runs against an already-cached base image are cheap.
create_flow.step1: Ensure the libvirt image directory exists.
create_flow.step10: New agent registers with the parent server; auto-approve token matches; host appears in the main hosts list.
create_flow.step2: Download the cloud image (skipped if already cached).
create_flow.step4: Clone the base image to the per-VM disk path via qemu-img convert
create_flow.step5: Resize the cloned disk to the requested capacity.
create_flow.step6: Build a cloud-init / nuageinit cidata ISO (genisoimage / mkisofs / xorrisofs fallback).
create_flow.step8: For FreeBSD: wait for DHCP + SSH, retry pubkey auth until nuageinit seeds ~/.ssh/authorized_keys
create_flow.step9: Inside the guest: bootstrap.sh installs the agent .pkg / .deb, writes /etc/sysmanage-agent.yaml
create_flow.title: VM Create Flow

header.description: Hardware virtualization lifecycle management for the three hypervisors SysManage agents can run as parent hosts: KVM/QEMU on Linux, bhyve on FreeBSD, and VMM/vmd on OpenBSD. Migrates ~30,000 lines of agent-side hypervisor orchestration to a server-side Cython engine so the agent retains only read-only listing + a thin command-execution shim.
header.title: Virtualization Engine

bhyve.description: Uses vm-bhyve. Raw FreeBSD images are pre-modified before first boot: an /etc/rc.d/sysmanage_firstboot
bhyve.title: bhyve (FreeBSD)

kvm.description: Uses libvirt + virt-install. Cloud-image guests boot via cloud-init seed ISO; the engine handles per-distribution cloud-init wiring — full cloud-init runcmd for Linux distros and a nuageinit + bootstrap.sh + SSH-driven post-boot path for FreeBSD's BASIC-CLOUDINIT images. Multi-distro autoinstall is supported via the same engine: Debian preseed, Ubuntu Subiquity autoinstall, and Alpine apkovl overlays.
kvm.title: KVM / QEMU (Linux)

vmm.description: Uses vmctl + vmd. OpenBSD guests install via httpd-served sets and an install.conf embedded into a modified bsd.rd ramdisk (rdsetroot extract / vnconfig mount / re-pack). After OpenBSD's installer finishes, the VM reboots from disk; the engine plan reloads vmd with the final vm.conf fragment.
vmm.title: VMM / vmd (OpenBSD)

virtualization_engine.meta_description: Server-side VM lifecycle management for KVM/QEMU (Linux), bhyve (FreeBSD), and VMM/vmd (OpenBSD): create, delete, start, stop, networking, storage, guest provisioning, safe parent reboot.

opensource.create: Professional+: full create / delete / start / stop / restart of KVM, bhyve, VMM child hosts.
opensource.guest_provisioning: Professional+: per-distribution guest provisioning (cloud-init runcmd, FreeBSD nuageinit, Debian preseed, Ubuntu autoinstall, Alpine apkovl, bhyve firstboot, OpenBSD install.conf).
opensource.list: Community Edition: agent reports the VMs it can see (list_child_hosts
opensource.networking: Professional+: libvirt network create/delete, vm-bhyve switch + pf NAT, VMM vm.conf fragments.
opensource.safe_reboot: Professional tier: safe-parent-reboot orchestration — stop child hosts, reboot parent, restart children automatically.
opensource.storage: Professional+: KVM qcow2 clone + resize, bhyve ZFS zvol create/destroy, VMM disk allocation via vmctl create.
opensource.title: Open Source vs Professional+

overview.description: The Virtualization Engine generates declarative deployment plans (files + shell command sequences) that the agent runs through its existing apply_deployment_plan
overview.title: Overview

troubleshooting.bind: API not reachable from inside the VM but reachable on the host: check /etc/sysmanage.yaml
troubleshooting.firewall: Inner-VM agent registration fails with connection-timeout: check that the parent host's firewall (ufw / firewalld / pf) allows traffic from the libvirt NAT subnet (typically 192.168.122.0/24
troubleshooting.skip_guard: Cloud-image download / decompression are skip-guarded against the final qcow2 path: if a previous VM left the decompressed image behind, subsequent creates re-use it without re-fetching.
troubleshooting.title: Troubleshooting

ui.create_button: When the hypervisor is ready, a Create VM
ui.create_dialog: The dialog asks for distribution, hostname, username, password, and an optional auto-approve checkbox. For VMM you also supply an ISO URL and a separate root password.
ui.enable_card: Each hypervisor card (KVM, bhyve, VMM, LXD, WSL) shows its capability state and an Enable / Initialize
ui.intro: From a parent host's Host Details → Child Hosts
ui.lifecycle_actions: Once a child host exists, per-row actions cover Start / Stop / Restart / Delete. Delete dispatches the engine's destroy + undefine + storage-purge plan; the row drops out of the UI immediately.
ui.title: Using the UI

downgrade.description: To go back to community edition: remove the license_key line from sysmanage.yaml and restart. The Pro+ engines will fail their license check and the server will mount the 402 stubs in their place. Optionally, delete the cached binaries to reclaim disk:
downgrade.title: Downgrade / removal

header.description: Apply a Professional or Enterprise license to a running open-source SysManage installation. The upgrade is non-destructive — your existing hosts, users, settings, and data carry over unchanged.
header.title: Upgrade Guide: Open Source → Professional+

upgrade_guide.meta_description: Step-by-step guide for upgrading a community-edition SysManage server to Professional+ Professional or Enterprise tier.

overview.description: Pro+ runs on top of the same open-source SysManage server you already have. There is no separate "Pro+ install" — the open-source server, when given a valid license key, downloads compiled Cython engines from the Pro+ license server at startup and mounts their routes alongside the open-source ones. Endpoints that don't have a loaded Pro+ engine return HTTP 402 (Payment Required) with {"licensed": false}; endpoints that do, behave normally.
overview.title: What changes

upgrade_guide.page_title: Upgrade Guide: Open Source → Professional+ - SysManage

prereq.disk: ~50 MB free disk under /var/lib/sysmanage/modules/ for the cached binaries
prereq.license: A Professional or Enterprise license key — either purchased or evaluation
prereq.outbound: Outbound HTTPS to license.sysmanage.org from the SysManage server (the module loader needs to pull the Cython engines + plugin bundles)
prereq.running: A running open-source SysManage server (any release from v1.3.0 onward)
prereq.title: Prerequisites

renewal.description: When your license is approaching expiry (visible in Settings → License in the web UI), generate a new key from the Pro+ portal and replace the license_key value in /etc/sysmanage.yaml. Restart the server. The module loader re-validates against the license server's public key on every restart and on a periodic background check (default every 24 hours).
renewal.expired: If the license expires while the server is running, all Pro+ endpoints transition back to {"licensed": false, "expired": true}. The open-source functionality keeps working — nothing breaks; only the Pro+ surface is gated off.
renewal.title: License renewal & rotation

step1.description: Edit /etc/sysmanage.yaml and add (or replace) the license_key field under the pro_plus section:
step1.note: The license is a signed JWT. Do not edit it; just paste the whole string.
step1.title: 1. Drop the license key into your config

step2.description: Restart the SysManage backend so it picks up the license and contacts the license server. On systemd-managed installs:
step2.dev: For developer installs (make start), stop and start as you normally would.
step2.title: 2. Restart the server

step3.description: During startup the loader fetches the public key, validates the license, and downloads each entitled module (one Cython binary plus one frontend plugin bundle per engine). Tail the log:
step3.timing: First-time downloads can take 30-90 seconds depending on the link to license.sysmanage.org. Subsequent restarts use cached binaries and complete in seconds.
step3.title: 3. Watch the module loader log

step4.description: The web UI caches the plugin bundle list in browser memory. After the server restart, hard-refresh (<kbd>Ctrl+Shift+R</kbd> / <kbd>Cmd+Shift+R</kbd>) so the new bundles load. Pro+ tabs and dashboard cards should appear.
step4.title: 4. Hard-refresh the browser

step5.description: In the browser DevTools console (logged in):
step5.title: 5. Verify the new endpoints

tier_diff.description: Both tiers run the same open-source server with different bundles of modules unlocked.
tier_diff.title: Tier comparison: Professional vs Enterprise

402.description: The license is valid but the specific feature isn't in your tier. Check the features array in your decoded JWT — if the feature code (e.g. av_install, firewall_compliance_check) isn't there, contact sales to upgrade tier.
402.title: "I see Pro+ features but every API call returns 402"

network.description: The SysManage server can't reach license.sysmanage.org. Confirm outbound HTTPS (TCP/443) is allowed, especially through any corporate proxy. For air-gapped installations a separate offline-licensing flow is planned for Phase 11.
network.title: "Module download failed: connection timeout"

no_features.step1: Confirm the JWT is well-formed: echo "$LICENSE" | cut -d. -f2 | base64 -d | python3 -m json.tool — should show your tier and the modules array
no_features.step2: Check the backend log for "License validated successfully" and the per-module download lines
no_features.step3: Hard-refresh the browser to drop cached plugin bundle metadata
no_features.step4: Log out and back in — some plugin state is per-session
no_features.title: "I added the license but Pro+ features don't appear"

slow.description: Expected. The loader is downloading every entitled module (.so) and plugin bundle (.iife.js) from license.sysmanage.org for the first time. Subsequent restarts use the on-disk cache and complete in seconds.
slow.title: "The first restart after adding the license takes forever"

what_doesnt_change.agents: Connected agents — no agent-side changes are required to use Pro+ features (the new declarative apply_deployment_plan handler is already in the open-source agent)
what_doesnt_change.api: Open-source API endpoints — existing scripts/integrations keep working byte-for-byte
what_doesnt_change.config: Existing sysmanage.yaml settings (the pro_plus.license_key field is the only addition needed)
what_doesnt_change.data: Your PostgreSQL/SQLite database, hosts table, users, certificates, audit log
what_doesnt_change.description: Upgrading to Pro+ is purely additive. None of the following move:
what_doesnt_change.title: What does NOT change

header.description: CVE-based security scanning with risk assessment and remediation guidance to protect your infrastructure from known vulnerabilities.
header.title: Vulnerability Scanning Module

vulnerability.meta_description: CVE-based vulnerability scanning for your infrastructure with risk scoring, severity tracking, and remediation guidance.

overview.description: The Vulnerability Scanning module correlates your installed software against multiple CVE databases to identify security vulnerabilities. It provides risk scoring, severity classification, and actionable remediation steps to help you prioritize and address security issues.
overview.title: Overview

requirements.license: Professional or Enterprise license with vuln_engine module
requirements.network: Network connectivity for CVE database updates
requirements.title: Requirements

risk.description: Each host receives a risk score from 0-100, calculated based on the number and severity of vulnerabilities found:

levels.critical: Immediate patching required - critical vulnerabilities present
levels.high: Priority attention - schedule patches within 24-48 hours
levels.low: Monitor - low severity issues, patch when convenient
levels.medium: Plan remediation - address in next maintenance window

risk.title: Risk Scoring System

scanning.description: The vulnerability engine performs the following steps:

steps.inventory: Collects software inventory from the host agent
steps.match: Matches installed packages against the CVE database
steps.score: Calculates risk score based on severity distribution
steps.version: Compares versions to identify vulnerable software

scanning.title: Scanning Process

critical.description: Severe impact - remote code execution, privilege escalation
critical.label: Critical (9.0-10.0)

severity.description: Vulnerabilities are classified by severity using CVSS scores:

high.description: Significant impact - data exposure, service disruption
high.label: High (7.0-8.9)

low.description: Minor impact - information disclosure, minor bugs
low.label: Low (0.1-3.9)

medium.description: Moderate impact - limited access or denial of service
medium.label: Medium (4.0-6.9)

severity.title: Severity Classification

sources.description: Vulnerability data is aggregated from multiple authoritative sources:

freebsd.description: FreeBSD Vulnerability XML database for BSD package vulnerabilities
freebsd.title: FreeBSD VuXML

nvd.description: National Vulnerability Database - CVE data with CVSS scoring
nvd.title: NIST NVD

redhat.description: Red Hat Security Advisories (RHSA) for RHEL/CentOS/Fedora vulnerabilities
redhat.title: Red Hat Security

sources.title: CVE Data Sources

ubuntu.description: Ubuntu Security Notices (USN) for Debian/Ubuntu package vulnerabilities
ubuntu.title: Ubuntu Security

history.description: Track vulnerability trends over time to monitor your security posture improvement. Historical data helps validate patching efforts and identify regression.
history.title: Scan History

running.description: Click the "Run Scan" button on the Vulnerabilities tab to perform a new vulnerability scan. Scan results are cached until a new scan is requested.
running.title: Running a Scan

viewing.description: Vulnerability scan results are accessible from the Vulnerabilities tab on the Host Detail page. The tab displays:
viewing.details: Detailed CVE information with package names

federation.meta_description: Hierarchical multi-server architecture for geographically distributed enterprise deployments — a coordinating server aggregates host inventory, compliance, and command dispatch across many subordinate site servers.

header.title: Multi-Site Federation
header.description: A coordinating SysManage server aggregates host inventory, compliance posture, vulnerability exposure, and dispatched commands across many subordinate site servers running independently at each data center, branch office, or cloud region.

overview.title: Overview
overview.problem: Large enterprises operate data centers, branch offices, and cloud regions across multiple geographic locations. Managing thousands of hosts from a single SysManage server creates scalability bottlenecks, network latency issues, and single-point-of-failure risk.
overview.solution: The federation Pro+ Enterprise SKU introduces a hierarchical architecture: many subordinate site servers operate autonomously at each site, while a Federation Controller aggregates data and dispatches commands across the entire enterprise. The controller never talks to agents directly — everything flows through site servers, preserving local-autonomy semantics if a network partition isolates a site.

modules.title: Engine Modules
modules.intro: Federation ships as two Pro+ engines in one Enterprise license — a server runs exactly one role at a time, set via Settings → Server Role (stored in the database, not a config file).

controller.title: Federation Controller Engine
controller.description: Loaded on the coordinating server. Owns the site registry, accepts upstream rollup pushes from sites, exposes cross-site host search, drives policy push and command dispatch, and writes the federation audit log.

features.registry: Site server registry: enroll, suspend, resume, remove
features.enrollment: Secure enrollment workflow with mutual-TLS certificate pinning
features.rollup: Aggregate rollups: host counts, compliance scores, CVE exposure
features.policy: Polymorphic policy push (update profiles, firewall roles, compliance baselines) with stale-version detection
features.dispatch: Command dispatch with a strict FSM (queued → in-progress → terminal)

site.title: Federation Site Engine
site.description: Loaded on every subordinate site server. Handles the inbound side of the federation protocol (enroll, accept policy pushes, accept dispatched commands) and drains an outbound sync queue back upstream.

features.upstream: Upstream data sync (host deltas, compliance + vulnerability snapshots)
features.autonomy: Local autonomy mode — agents continue reporting and commands run locally when the coordinator is unreachable

architecture.title: Data Architecture
architecture.intro: Federation data is split into three tiers, deliberately partitioned so the coordinator scales linearly with sites rather than with hosts:

aggregate.title: Aggregate tier
aggregate.body: (coordinator) — one row per site per metric. Host count, healthy/unhealthy ratio, compliance %, top CVEs by severity, alert counts, last-sync timestamp.

directory.title: Host-directory tier
directory.body: (coordinator) — one row per host fleet-wide, holding only the columns operators filter on (hostname, IP, OS, status, geo, tags). Sized for ~1 KB × 1M hosts.

detail.title: Detail tier
detail.body: (sites) — full software inventory, certificate chains, audit log bodies, OS-specific facts. Never replicated upstream; drill-down queries are proxied to the originating site.

ui.title: Coordinator UI
ui.intro: When the federation controller engine is loaded, several new pages become available in the SysManage web UI:

sites.title: Sites
sites.body: (/sites) — card grid of enrolled sites with status traffic light, last-sync, host count. "Enroll Site" button generates a one-time enrollment token for delivery to the new site server.

detail.title: Site detail
detail.body: (/sites/:siteId) — per-site metadata, connection card, and lifecycle actions (Suspend / Resume / Remove). "See hosts" deep-links into the Hosts page filtered by this site; "View audit log" deep-links the audit log viewer pre-filtered.

map.title: Sites map
map.body: (/sites/map) — geographic Leaflet map of every site at its operator-supplied (lat, lng). Click markers for popup details + deep-link into site detail.

policies.title: Policies
policies.body: (/federation/policies) — CRUD on coordinator-defined policies (update profiles, firewall roles, compliance baselines). Assign to multiple sites, push immediately, deactivate.

audit.title: Federation audit log
audit.body: (/audit/federation) — every cross-site operation (enrollment, suspend/resume/remove, policy push, command dispatch) with URL-shareable filters by site, operation, and actor.

timeline.title: Sync timeline
timeline.body: — the site detail page renders a per-site upstream-sync latency sparkline, the site's reported SysManage version and loaded engine capabilities, and an “Operating independently” banner when the site reports that it is offline from the coordinator (local-autonomy mode).

actions.title: Per-site actions
actions.body: — the site detail header offers a one-click Dispatch command (batch command to the site's hosts) and Push policies (re-queue every policy assigned to the site for re-delivery on the next push tick). Both are gated to actively enrolled sites.

reports.title: Cross-site reports
reports.body: (/reports#federation) — a Federation tab on the Reports page with a site multi-select that produces a cross-site rollup table (per-site host counts, worst compliance baseline, CVE-severity counts) plus enterprise-wide totals.

enrollment.title: Enrolling a Site
enrollment.step2: Copy the displayed enrollment token once — it is not retrievable afterwards.
enrollment.step4: After successful handshake, the site flips to enrolled status on the coordinator, the token hash is scrubbed, and the enrolled_at timestamp is stamped.
enrollment.regenerate: If the token leaks or expires before the site completes enrollment, use Regenerate token on the site detail page to invalidate the old one and issue a fresh value.
enrollment.step1: On the coordinator's Sites page, click Enroll Site and fill in name, URL, an optional location label, and the site's identity public key — copied out of band from the site's Settings → Server Role page. The identity key is required: it is what the coordinator uses to authenticate the site at enrollment. The default token TTL is 24 hours.
enrollment.step3: On the new site server, open Settings → Server Role, set the federation role to Site, and use the Enroll with coordinator action: paste the coordinator URL, the token, and the coordinator's identity public key (copied out of band from the coordinator's Server Role page), then click Enroll. The TLS certificates are still auto-presented and auto-fetched — you never mint or paste a cert — but each side must additionally prove its out-of-band identity key signed the cert being pinned, which is what defeats an enrollment-time man-in-the-middle.

licensing.title: Licensing
licensing.body: Federation is part of the SysManage Enterprise tier. The Enterprise license entitles a deployment to load the federation controller or site engine on any server — the role is chosen in the web UI under Settings → Server Role. On Community / Professional installs, the federation API endpoints return 403 pro_plus_required and the UI renders an Enterprise upsell on every federation page.

health.title: Connection Health & Local Autonomy
health.intro: Each site server continuously classifies the health of its own uplink to the coordinator and keeps operating regardless of that uplink's state. The site engine's sync tick records every attempt and derives a connection state:

degraded.title: degraded
degraded.body: — one or two consecutive sync failures; usually a transient blip, not worth paging on.

offline.title: offline
offline.body: — three or more consecutive failures. The site enters local autonomy mode: agents keep reporting, OS upgrades keep running, and local deltas keep queuing for replay — nothing blocks on the coordinator.

health.reconnect: While offline, the site does not hammer a down coordinator every interval — it backs off with an exponential, capped reconnect schedule and resumes draining its queue automatically once the coordinator answers. On reconnect, deduplication-on-replay ensures queued deltas don't create duplicates upstream.
health.metadata: Every tick the site also reports lightweight metadata upstream — its SysManage version, active-host count and OS breakdown, loaded engine capabilities, and its self-reported connection state — which the coordinator caches on the site's registry row and surfaces on the site detail page.

alerts.title: Rollup Alerting
alerts.intro: The coordinator evaluates every enrolled site against three built-in cross-site conditions on each push-worker cycle, opening, refreshing, or auto-resolving alerts so there is at most one open alert per site per condition:
alerts.offline: Site offline — a site hasn't synced within a multiple of its configured sync interval.
alerts.compliance: Compliance below threshold — a compliance baseline's score dropped under a configured percentage.
alerts.vulnerabilities: Vulnerabilities high — a site's critical-CVE count crossed a configured threshold.
alerts.thresholds: The thresholds for all three conditions are operator-configurable. An Alert thresholds card on the Federation Policies page lets you override any subset; a blank field reverts that threshold to its built-in default. Open alerts appear on the relevant site's detail page with an acknowledge action.

secrets.title: Federation-Aware Dynamic Secrets
secrets.intro: The coordinator owns the master OpenBAO/Vault, so restricted sites never need direct Vault access. A site can request a short-lived dynamic credential on behalf of one of its hosts, and the coordinator issues and rotates it centrally.
secrets.step1: The site enqueues a lease request upstream (it never calls the coordinator directly — queue-everything), naming the host, the secret, the Vault backend role, and a TTL.
secrets.step2: The coordinator records the request and, on its next reconcile pass, issues the lease from the master Vault.
secrets.step3: The lease result is echoed back to the requesting site's inbox so the site engine can deliver the credential to the host over the agent's secure channel.
secrets.reconcile: A single coordinator-side reconcile loop services every site's leases — issuing pending requests, expiring leases whose TTL has elapsed, and pruning old terminal records. There are no per-site sweepers because every lease lives in the one master Vault. Leases are viewable (and revocable) at /api/v1/federation/secret-leases.
secrets.security: The credential value is never persisted in the federation tables — only the Vault lease id (for renew/revoke) and non-sensitive metadata such as a generated username.

deploy.title: Deployment & Operations
deploy.intro: A federated estate is one coordinator and N site servers. Each server plays exactly one role, chosen at install time and fixed thereafter unless re-provisioned.

roles.title: Choosing a role
roles.body: The role is set in the web UI under Settings → Server Role (persisted in the database) — coordinator or site, never both. It is a separate axis from the air-gap server role, so a server can be, e.g., an air-gap collector AND a federation site. A server with the role left at 'none' is a standalone install. The Enterprise license entitles the engines; you pick the role per server.

sequence.title: Bring-up sequence
sequence.step1: Stand up the coordinator first and confirm its federation pages load (/sites renders the empty registry rather than the Enterprise upsell).
sequence.step2: Stand up each site server as a normal SysManage install with the site engine loaded; let its local agents enroll and report as usual — a site is fully operational before it ever joins the federation.
sequence.step3: Enroll each site into the coordinator (see Enrolling a Site above and the mutual-TLS procedure below). Sites can be added incrementally; there is no big-bang cutover.
sequence.step4: Define centrally-managed policies (update profiles, firewall roles) on the coordinator and assign them to sites; the coordinator pushes them on its next cycle.

ops.title: Day-2 operations
ops.monitor: Watch site health on the Sites page and the per-site sync timeline; an “Operating independently” banner means that site is in local autonomy mode (cut off from the coordinator but running normally).
ops.lifecycle: Suspend a site to stop it pulling policy/commands while keeping its registry row; resume to re-attach; remove to disenroll (the row is retained with status=removed for audit).
ops.intervals: Tune a bandwidth-constrained site's sync_interval_seconds so it syncs less often; the coordinator's offline-alert threshold scales off that interval.
ops.alerts: Adjust the cross-site alert thresholds (offline multiple, compliance floor, critical-CVE ceiling) on the Federation Policies page; leave a field blank to use the built-in default.

trouble.title: Troubleshooting
trouble.offline: Site shows offline: check the site engine's last-sync error on its detail page. The site backs off and auto-reconnects on an exponential schedule, so transient outages clear themselves; queued deltas replay (deduplicated) on reconnect.
trouble.backlog: Growing upstream-queue depth: the site is producing deltas faster than it can ship them (usually a coordinator outage). It is not data loss — the queue drains when connectivity returns.
trouble.enroll: Enrollment fails: the most common causes are an expired or already-used token, or an identity-key mismatch (a 401 identity-verification error means the pre-registered identity public key doesn't match the peer's actual key — re-copy it from the peer's Server Role page). Regenerate the token and re-run the enroll action after correcting the key.

mtls.title: Mutual-TLS Enrollment Procedures
mtls.intro: Federation traffic is authenticated in both directions. Enrollment establishes the trust material; every subsequent sync and push rides on it.

certs.title: Certificate pinning
certs.body: Each server auto-mints a self-signed federation certificate the first time it is given a federation role — the operator never generates or pastes one. At enrollment the site presents its own certificate and fetches the coordinator's from the coordinator's public /api/v1/federation/tls-cert endpoint; the coordinator pins the site's certificate on the registry row and the site pins the coordinator's. Each side thereafter rejects a request whose signature doesn't match the pinned certificate — a leaked bearer token alone can't impersonate either party. The certificate fetched at enrollment is not trusted blindly, though — see Out-of-band identity verification below.

tokens.title: Bearer tokens
tokens.body: A successful handshake mints two long-lived bearer tokens, one per direction. The site presents its token on every upstream sync POST; the coordinator presents a separate token on every downstream push. Each side stores only the SHA-256 hash of the token it verifies, and the plaintext only of the token it sends — so a database leak on the verifying side never yields a usable credential.

flow.title: Handshake flow
flow.step1: The operator generates a one-time enrollment token on the coordinator (default 24-hour TTL); only its SHA-256 hash is stored.
flow.step4: The site stores its assigned id + bearers, flips to enrolled, and the coordinator scrubs the enrollment-token hash. From here, only the bearer-authenticated sync/push endpoints are used.
flow.step2: On the site, the Enroll with coordinator action fetches the coordinator's certificate and its identity proof, and verifies the proof against the coordinator identity key the operator pre-registered out of band — aborting if it doesn't match. It then POSTs the token to the coordinator with its own certificate and an identity proof attached.
flow.step3: The coordinator validates the token (unused + unexpired), verifies the site's identity proof against the site identity key registered when the site row was created, pins the site certificate, assigns the site a UUID, mints the two bearer tokens, and returns them once. A missing or invalid identity proof is rejected with 401.

rotation.title: Rotation & revocation
rotation.body: Re-running enrollment (or migrating a site to a different coordinator via clear_enrollment) replaces the bearer tokens and re-pins certificates — the critical step that prevents a stale token from continuing to fire at a former coordinator. Removing a site on the coordinator scrubs its credentials so a disenrolled site can no longer authenticate.

enforcement.title: Signature enforcement (HTTP vs HTTPS)
enforcement.body: Every sync and push request is signed with the sender's federation private key and verified against the pinned certificate. Enforcement is derived from the deployment posture automatically — no configuration flag. A server running plain HTTP (no certFile/keyFile in sysmanage.yaml) is treated as a development deployment: requests are still signed, but signatures are not rejected, so local testing is never blocked. Configure TLS so the API serves HTTPS and signature verification activates on its own — a request with a missing or invalid signature is then rejected with 401. Production federation deployments should always run HTTPS.

identity.body: Pinning the certificate that is fetched over the network would, on its own, be trust-on-first-use: an attacker who intercepts the enrollment could present its own certificate and be pinned. The enrollment token cannot close this gap because it is a shared secret that travels over the same channel. SysManage therefore anchors enrollment on each server's long-lived Ed25519 identity key, whose private half never leaves the box. Before enrolling, the operator exchanges the two servers' identity public keys out of band (they are public, so any channel works — the Server Role page shows each key and its fingerprint). During enrollment each side signs the exact certificate it is presenting with its identity private key, and the other side verifies that signature against the pre-shared identity public key before pinning. A man-in-the-middle that swaps a certificate cannot forge the matching signature, so enrollment is refused rather than pinned. This verification is strict and mandatory: enrollment fails if an identity key was not pre-registered or the proof does not verify. Because the proof is over the certificate fingerprint, rotating a TLS certificate later is just a re-sign by the same identity key — no second out-of-band exchange.
identity.title: Out-of-band identity verification

defense_in_depth.description: Every protected operation in SysManage uses a two-layer security validation approach:

layer1.description: UI elements (buttons, menus, actions) are hidden or disabled based on user permissions. This prevents users from attempting unauthorized actions and provides a better user experience.
layer1.title: Frontend Layer

layer2.description: API endpoints validate user permissions before executing any operation. Even if the frontend is bypassed, the backend enforces security with HTTP 403 errors for unauthorized access.
layer2.title: Backend Layer

defense_in_depth.title: Defense-in-Depth Architecture

why.description: The frontend layer cannot be trusted for security enforcement since it runs in the user's browser and can be manipulated. The backend layer provides the actual security guarantee. Together, they offer both security (backend) and usability (frontend).
why.title: Why Two Layers?

mirror.subtitle: For each supported (platform, version) pair, pick which mirror new and existing hosts of that family use as their default. Cloud means hosts hit the public upstream directly. Only mirrors that have completed a successful sync are eligible.
